project.goal: Publish e-sports league stats as a fast, globally cached static site
project.target_users: Fans and analysts
project.timeline: Season 3 launch in 6 weeks

data.source: League admin CSV exports compiled to JSON at build
data.format: Per-player, per-team, and per-match static JSON data files

devops.ci: GitHub Actions triggering Cloudflare Pages deploy hook
devops.environments: preview per branch, production on main

constraints.runtime: No server-side code on Cloudflare Pages

reasoner_reviews.product: The stat pages cover all major fan consumption patterns: rankings, head-to-head records, and tournament brackets. Seasonal archiving is well-scoped as an immutable snapshot strategy. The filterable leaderboard is the highest traffic feature and should be prioritised for build-time optimisation.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro island architecture is well-suited: base stat pages render as zero-JS HTML for fast initial load, with selective hydration only for interactive chart and filter components. The build-time JSON compilation pipeline is the right pattern; ensure data partitioning by game title to keep individual page data payloads small.
reasoner_reviews.security: Public static site presents a minimal attack surface. Primary concern is build pipeline integrity: league admin CSV exports must come from an authenticated source to prevent stat tampering before publication. CSP headers should block inline scripts to prevent XSS from any future third-party embed integrations.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 8-minute build budget with Astro requires careful attention to page count. With 500+ player profiles across 5 game titles the Astro content collection build can become slow; consider incremental builds or splitting player pages across parallel build jobs.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan addressed the slow build concern by introducing parallel Astro build shards but left the seasonal archive immutability strategy underspecified. The versioned snapshot deployment pattern needs explicit tagging and rollback documentation.

backend.service_design: No runtime backend; build-time Node.js 20 scripts handle CSV ingestion, stat aggregation, and JSON file generation under /src/data/. A separate script validates data integrity before emitting files.
backend.background_jobs: GitHub Actions scheduled workflow runs the data compilation scripts on a weekly cadence and on manual trigger for mid-season updates.
backend.backend_risks: Data pipeline failure silently producing stale JSON is the main risk; build scripts must exit non-zero on validation failures to block deploys.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 content collections for player_profiles, team_rosters, and match_records; dynamic routes generate one page per player and per team. Chart components use Astro island directive client:visible for lazy hydration.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home (season highlights), /rankings/[game] (filterable leaderboard), /players/[slug] (stat profile), /teams/[slug] (roster + match history), /matches/[id] (detailed scorecard), /season/[year] (archived snapshot)
frontend.performance_notes: Static HTML for all data-heavy pages; Chart.js bundles loaded only on pages with visualizations. Fuse.js leaderboard search runs client-side against a pre-built search index JSON file generated at build time.
frontend.frontend_risks: Chart.js bundle size (~200KB) loading on every player page would degrade LCP; must use client:visible hydration and lazy import.

security.auth_design: No authentication; site is fully public.
security.abuse_prevention: Cloudflare WAF blocks automated scraping above rate thresholds; no API endpoints to protect.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Build pipeline logs capture every data compilation run with checksums of source CSV files for auditability.

data.entities: player_profiles (slug, display_name, team_id, game_title, kda_ratio, win_rate, match_count, peak_rank, seasons_active), team_rosters (team_id, name, game_title, active_players[], coach), match_records (match_id, date, game_title, team_a_id, team_b_id, score_a, score_b, map_sequence[], mvp_player_id), seasonal_rankings (season, game_title, rank, team_id, wins, losses, point_differential), tournament_brackets (tournament_id, game_title, rounds[], match_refs[])
data.storage_design: All data as static JSON files under /src/data/ grouped by game_title; Astro content collections typed with Zod schemas for build-time validation.
data.retention_and_deletion: Historical seasons retained as immutable versioned JSON snapshots under /src/data/archives/; no deletion policy needed as no PII stored.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloudflare Pages project with automatic preview deployments per PR branch and production deployment on merge to main.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: (1) lint and type-check, (2) run data compilation scripts, (3) validate output JSON schemas, (4) astro build, (5) deploy via Cloudflare Pages API. Build cache keyed on source CSV hash to skip recompilation when data unchanged.
devops.observability_stack: Cloudflare Web Analytics for page views, bounce rate, and Core Web Vitals by page path. Build duration tracked via GitHub Actions annotations.
devops.rollback_strategy: Cloudflare Pages maintains last 10 deployments; instant rollback via Pages dashboard or wrangler CLI to any previous build.

ISS-001.detail: Round 2 plan introduced parallel Astro build shards but did not define how versioned seasonal snapshots are tagged, stored, or rolled back independently from the live site.

strengths: Parallel build sharding addresses 8-minute build constraint for large player counts
Astro island hydration strategy correctly limits JS bundle to chart-heavy pages
Cloudflare Pages rollback capability documented

previous_plan.title: E-Sports League Stats Showcase v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Astro 4 static site on Cloudflare Pages serving per-player, per-team, and per-match stats compiled from league admin CSV exports, with parallel build shards to stay within the 8-minute budget.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Zero-runtime static site built with Astro 4 content collections; player and team pages generated at build time from compiled JSON data files; Chart.js islands hydrated client-side only on pages requiring visualizations.
previous_plan.data_model: player_profiles, team_rosters, match_records, seasonal_rankings, tournament_brackets stored as typed static JSON under /src/data/ with Zod schema validation at build time.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline with parallel Astro build shards; Cloudflare Pages for hosting with preview per branch; rollback via Pages dashboard.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 revision for an Astro 4 static e-sports stats site. Two unresolved medium issues: ISS-001 (seasonal archive versioning/rollback) and ISS-002 (CSV source authentication). The fix for ISS-001 is to define a semantic versioning convention for archive snapshots stored on Cloudflare R2 with independent rollback paths. ISS-002 is addressed by adding SHA-256 checksum verification of CSV exports in the CI pipeline before data compilation. Both fixes slot into the deployment and security sections without changing the core Astro architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined a semantic seasonal archive convention: end-of-season builds tagged vSEASON-YEAR (e.g. v3-2026) with archive JSON published to Cloudflare R2 under /archives/v3-2026/ and a separate Cloudflare Pages alias deployment pointing to that snapshot path, allowing independent rollback without affecting the live site.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Seasonal archives are immutably versioned, independently accessible via alias URLs, and rollbackable without touching the production deployment

fix_report.action_taken: Added a CSV integrity verification step to the GitHub Actions pipeline: league admin exports are accompanied by SHA-256 checksums signed with a shared HMAC key; the CI script verifies the signature before invoking the data compilation scripts, failing the build if verification fails.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Tampered or unauthorised CSV exports are rejected at CI entry point before any data reaches the Astro build

target_output.title: E-Sports League Stats Showcase
target_output.executive_summary: An Astro 4 static site on Cloudflare Pages delivering per-player performance profiles, team rosters, match histories, seasonal rankings, and tournament brackets for a competitive e-sports league. All data is compiled from authenticated league admin CSV exports at build time into typed JSON content collections. The site serves a global fanbase with edge-cached HTML and selective island hydration for interactive chart and filter components.
target_output.architecture_overview: Zero-server-runtime architecture built on Astro 4 content collections. League admin CSV exports are verified via HMAC-signed SHA-256 checksums, then compiled by Node.js build scripts into strongly-typed JSON data files under /src/data/ partitioned by game_title. Astro generates static HTML for every player profile, team page, match scorecard, and seasonal ranking page. Chart.js visualisation islands are hydrated client:visible only when the user scrolls them into view, keeping initial page weight under 50KB for ranking tables. Cloudflare Pages distributes the built assets globally with HTTP/3 and Brotli compression. Seasonal end-of-season snapshots are versioned, published to Cloudflare R2, and served via dedicated alias deployments independent of the live site.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4.x, TypeScript 5, Chart.js 4 (island hydration), Fuse.js (client-side leaderboard search). Build tooling: Node.js 20 data compilation scripts, Zod for JSON schema validation at build time. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Cloudflare Pages deploy hook. Hosting: Cloudflare Pages (production + preview) + Cloudflare R2 for seasonal archives. Analytics: Cloudflare Web Analytics (cookieless).
target_output.functional_feature_map: Player Profiles: per-player page with career KDA, win rate, peak rank, active seasons, and per-game-title breakdown chart. Team Pages: roster listing, head-to-head record table, recent match history, season-by-season point differential. Match Scorecards: map-by-map breakdown, MVP highlight, team stat comparison bars. Seasonal Rankings: filterable leaderboard by game title, sortable by wins/losses/point differential. Tournament Brackets: static SVG bracket diagrams generated from tournament_brackets JSON at build time. Archive Browser: versioned snapshot pages accessible at /season/[year] via Cloudflare R2 alias.
target_output.system_components: GitHub repository (source and CSV data inputs), GitHub Actions (CI pipeline with checksum verification and parallel Astro build), Cloudflare Pages (production hosting + preview deployments), Cloudflare R2 (seasonal archive snapshot storage), Cloudflare Web Analytics (cookieless page metrics), Cloudflare CDN (global edge cache, HTTP/3, Brotli). No runtime compute components.
target_output.workflows: Stats ingestion: League admin exports CSV files with an accompanying SHA-256 checksum file signed with the shared HMAC key. CI pipeline verifies the signature, runs the Node.js data compilation scripts to aggregate per-player and per-team stats, validates output JSON against Zod schemas, then invokes astro build. Build fails and deploy is blocked if any checksum or schema validation fails. Seasonal archive: At season end, the CI pipeline runs a full rebuild with the archival flag set, tags the build vSEASON-YEAR, uploads the compiled /dist output to Cloudflare R2 under /archives/v3-2026/, and creates or updates a Cloudflare Pages alias deployment pointing to the R2 bucket path. The live production deployment continues to serve the current season without interruption.
target_output.data_model: player_profiles (slug, display_name, team_id, game_title ENUM valorant/league_of_legends/cs2/rocket_league/apex, kda_ratio FLOAT, win_rate FLOAT, match_count INT, peak_rank STRING, seasons_active INT[]). team_rosters (team_id, name, game_title, active_player_slugs STRING[], coach STRING, region STRING). match_records (match_id, date ISO8601, game_title, team_a_id, team_b_id, score_a INT, score_b INT, map_sequence STRING[], mvp_player_slug, duration_minutes INT). seasonal_rankings (season STRING, game_title, rank INT, team_id, wins INT, losses INT, point_differential INT). tournament_brackets (tournament_id, game_title, format ENUM single_elim/double_elim/swiss, rounds OBJECT[], match_refs STRING[]). All stored as static JSON under /src/data/[game_title]/ validated with Zod at build time.
target_output.api_design: No runtime API. Build-time data access: Node.js scripts read from /data/raw/[game_title]/*.csv, aggregate into /src/data/[game_title]/players.json, teams.json, matches.json, rankings.json. Astro content collections expose typed helpers: getCollection('players'), getEntry('teams', teamId). Client-side leaderboard search: GET /search-index/[game_title].json (pre-built Fuse.js index served as static asset). Cloudflare Pages Functions not used; no runtime endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced globally via Cloudflare. CSP header via _headers file: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; no inline scripts permitted. HSTS with max-age 31536000. Build pipeline security: league admin CSV exports accompanied by SHA-256 checksums signed with a 256-bit HMAC key stored in GitHub Actions secrets; CI script verifies signature before data compilation and aborts with exit code 1 on mismatch. No user data collected; Cloudflare Web Analytics is cookieless with no session tracking, eliminating GDPR consent requirements. X-Frame-Options DENY to prevent stat page embedding in malicious frames.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline stages: (1) checkout and install, (2) verify CSV HMAC checksums, (3) run data compilation scripts with game_title partitioning, (4) validate JSON output schemas with Zod, (5) run astro check and tsc, (6) parallel astro build shards (one per game_title entry collection), (7) merge build outputs, (8) deploy to Cloudflare Pages via wrangler. Build cache keyed on SHA-256 of source CSV files to skip recompilation when data unchanged. Environments: production (main branch), preview (all other branches auto-deployed). Seasonal archive flow: CI parameter archive=true triggers R2 upload step and alias deployment creation for /season/[year]. Rollback: live site via Cloudflare Pages dashboard (last 10 builds retained); archive aliases point to immutable R2 paths and never need rollback.
target_output.observability: Cloudflare Web Analytics tracks page views, unique visitors, and Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, FID) per page path. Key metrics to monitor: LCP on /rankings/[game] target <2s, player profile pages target <1.5s. GitHub Actions build duration annotations alert if full build exceeds 7 minutes (headroom before 8-minute limit). Cloudflare R2 upload logs confirm archive snapshot size and completion per seasonal build run.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloudflare Pages free tier handles up to 500 builds/month and unlimited requests. Cloudflare Web Analytics is free. R2 storage for seasonal archives estimated at <5GB across 10 seasons at ~$0.015/GB/month. Total expected cost under $5/month. Traffic scaling handled automatically by Cloudflare edge with no origin server to scale.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): Core Astro content collections for players, teams, matches for 2 primary game titles. Seasonal rankings leaderboard with Fuse.js search. HMAC CSV checksum verification in CI. Cloudflare Pages deploy pipeline. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Remaining 3 game titles ingested. Tournament bracket SVG generation. Phase 3 (1 week): Seasonal archive versioning with R2 storage, alias deployment configuration. Cloudflare Web Analytics instrumentation and Core Web Vitals baseline.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode throughout Astro project and data compilation scripts. Zod schemas are the single source of truth for all data shapes; generated types used in both build scripts and Astro content collections. Each game title's data must compile independently to support parallel build shards. Commit CSV exports and compiled JSON together so build is reproducible from any commit. No inline scripts in Astro components; Chart.js islands must use client:visible directive.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Astro content collection build time exceeds 8 minutes as player count grows beyond 1000 per game title. Mitigation: partition collections by game_title and run parallel build shards in GitHub Actions matrix strategy; benchmark per season. Risk: HMAC key rotation requires coordinated update between league admin tooling and GitHub Actions secret. Mitigation: document key rotation runbook and test in staging before production. Tradeoff: static-only architecture means live match score updates require a full redeploy; acceptable given league match cadence but noted for future Durable Objects evaluation. Tradeoff: client-side Fuse.js search over server-side search API avoids runtime cost at the expense of search index size; monitor index JSON file size per game title.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Astro content collections over Next.js SSG for superior static output and zero JS by default. Confirmed Cloudflare Web Analytics over GA4 to avoid cookie consent banner. Chose Fuse.js client-side search over Cloudflare Workers AI search to stay within static-only constraint. Seasonal archive aliases confirmed as separate Cloudflare Pages deployments pointing to R2 to isolate from live site build cycle.

metadata.notes: Round 3 e-sports static stats site; resolves archive versioning and CSV authentication issues from round 2 audit.

profile.domain: Cloud Infrastructure Consolidation - Mobile Game Studio

project_goal.value: Right-size and consolidate cloud infrastructure for a mid-sized mobile game studio running dozens of underutilised microservices across multiple AWS accounts, reducing monthly spend by migrating to shared ECS clusters with spot instance strategies
project_goal.rationale: Studio cloud bill has grown 3x in 2 years without proportional traffic growth; consolidation is the top infrastructure priority

target_users.value: Platform engineering team and game service developers who deploy and operate studio microservices
target_users.rationale: Internal-only tooling for engineers managing AWS resources

project_class.rationale: No user-facing product; purely platform and environment architecture with Terraform, ECS, and cost tooling

capabilities.rationale: Infrastructure automation, Go tooling scripts, and cost analytics dashboards cover all deliverables

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-account AWS consolidation with spot instance strategies and live service migration requires careful sequencing

risk_level.rationale: Studio internal services; no player PII in consolidated infrastructure but service disruption risk during migration is real

data_sensitivity.rationale: Infrastructure metadata, cost allocations, and internal service configs are studio-confidential but not player data

external_exposure.rationale: All tooling accessed by engineers inside studio VPN; no public endpoints

access_model.value: AWS IAM with SSO federation; engineers assume scoped IAM roles per environment via AWS SSO permission sets
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege role assumption model aligned with SOC 2 requirements

feature_scope.value: Terraform modules for shared ECS clusters, spot capacity provider configuration, cross-account service migration tooling, consolidated CloudWatch logging, cost allocation tagging enforcement, Grafana dashboards for per-service spend and utilisation
feature_scope.rationale: Full consolidation scope agreed in engineering all-hands

mvp_scope.value: Shared ECS cluster Terraform module, spot capacity provider configuration, and cost allocation tagging enforcement across all accounts
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum to demonstrate cost savings before migrating all services

security_baseline.value: AWS IAM least-privilege, SSO, CloudTrail audit logging across all accounts, Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB lock, no direct console access in production accounts
security_baseline.rationale: SOC 2 Type II controls for infrastructure access

frontend_stack.value: No frontend; Grafana dashboards for internal cost and utilisation visibility
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure project with no user-facing UI

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with custom tooling scripts for drift detection, right-sizing recommendations, and migration automation
backend_stack.rationale: Studio has existing Go expertise; lightweight CLI tooling preferred over heavy frameworks

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for cost allocation tracking and migration state; Redis 7 for spot interruption event buffering
data_platform.rationale: Lightweight persistence for tooling metadata; not application data

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate with Spot capacity providers across consolidated shared clusters per environment
hosting_target.rationale: Target architecture for all studio services post-consolidation

privacy_retention_policy.value: Cost allocation records retained 3 years for financial audit; infrastructure event logs retained 1 year per SOC 2; no player data in scope
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial audit trail and SOC 2 log retention requirements

future_scope.value: Automated right-sizing recommendations pushed to Jira, multi-region active-active failover configuration, FinOps anomaly alerting with Slack integration
future_scope.rationale: Post-consolidation optimisation roadmap

constraints.value: Zero-downtime migration for all live game services; no single-account outage allowed; Buildkite as the only CI platform
constraints.rationale: Studio games are live; player-facing services cannot be interrupted

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for service metrics; AWS Cost Explorer integration; PagerDuty alerts on spot interruption SLO breach
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing monitoring stack extended for infrastructure cost visibility

execution_preference.value: Iterative migration waves: one game title's services per wave with full rollback capability before proceeding
execution_preference.rationale: Reduces blast radius of migration errors

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II: studio internal security programme requires CloudTrail in all accounts, least-privilege IAM, and annual pen test
compliance_context.rationale: Studio carries SOC 2 certification required by enterprise game publisher contracts

project.goal: Consolidate multi-account AWS microservices onto shared ECS Fargate clusters with spot strategies
project.target_users: Platform engineering and game service developers
project.timeline: 6-month migration programme

backend.tooling: Custom CLI scripts for drift detection and migration automation
backend.api: Internal CLI commands, no HTTP API

security.auth: AWS IAM SSO with scoped permission sets

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for cost tracking and migration state
data.cache: Redis 7 for spot interruption event buffer

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate with Spot capacity providers
devops.environments: dev, staging, production per game title

reasoner_reviews.product: The cost reduction goal is well-defined: consolidate underutilised per-service ECS clusters into shared clusters with mixed capacity providers. Spot instance strategies for non-critical game services (analytics workers, batch jobs) are the fastest path to 40-50% compute savings. Live game server services must stay on on-demand baseline to protect player experience.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Shared ECS cluster architecture requires careful namespace isolation between game titles using ECS capacity provider strategies and task definition families. The migration tooling in Go should generate Terraform plan diffs for engineer review before applying. Terraform state must be per-account to avoid cross-account blast radius on a bad apply.
reasoner_reviews.security: SOC 2 requires CloudTrail enabled in every AWS account including newly consolidated ones. IAM permission sets via AWS SSO should be reviewed per migration wave to ensure no privilege escalation occurs when services move accounts. All Terraform runs should be gated behind Buildkite approval steps in production.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero-downtime migration means ECS service migration must use blue-green deployment: bring up tasks in the target cluster, verify health checks, then drain and deregister from source cluster. Buildkite pipeline must have a manual gate before each production migration wave.

backend.service_design: Go 1.22 CLI tooling with subcommands: drift-audit (compares live ECS service configs against Terraform state), right-size (queries CloudWatch metrics to recommend CPU/memory adjustments), migrate-wave (generates migration Terraform plan for a named game title wave).
backend.background_jobs: Buildkite scheduled pipeline runs weekly drift-audit and right-size recommendation jobs; outputs written to PostgreSQL cost_allocations table and surfaced in Grafana.
backend.failure_handling: Migration wave tooling supports --dry-run flag; all Terraform applies gated behind Buildkite approval step; automatic rollback script drains new cluster tasks and re-enables source cluster if health check fails within 5-minute window.
backend.backend_risks: Spot interruption during a migration wave could leave tasks split across source and target clusters; Redis interruption event buffer must flush before wave completion is confirmed.

security.auth_design: AWS SSO with permission sets: PlatformEngineerReadOnly for audit access, PlatformEngineerDeployer for staging applies, PlatformEngineerProduction for production applies (requires MFA and manager approval ticket).
security.authorization_model: Per-account IAM boundaries prevent cross-account resource modification; Terraform state S3 buckets have bucket policies restricting write to the Buildkite pipeline role only.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail enabled in all accounts with log archival to a dedicated audit S3 bucket in the security account; CloudTrail logs retained 1 year; Terraform plan output logged to Buildkite artefact store per apply.
security.security_risks: Account consolidation reduces IAM boundary count; mitigate by enforcing service control policies (SCPs) at the AWS Organisation level to limit blast radius per account type.

data.entities: aws_accounts (account_id, alias, environment, game_title, monthly_budget_usd, owner_team), ecs_clusters (cluster_arn, account_id, capacity_providers[], active_service_count, target_shared BOOL), service_definitions (service_name, cluster_arn, task_family, cpu_reservation, memory_reservation, spot_eligible BOOL, migration_status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/rolled_back), cost_allocations (id, account_id, service_name, month, actual_spend_usd, reserved_spend_usd, spot_savings_usd), spot_interruption_events (id, service_name, cluster_arn, interrupted_at, tasks_affected INT, recovery_duration_seconds INT), budget_alerts (id, account_id, threshold_usd, current_spend_usd, alert_sent_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS for cost_allocations, service_definitions, and aws_accounts; Redis 7 ElastiCache for spot_interruption_events buffer (TTL 24h); Terraform state in S3 with per-account prefixes.
data.consistency_notes: Migration state machine in service_definitions must be updated atomically with ECS service registration/deregistration to prevent split-brain during wave execution.

devops.deployment_topology: Buildkite agents running in the platform account ECS cluster; Terraform modules in a dedicated module registry S3 bucket; shared ECS clusters provisioned in game-title-grouped AWS accounts.
devops.ci_cd_design: Buildkite pipelines: (1) weekly drift-audit and right-size (automated), (2) migration wave (manual trigger, requires approval step before production apply, auto-rollback on health check failure within 5 min). Terraform plan artefacts uploaded to Buildkite on every run.
devops.observability_stack: Grafana dashboards: per-service ECS CPU/memory utilisation vs reservation (right-sizing view), monthly cost by game title and account, spot savings trend, spot interruption rate by service. Prometheus scrapes ECS task metrics via CloudWatch Metrics Streams.
devops.cost_controls: AWS Budgets alerts at 80% and 100% of per-account monthly budget; Buildkite migration job validates spot_savings_usd projection before proceeding; reserved instances for ECS Fargate baseline capacity reviewed quarterly.
devops.devops_risks: Spot interruption spikes during high-demand AWS capacity periods (Black Friday, game launch events) could exceed SLO thresholds; mitigation is per-service on-demand baseline minimum task count configuration.

ISS-001.title: Migration rollback window too short for large services
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan defined a 5-minute health check window before automatic rollback, but large game server services with slow JVM startup times require up to 12 minutes to reach healthy state, making the 5-minute window cause false rollbacks.

history.note: Raised by service owners during review; JVM-based game services take 8-12 minutes to initialise

focus_issues.title: Migration rollback window too short for large services
focus_issues.detail: 5-minute health check window triggers false rollbacks for JVM-based game services that require 8-12 minutes to start.

latest_recommendations: Make the rollback health check timeout configurable per service definition, with a default of 5 minutes and a maximum of 15 minutes for JVM-based services
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Mobile Game Studio AWS Consolidation v1

convergence_state.reason: High severity rollback window issue unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Solid infrastructure consolidation plan with good Terraform module design, but the fixed rollback window is a high-severity operational risk for JVM-based services.
strengths: Spot capacity provider strategy well-specified with per-service spot_eligible flag
Buildkite approval gate before production applies correctly enforces change control
PostgreSQL migration state machine prevents split-brain during wave execution
concerns: 5-minute health check rollback window insufficient for JVM-based game services
No documentation of how service startup time requirements are captured in service_definitions
blocking_issues: Fixed 5-minute rollback window will cause false rollbacks for known JVM services, making production migrations operationally unsafe
recommendations: Add a startup_timeout_seconds field to service_definitions defaulting to 300s with a maximum of 900s; migration wave tooling uses this field to configure per-service health check window

issue_updates.title: Migration rollback window too short for large services
issue_updates.detail: Deployment operations section specifies a flat 5-minute rollback window with no mechanism to override per service type

previous_plan.title: Mobile Game Studio AWS Consolidation v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Terraform-driven consolidation of multi-account AWS microservices onto shared ECS Fargate clusters with spot capacity providers, managed by Go CLI tooling and Buildkite CI with approval gates.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Buildkite migration wave pipeline with manual approval gate; blue-green ECS task migration with 5-minute health check window before automatic rollback; Terraform state per account.
previous_plan.data_model: aws_accounts, ecs_clusters, service_definitions, cost_allocations, spot_interruption_events, budget_alerts stored in PostgreSQL 16.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: AWS SSO with scoped IAM permission sets, CloudTrail in all accounts, Terraform state locked in S3 with DynamoDB, SOC 2 controls maintained.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for a multi-account AWS ECS consolidation project. One high-severity issue: ISS-001 requires making the migration rollback health check timeout configurable per service rather than a flat 5 minutes. The fix is adding startup_timeout_seconds to service_definitions and threading it through the migration wave tooling and Buildkite pipeline. This is a targeted change to the data model and deployment operations sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Added startup_timeout_seconds INT to the service_definitions entity (default 300, max 900). Migration wave Go tooling reads this field when configuring the ECS deployment circuit breaker and health check grace period. Buildkite wave pipeline step now displays the configured timeout in the approval summary so engineers can verify before proceeding.
fix_report.expected_outcome: JVM-based game services with 8-12 minute startup times use a 720s timeout; fast Go microservices continue with the 300s default; false rollbacks eliminated

target_output.title: Mobile Game Studio AWS Infrastructure Consolidation
target_output.executive_summary: Terraform-driven consolidation of a mobile game studio's multi-account AWS microservices onto shared ECS Fargate clusters using mixed on-demand and spot capacity providers. Go 1.22 CLI tooling automates drift detection, right-sizing recommendations, and zero-downtime blue-green migration waves. Buildkite pipelines gate all production changes behind engineer approval. PostgreSQL 16 tracks migration state and cost allocations; Grafana dashboards surface per-game-title spend and spot savings trends.
target_output.architecture_overview: Multi-account AWS architecture with a central platform account hosting shared ECS clusters, Buildkite agents, and cost analytics tooling. Individual game title accounts are migrated wave-by-wave from isolated single-service ECS clusters to the shared cluster using blue-green task migration. Terraform 1.7 modules define the shared cluster configuration, capacity provider strategies, and per-account IAM boundaries. Go CLI tooling (drift-audit, right-size, migrate-wave subcommands) provides the migration automation layer. All Terraform state is stored per-account in S3 with DynamoDB locking. A dedicated security account aggregates CloudTrail logs from all accounts for SOC 2 audit evidence.
target_output.technology_stack: Infrastructure-as-Code: Terraform 1.7 with S3 module registry and per-account state. Automation tooling: Go 1.22 CLI with subcommands (drift-audit, right-size, migrate-wave). Container orchestration: ECS Fargate with spot and on-demand capacity providers. CI/CD: Buildkite with approval gates. Data: PostgreSQL 16 (RDS) for migration state and cost allocations, Redis 7 (ElastiCache) for spot interruption event buffering. Observability: Grafana + Prometheus via CloudWatch Metrics Streams. Identity: AWS IAM with SSO permission sets.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Drift Audit: weekly Go CLI job compares live ECS service CPU/memory reservations against Terraform-declared values and reports divergence per service per account. Right-Sizing Engine: queries 30-day CloudWatch p95 CPU and memory metrics per ECS service and generates Terraform variable override recommendations. Migration Wave: blue-green ECS task migration per game title wave with configurable per-service startup_timeout_seconds, health check validation, and automatic rollback. Cost Dashboard: Grafana views showing monthly spend by game title, spot savings vs on-demand baseline, and budget alert status. SOC 2 Audit Trail: consolidated CloudTrail log aggregation in security account with 1-year retention.
target_output.system_components: AWS Organisations multi-account structure (platform account, per-game-title accounts, security account), Shared ECS Fargate clusters in platform account with spot + on-demand capacity providers, Terraform module S3 registry, Buildkite agents on ECS (platform account), Go CLI tooling binaries published to internal S3, PostgreSQL 16 RDS (migration state and cost tracking), Redis 7 ElastiCache (spot event buffer), Grafana + Prometheus stack, CloudWatch Metrics Streams to Prometheus, CloudTrail aggregation in security account S3.
target_output.workflows: Cost Audit workflow: Buildkite scheduled pipeline runs weekly; Go drift-audit subcommand scans all accounts via AWS SDK, compares ECS service specs against Terraform state, writes divergence records to service_definitions table, outputs Grafana-linked Buildkite annotation. Migration Wave workflow: engineer triggers Buildkite migrate-wave pipeline specifying target game title; tooling generates Terraform plan showing new task definitions on shared cluster; approval step displays plan diff and per-service startup_timeout_seconds values for review; on approval, Terraform applies; ECS waits up to startup_timeout_seconds per service for healthy tasks; traffic drains from source cluster; PostgreSQL migration_status updated to 'completed'; on failure within timeout, rollback drains new tasks and re-enables source cluster, status set to 'rolled_back'.
target_output.data_model: aws_accounts (account_id VARCHAR PK, alias, environment ENUM dev/staging/production, game_title, monthly_budget_usd DECIMAL, owner_team). ecs_clusters (cluster_arn VARCHAR PK, account_id FK, capacity_providers TEXT[], active_service_count INT, is_shared BOOL). service_definitions (service_name VARCHAR, cluster_arn FK, task_family, cpu_reservation INT, memory_reservation INT, spot_eligible BOOL, startup_timeout_seconds INT DEFAULT 300 CHECK <= 900, migration_status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/rolled_back, migrated_at TIMESTAMP). cost_allocations (id UUID PK, account_id FK, service_name, month DATE, actual_spend_usd DECIMAL, spot_savings_usd DECIMAL). spot_interruption_events (id UUID PK, service_name, cluster_arn, interrupted_at TIMESTAMP, tasks_affected INT, recovery_duration_seconds INT). budget_alerts (id UUID PK, account_id FK, threshold_usd DECIMAL, current_spend_usd DECIMAL, alert_sent_at TIMESTAMP).
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API surface. Go CLI interface: drift-audit --account-id [id] --output table|json, right-size --account-id [id] --lookback-days 30 --output terraform-vars, migrate-wave --game-title [name] --env production --dry-run, rollback-wave --game-title [name] --env production. Terraform module inputs: shared_cluster_name, capacity_provider_strategy (on_demand_base, spot_weight), allowed_game_title_tags. Internal: PostgreSQL accessed via Go database/sql with pgx driver; Redis accessed via go-redis for event buffering.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 Type II controls: CloudTrail enabled in every account with logs shipped to dedicated security account S3 bucket (1-year retention, S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode). IAM permission sets via AWS SSO: PlatformEngineerReadOnly (audit), PlatformEngineerDeployer (staging applies), PlatformEngineerProduction (production applies, MFA required). Service Control Policies at AWS Organisation level prevent IAM policy escalation across account boundaries. Terraform state S3 buckets have bucket policies restricting writes to Buildkite pipeline IAM role only; DynamoDB lock prevents concurrent applies. Buildkite production pipeline requires manual approval step with Jira ticket reference before any production Terraform apply. Go tooling authenticates to AWS via assumed IAM roles with 1-hour session tokens.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Buildkite pipelines: (1) Weekly drift-audit and right-size (fully automated, no approval gate, Grafana annotation on completion). (2) Migration wave (manual trigger): Step 1 generates Terraform plan for target game title and uploads plan artefact; Step 2 displays per-service migration summary including startup_timeout_seconds values and spot_savings_usd projection; Step 3 requires engineer approval and Jira ticket; Step 4 applies Terraform and invokes ECS deployment with per-service startup timeout; Step 5 validates all services healthy; Step 6 drains source cluster; on failure at Step 4-6, auto-rollback re-enables source cluster and sets migration_status to rolled_back. Zero-downtime guaranteed via blue-green: new tasks registered to ALB target group and verified healthy before source tasks deregistered. Terraform state: per-account S3 buckets in platform account with DynamoDB lock. Buildkite agents run on ECS Fargate in platform account with least-privilege IAM role.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards (Prometheus data source via CloudWatch Metrics Streams): per-service ECS CPU and memory reservation vs actual utilisation (right-sizing view), monthly cost by game title with spot savings breakdown, spot interruption rate per service over rolling 30 days, migration wave status board (pending/in_progress/completed/rolled_back per service). PagerDuty alerts on: spot interruption rate > 5% for any on-demand-eligible service, ECS service unhealthy task count > 0 for >3 minutes post-migration, monthly budget_alerts threshold breached. Buildkite annotations link directly to Grafana per-wave dashboards after each migration run.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Target 40-50% compute cost reduction via spot capacity for analytics workers and batch jobs (spot_eligible=true). On-demand baseline task minimums maintained per game title for live player-facing services. AWS Budgets per-account alerts at 80% and 100% of monthly_budget_usd. Reserved Fargate capacity for baseline on-demand tasks reviewed quarterly. Estimated studio-wide monthly saving: $18K-$25K based on current $55K compute spend with 45% spot-eligible workloads.
target_output.phased_implementation: Wave 0 (2 weeks): Deploy shared ECS cluster Terraform module in platform account. Configure spot capacity providers. Migrate one non-production analytics service as proof-of-concept to validate tooling. Wave 1 (4 weeks): Migrate all analytics workers and batch job services across all game titles (all spot_eligible). Validate spot savings against projection. Wave 2 (6 weeks): Migrate staging environments for all game title services. Wave 3 (6 weeks): Production migration per game title, one per week, with manual approval gates and 48-hour post-migration monitoring window before next title proceeds.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go CLI tooling: all AWS SDK calls use context with 30-second timeout; no global state; all PostgreSQL writes in transactions; migration_status state transitions tested with integration tests against a LocalStack ECS mock. Terraform modules: no hardcoded account IDs; all resources tagged with game_title, environment, and cost_centre. Buildkite pipeline YAML reviewed via terraform-pipeline-linter before merge.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: spot interruption spike during a game title launch event disrupts migration wave health checks. Mitigation: exclude game title from migration wave during the 2 weeks around any scheduled launch. Risk: JVM-based services with startup_timeout_seconds > 600 hold up migration wave for 10+ minutes. Mitigation: identify all JVM services during Wave 0 profiling and document startup times in service_definitions. Tradeoff: per-account Terraform state adds operational overhead vs single-state-per-region; accepted to limit blast radius on bad applies. Tradeoff: shared ECS cluster requires resource tagging discipline for cost attribution; enforced via SCPs blocking ECS task registration without required tags.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed per-account Terraform state over single centralised state for blast radius isolation. Selected Buildkite over GitHub Actions per studio constraint. Chose PostgreSQL over DynamoDB for migration state because relational queries are needed for cost attribution reports. startup_timeout_seconds capped at 900s to prevent indefinitely stuck migration waves.

metadata.notes: Round 2 AWS consolidation for mobile game studio; fixes configurable rollback window for JVM services.

profile.domain: AI-Powered Game QA Bug Triage - AAA Studio

project_goal.value: Replace a legacy manual bug reporting pipeline with an AI-powered QA triage system that automatically classifies crash dumps, assigns severity labels, deduplicates reports, and routes issues to the correct engineering team using an LLM-backed triage engine trained on historical ticket data
project_goal.rationale: QA team processes 300+ crash reports per day; manual triage adds 4+ hours to average time-to-fix; LLM triage is projected to reduce triage time by 80%

target_users.value: QA testers uploading crash dumps, game engineers reviewing triage results, QA leads monitoring triage accuracy in admin panel
target_users.rationale: Three distinct user types with different access levels identified in discovery

project_class.rationale: LLM-backed triage engine with embedding-based deduplication is the defining capability

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, ai_llm, integrations, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL data, Okta SSO auth, LLM triage, Jira integration, and React admin panel

complexity_level.rationale: Crash dump symbolicaton pipeline, vector embedding deduplication, LLM classification, and Jira integration in a single system

risk_level.rationale: Internal tooling with proprietary game build data; no player PII but IP sensitivity around unreleased game content in crash dumps

data_sensitivity.rationale: Crash dumps may contain proprietary game asset references and unreleased code; classified as studio-confidential internal data

external_exposure.rationale: Accessed only by studio staff inside GCP VPC; no public endpoints

access_model.value: SAML SSO via Okta with role-based access: QATester (submit crashes), QALead (review triage, manage routing rules), Engineer (view assigned tickets), Admin (system configuration)
access_model.rationale: Studio uses Okta for all internal tools; RBAC ensures QA testers cannot modify routing rules

feature_scope.value: Crash dump ingestion and symbolication, LLM severity classification and title generation, embedding-based deduplication with parent ticket linking, Jira issue auto-creation with routing to correct team, admin panel for routing rules management and triage accuracy monitoring, historical ticket data ingestion for LLM context
feature_scope.rationale: Full replacement of manual pipeline

mvp_scope.value: Crash dump ingestion, LLM triage (severity + title), Jira auto-creation with default routing rules for the 5 core engineering teams
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum to replace manual triage for the highest-volume crash categories

security_baseline.value: Okta SAML SSO for all access, TLS 1.3 in transit, GCP VPC internal-only endpoints, crash dumps stored encrypted in GCS with CMEK, no LLM API calls containing raw source code or asset paths
security_baseline.rationale: Proprietary game content must not leave studio infrastructure via LLM API calls

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript (admin panel only)
frontend_stack.rationale: Lightweight admin panel for routing rule configuration and triage monitoring; QA testers use CLI or Slack bot for crash submission

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI on GKE Autopilot
backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem for LLM integrations, symbolication libraries, and ML tooling; GKE Autopilot for managed scaling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for structured ticket data and routing rules; Redis 7 for async job queue; GCS for crash dump binary storage; PgVector extension for embedding storage and similarity search
data_platform.rationale: PgVector avoids a separate vector database while PostgreSQL handles relational ticket data

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in GCP us-central1
hosting_target.rationale: Studio standardised on GCP; Autopilot reduces node management overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: Crash dumps retained 90 days then deleted from GCS; triage decision records retained 2 years for model accuracy auditing; Jira tickets managed by existing Jira retention policy
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Storage cost management; model audit trail requirement

future_scope.value: Fine-tuned in-house severity classification model replacing LLM API calls, automated regression detection across crash clusters, Slack bot for triage result notifications to engineers
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap; current LLM approach establishes baseline for model training data collection

constraints.value: No raw source code or asset path strings may be sent to external LLM APIs; crash dump symbolication must complete within 90 seconds; Jira is the authoritative issue tracker
constraints.rationale: IP protection and QA tester workflow expectation

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for API latency and error rates; custom metrics for triage accuracy rate, deduplication hit rate, and average triage decision latency
observability_baseline.rationale: Studio Datadog contract covers GKE workloads; custom metrics required to track AI system performance

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; LLM triage component developed first with human-in-the-loop review mode before auto-Jira-creation goes live
execution_preference.rationale: Human review mode allows QA leads to validate triage accuracy before full automation

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II: internal tooling handling proprietary game builds; access logs and triage audit trail required for annual SOC 2 review
compliance_context.rationale: Studio SOC 2 programme covers all systems handling proprietary assets

llm_integration.value: OpenAI GPT-4o via API for crash triage classification and title generation; crash data sanitised before API call to strip source file paths and asset names; prompt includes symbolicated stack trace summary, error code, and historical similar ticket titles only
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o provides best accuracy for severity classification tasks; data sanitisation prevents IP leakage

project.goal: LLM-powered crash dump triage replacing manual QA bug routing pipeline
project.target_users: QA testers, game engineers, QA leads
project.timeline: Q2 delivery with human-in-the-loop mode first

frontend.scope: Admin panel for routing rules and accuracy dashboard

security.auth: Okta SAML SSO
security.data_controls: No raw source paths to LLM API

constraints.llm_data: No raw source code to external APIs

reasoner_reviews.product: The crash triage automation addresses a real QA bottleneck; 300+ daily crashes with manual 4-hour triage delay is a validated pain point. Human-in-the-loop review mode is the right launch strategy: it builds QA lead trust in triage accuracy before full automation. The admin panel routing rule editor is critical for QA leads who will need to tune team assignments as the game ships new modules.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI async architecture is well-suited for the async crash ingestion pipeline. Symbolication is the latency-critical path (90s SLA); recommend a dedicated GKE worker pool for Breakpad/Crashpad symbolication jobs separate from the LLM triage workers to prevent queue starvation. PgVector for embedding storage is pragmatic given the moderate scale (300 crashes/day, ~100K historical tickets); a dedicated vector DB is not justified at this volume.
reasoner_reviews.security: The constraint against sending raw source code to the LLM API is critical and must be implemented as a hard sanitisation step in the triage pipeline, not a best-effort filter. Crash dumps contain symbol tables with source file paths; symbolication must strip these before the LLM prompt is assembled. All access to the triage system must be logged with Okta identity for SOC 2 audit trail.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 90-second symbolication SLA is achievable with a dedicated GKE worker pool and Breakpad symbolication; benchmark shows 20-40s for typical crash dumps under 50MB. Jira integration must handle Jira rate limits gracefully; Jira Cloud API allows 10 requests/second per OAuth app; batch creation or queue-based retries needed for burst scenarios.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The embedding deduplication threshold needs explicit tuning guidance; a cosine similarity threshold that is too loose will under-deduplicate, flooding Jira with duplicate tickets, while too tight will over-deduplicate and miss distinct issues. The plan should specify an initial threshold value and a process for QA leads to adjust it via the admin panel.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application split into three modules: ingestion_service (crash upload, symbolication job dispatch), triage_service (LLM classification, embedding deduplication, Jira creation), admin_service (routing rules CRUD, accuracy metrics API). Async task execution via Celery with Redis broker.
backend.llm_integration_design: Triage pipeline: (1) extract sanitised stack trace summary (strip source paths, keep function names and error codes), (2) retrieve top-5 similar historical tickets via PgVector cosine similarity, (3) assemble prompt with sanitised trace, error code, and similar ticket titles, (4) GPT-4o returns JSON with severity ENUM and suggested_title, (5) confidence score below 0.7 routes to human review queue. Prompt template versioned in database for auditability.
backend.background_jobs: Celery worker pools: symbolication_pool (4 workers, 90s task timeout, dedicated GKE node pool), triage_pool (2 workers, 30s LLM timeout), jira_pool (1 worker with Jira API rate limit backoff). Redis queues: crash_symbolication, llm_triage, jira_create, human_review.
backend.failure_handling: Symbolication timeout: crash moved to human_review queue with symbolication_failed flag. LLM API error: exponential backoff x3, then human_review fallback. Jira API rate limit: jira_pool retries with Jira-recommended Retry-After header. Dead-letter queue for crashes failing all retry attempts with PagerDuty alert.
backend.backend_risks: GPT-4o prompt injection via crafted crash dump content (function names containing instruction-like text); mitigate with output schema enforcement (JSON mode) and output validation before Jira creation.

security.auth_design: Okta SAML 2.0 SSO; FastAPI validates SAML assertions via python-saml; session tokens stored in Redis with 8-hour TTL. React admin panel uses Okta React SDK for SAML redirect flow.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: QATester can only POST /crashes; Engineer can GET /tickets assigned to their team; QALead can manage routing rules and review human review queue; Admin can modify LLM configuration and deduplication threshold.
security.secrets_management: GCP Secret Manager for OpenAI API key, Jira OAuth token, Okta SAML cert; mounted into GKE pods as environment variables via GKE Workload Identity. Crash dumps encrypted in GCS with CMEK; KMS key rotation annual.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every triage decision logged to triage_decisions table with Okta user_id, prompt_version, severity output, confidence score, and jira_issue_key. QA lead overrides logged separately. All logs shipped to Datadog with 90-day retention.

data.entities: crash_reports (id UUID PK, uploaded_by_user_id, game_build_version, platform ENUM pc/ps5/xbox, crash_dump_gcs_path, symbolicated_trace_text, symbolication_status ENUM pending/completed/failed, submitted_at), triage_decisions (id UUID PK, crash_report_id FK, severity_label ENUM critical/high/medium/low, suggested_title TEXT, confidence_score FLOAT, jira_issue_key, routing_team_id, decision_mode ENUM auto/human_review, prompt_version, decided_at), deduplication_clusters (id UUID PK, parent_crash_id FK, member_crash_ids UUID[], embedding_centroid VECTOR(1536), created_at), team_routing_rules (id UUID PK, team_name, module_pattern REGEX, severity_filter ENUM[], priority INT), bug_tickets (jira_issue_key VARCHAR PK, crash_report_id FK, status ENUM open/in_progress/resolved, assigned_team, created_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with PgVector extension; crash_reports.symbolicated_trace_text full-text indexed; deduplication_clusters.embedding_centroid stored as VECTOR(1536) with ivfflat index (lists=100) for cosine similarity search. GCS bucket for crash dump binaries with CMEK and 90-day object lifecycle rule.
data.analytics_events: triage_accuracy_review (triggered when QA lead overrides auto triage; captures original severity, corrected severity, crash_report_id for model improvement dataset)

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1; three Kubernetes Deployments: api (FastAPI, 2-4 replicas HPA), symbolication-worker (Celery, dedicated node pool, 2-8 replicas), triage-worker (Celery, 2-4 replicas). React admin SPA served from GCS bucket via Cloud CDN.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint (ruff + mypy), pytest (90% coverage gate for triage and deduplication modules), docker build + push to Artifact Registry, helm upgrade --install to dev/staging/production namespaces. Staging deploy requires manual approval.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM on all GKE pods; custom metrics: crash_triage_latency_p99, deduplication_hit_rate, llm_confidence_score_avg, human_review_queue_depth. Datadog monitors alert on: triage latency p99 > 120s, deduplication hit rate drops >20% in 1 hour (possible embedding pipeline failure).
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to previous release on failed readiness probe; symbolication_pool and triage_pool workers drain current tasks before pod termination via SIGTERM handler.

target_output.thinking_summary: First pass for an AI-powered game QA crash triage system. Key architectural decisions: async Celery pipeline with dedicated worker pools for symbolication and LLM triage prevents queue starvation; PgVector on PostgreSQL avoids a separate vector database at 300 crashes/day scale; data sanitisation layer strips source file paths before GPT-4o API calls to protect IP; human-in-the-loop mode via confidence threshold routing is the right first deployment posture. Compliance requirement: SOC 2 audit trail on every triage decision.
target_output.title: Game QA Crash Triage AI System
target_output.executive_summary: An LLM-backed crash triage system that replaces the manual QA bug routing pipeline at a AAA game studio, processing 300+ daily crash dumps through a symbolication-to-classification pipeline on GKE Autopilot. GPT-4o assigns severity labels and generates ticket titles from sanitised stack trace summaries; PgVector-based embedding deduplication links duplicate reports to parent Jira tickets rather than spawning new ones. A React admin panel gives QA leads visibility into triage accuracy and control over team routing rules. Human-in-the-loop review mode routes low-confidence decisions to QA leads before Jira creation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Async pipeline architecture on GKE Autopilot: crash dumps submitted by QA testers are stored in GCS (CMEK-encrypted) and dispatched to a Redis-backed Celery queue. A dedicated symbolication worker pool runs Breakpad/Crashpad symbolication within the 90-second SLA. Sanitised symbolicated traces are passed to the triage worker pool, which assembles a GPT-4o prompt using the sanitised trace, error code, and top-5 similar historical tickets retrieved via PgVector cosine similarity. GPT-4o returns severity and suggested_title; decisions with confidence >= 0.7 auto-create Jira issues via Jira OAuth API; decisions below threshold route to the human review queue in the React admin panel. All triage decisions are immutably logged to PostgreSQL for SOC 2 audit and model accuracy tracking. QA lead overrides feed a correction dataset for future fine-tuning.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI (async), Celery 5 with Redis broker, python-saml for Okta SAML, psycopg3, openai SDK. LLM: GPT-4o via OpenAI API. Vector store: PgVector extension on PostgreSQL 16 (VECTOR(1536), ivfflat index). Symbolication: Google Breakpad library via Python ctypes wrapper. Database: PostgreSQL 16 (Cloud SQL) + PgVector. Queue/Cache: Redis 7 (Memorystore). Blob storage: GCS with CMEK. Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Okta React SDK. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, Artifact Registry, GCP Secret Manager, Cloud CDN. CI/CD: GitHub Actions + Helm. Observability: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Crash Ingestion: QA tester CLI or Slack bot uploads crash dump binary to GCS via signed URL; metadata recorded in crash_reports table; symbolication job dispatched to Redis queue. Symbolication: dedicated Celery workers run Breakpad symbolication, strip source file paths from output, write symbolicated_trace_text to crash_reports. LLM Triage: triage workers retrieve PgVector similar tickets, assemble sanitised prompt, call GPT-4o, parse JSON severity+title response, compute confidence score. Deduplication: cosine similarity against deduplication_clusters centroids; matches link to parent ticket and skip Jira creation. Jira Auto-Creation: triage worker maps severity+team routing rule to Jira project and priority, creates issue via Jira OAuth, stores jira_issue_key. Human Review Queue: low-confidence decisions displayed in admin panel for QA lead approval or override. Admin Panel: routing rules editor, accuracy dashboard showing auto vs override rates per severity, deduplication threshold configuration.
target_output.system_components: GKE Autopilot cluster (api Deployment, symbolication-worker Deployment on dedicated node pool, triage-worker Deployment), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with PgVector, Memorystore Redis 7 (Celery broker + session store), GCS bucket (crash dump binaries, CMEK), React admin SPA served via GCS + Cloud CDN, GCP Secret Manager (OpenAI key, Jira OAuth, Okta cert), Datadog agent DaemonSet, Jira Cloud (external integration), Okta (external SAML IdP).
target_output.workflows: Crash Ingestion and Auto-Triage: QA tester uploads .dmp file via CLI (POST /v1/crashes with multipart form); FastAPI stores binary to GCS, creates crash_reports record (status=pending), pushes job to crash_symbolication Redis queue; symbolication Celery worker claims job, runs Breakpad on the binary (timeout 90s), strips source file paths from symbol output, writes symbolicated_trace_text, updates status to 'symbolicated', pushes to llm_triage queue; triage worker queries PgVector for top-5 embeddings within cosine distance < 0.15 (deduplication check), assembles GPT-4o prompt (sanitised trace + error code + similar ticket titles), receives JSON response, evaluates confidence; if >= 0.7 and no deduplication match, pushes to jira_create queue; jira worker calls Jira OAuth API and creates issue. Human Review Override: crash with confidence < 0.7 appears in admin panel review queue; QA lead sees triage suggestion, can approve or change severity/team; on approval, jira_create job dispatched; override logged to triage_decisions with corrected values for model accuracy dataset.
target_output.data_model: crash_reports (id UUID PK, uploaded_by_user_id VARCHAR, game_build_version VARCHAR, platform ENUM pc/ps5/xbox/pc_dev, crash_dump_gcs_path VARCHAR, symbolicated_trace_text TEXT, symbolication_status ENUM pending/symbolicated/failed, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). triage_decisions (id UUID PK, crash_report_id UUID FK, severity_label ENUM critical/high/medium/low, suggested_title TEXT, confidence_score FLOAT CHECK 0-1, jira_issue_key VARCHAR, routing_team_id UUID FK, decision_mode ENUM auto/human_review, override_by_user_id VARCHAR NULLABLE, prompt_version INT, decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ). deduplication_clusters (id UUID PK, parent_crash_report_id UUID FK, member_crash_report_ids UUID[], embedding_centroid VECTOR(1536), similarity_threshold FLOAT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). team_routing_rules (id UUID PK, team_name VARCHAR, module_pattern TEXT, jira_project_key VARCHAR, severity_filter TEXT[], priority INT, active BOOL). bug_tickets (jira_issue_key VARCHAR PK, crash_report_id UUID FK, status ENUM open/in_progress/resolved, assigned_team VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). engineer_assignments (jira_issue_key VARCHAR FK, engineer_okta_id VARCHAR, assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI REST API on /v1: POST /v1/crashes (multipart upload, returns crash_report_id), GET /v1/crashes/:id/status (symbolication and triage status), GET /v1/review-queue (QA lead: list pending human review items), POST /v1/review-queue/:id/decision (QA lead override: severity + team), GET /v1/admin/routing-rules (list), POST /v1/admin/routing-rules (create), PUT /v1/admin/routing-rules/:id (update), DELETE /v1/admin/routing-rules/:id (delete), GET /v1/admin/accuracy (triage accuracy metrics, time range param), GET /v1/admin/config (deduplication threshold, prompt version), PUT /v1/admin/config (update threshold). Auth: Okta SAML session JWT on all endpoints; RBAC enforced per endpoint by FastAPI dependency injection.
target_output.security_and_compliance: IP protection: crash dump sanitisation strips all source file paths, symbol file names, and asset reference strings before GPT-4o API call; only function signatures, error codes, and stack frame offsets included in prompt. This is enforced in the symbolication worker, not the triage worker, so sanitisation is unavoidable. Okta SAML 2.0 SSO for all access; RBAC enforced at FastAPI layer; session tokens in Redis with 8-hour TTL. TLS 1.3 in transit; GCS CMEK for crash dump binaries. GCP Secret Manager for all credentials; GKE Workload Identity for pod-level secret access without long-lived keys. SOC 2 controls: all triage_decisions rows are immutable (no UPDATE/DELETE permitted, only INSERTs); QA lead overrides create new triage_decision rows referencing the original. CloudAuditLogs for all Secret Manager and GCS access. Access logs forwarded to Datadog with 90-day retention.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1. Kubernetes Deployments: api (2 replicas min, HPA scale to 8 on CPU 70%), symbolication-worker (GKE node pool with 4-core nodes, 2 replicas min, HPA scale to 8, Celery concurrency 2 per pod given memory needs), triage-worker (2 replicas min, HPA scale to 4). GitHub Actions CI: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest with 90% coverage gate for triage and deduplication modules, docker build and push to Artifact Registry, helm upgrade --install with --atomic flag. Environments: dev (auto-deploy on merge to main), staging (manual approval gate), production (manual approval gate). Helm chart values per environment. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16: single region with automatic daily backups, point-in-time recovery. Redis: Memorystore standard tier with automatic failover. React admin SPA: GitHub Actions builds and pushes to GCS bucket; Cloud CDN cache invalidated on deploy.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM instrumented on all GKE pods with dd-trace-py for FastAPI and Celery workers. Custom Datadog metrics: crash_triage_latency_p99 (target <120s end-to-end), symbolication_latency_p99 (target <90s), llm_triage_latency_p99 (target <30s), deduplication_hit_rate (daily ratio of duplicate-matched vs new Jira tickets), llm_confidence_score_avg (daily average; drop below 0.6 indicates prompt or LLM degradation), human_review_queue_depth (alert if >50 pending reviews). PagerDuty alerts on: triage latency p99 > 120s, symbolication failure rate > 5%, LLM confidence average drop > 20% vs 7-day baseline, dead-letter queue non-empty.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot costs scale with pod CPU/memory requests; estimated $800-1200/month for 300 crashes/day with HPA. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL: $150/month for db-n1-standard-2. GPT-4o API: ~$0.015 per triage call; 300 crashes/day = $4.50/day = $135/month. Deduplication reduces Jira noise and GPT-4o calls for duplicate crashes. GCS: minimal storage cost for 90-day retention of crash dumps. Total estimated: $1200-1600/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Crash ingestion pipeline with GCS storage. Breakpad symbolication worker. Sanitisation layer. GPT-4o triage with human-in-the-loop review mode (all decisions go to review queue). React admin panel with review queue and routing rule editor. Jira OAuth integration. Phase 2 (4 weeks): PgVector deduplication with admin-configurable threshold. Auto-Jira-creation for decisions with confidence >= 0.7. Accuracy dashboard with override rate tracking. Phase 3 (3 weeks): Slack bot for QA tester crash submission. Triage accuracy reporting for QA lead weekly review. Historical ticket ingestion to pre-populate PgVector embeddings.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: ruff for linting, mypy strict mode, pytest with 90% coverage gate for triage_service and deduplication modules. FastAPI: Pydantic v2 models for all request/response shapes; no raw dicts in endpoint handlers. LLM prompt template changes require a new prompt_version in the database and a unit test verifying sanitisation removes source paths. Celery task functions must be idempotent; all tasks accept a task_id to support retry without duplication. PgVector queries must include EXPLAIN ANALYZE benchmarks in PR for any new similarity search query.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o model update changes severity classification behaviour without notice, degrading triage accuracy. Mitigation: pin to a specific model version (gpt-4o-2024-11-20); monitor llm_confidence_score_avg daily; evaluate new model versions in staging before upgrading. Risk: PgVector ivfflat index accuracy degrades as embedding count grows beyond 500K; plan migration to HNSW index at that scale. Tradeoff: running symbolication and triage in separate Celery pools adds operational complexity but is necessary to prevent slow symbolication jobs from blocking LLM triage throughput. Tradeoff: PgVector over a dedicated vector database (Weaviate, Pinecone) saves operational overhead and cost at current scale but may need reassessment if crash volume scales 10x.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected PgVector over Weaviate for embedding storage given 300 crashes/day scale and desire to minimise infrastructure components. Confirmed GPT-4o over Claude for initial deployment due to existing OpenAI API access. Human-in-the-loop review mode confirmed as mandatory for Phase 1 to build QA lead trust before full automation. Deduplication threshold set initially at cosine similarity distance < 0.15 with admin-configurable override.

metadata.notes: First-pass strong plan for AI game QA triage system replacing manual pipeline; full LLM integration with data sanitisation for IP protection.

profile.domain: Multiplayer State Sync SDK for Browser and Node.js Games

project_goal.value: TypeScript/JavaScript SDK for integrating real-time multiplayer state synchronisation into browser-based and Node.js games, providing authoritative server reconciliation, client-side prediction, and delta-compressed snapshot delivery over WebSockets
project_goal.rationale: Game developers need a drop-in SDK that handles the complexity of authoritative server reconciliation and client prediction without building it from scratch

target_users.value: Game developers building browser-based or Node.js multiplayer games who want a library they can embed in their own server and client code
target_users.rationale: npm package consumers integrating SDK into their own infrastructure

project_class.rationale: Distributed as an npm package with no hosted deployment; consumer provides their own WebSocket server

capabilities.rationale: Server-side state engine, serialisation/delta format, and WebSocket transport abstraction

complexity_level.rationale: Client-side prediction with rollback, delta compression, and authoritative reconciliation are algorithmically complex

risk_level.rationale: No hosted service; SDK runs in consumer infrastructure; no data sensitivity risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK handles game state objects defined by consumer; no PII or financial data in scope

external_exposure.rationale: Open-source npm package published for public consumption

access_model.value: Open npm package; no authentication; consumer implements their own player auth layer
access_model.rationale: Library does not manage player identity; that is the consumer's responsibility

feature_scope.value: Authoritative server state engine with configurable tick rate, client-side prediction with input buffering, delta snapshot compression and delivery, client rollback and re-simulation on corrective snapshot receipt, game room lifecycle management, transport-agnostic WebSocket adapter interface
feature_scope.rationale: Core synchronisation feature set for competitive multiplayer games

mvp_scope.value: Server tick loop with authoritative snapshot generation, delta encoding between consecutive snapshots, WebSocket broadcast to room members, client prediction with rollback on correction receipt
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for a game developer to build a functional multiplayer prototype

security_baseline.value: Input validation on all state mutations to prevent malformed inputs crashing server; no authentication in SDK (consumer responsibility)
security_baseline.rationale: SDK must be resilient to malformed client messages without exposing SDK internals

frontend_stack.value: No frontend; client-side SDK module for browser and Node.js
frontend_stack.rationale: Library with dual ESM/CJS builds for browser and Node.js environments

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 (library target runtime)
backend_stack.rationale: Server-side SDK module runs in Node.js game server code

data_platform.value: No database; in-memory state only within the SDK; consumers manage their own persistence
data_platform.rationale: SDK is stateful in-process; no external data store

hosting_target.value: npm package; no hosted deployment
hosting_target.rationale: Distributed via npm registry; consumer provides hosting

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained by SDK; consumer owns all game state data
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: In-process library with no external data storage

future_scope.value: Lag compensation and rewind algorithms, interest management for large-world games, WebRTC DataChannel transport adapter, Unity WebGL integration guide
future_scope.rationale: Competitive game feature set expansion roadmap

constraints.value: Must support both browser (ESM) and Node.js (CJS/ESM) environments; zero external runtime dependencies; snapshot delta must achieve >= 60% size reduction vs full state for typical shooter game state objects
constraints.rationale: Bandwidth efficiency is critical for mobile game consumers on metered connections

observability_baseline.value: SDK emits optional event hooks for tick latency, snapshot delta ratio, and client prediction error rate that consumers can wire to their own telemetry; no built-in telemetry endpoint
observability_baseline.rationale: Library cannot dictate consumer telemetry stack

execution_preference.value: Test-driven development; all core algorithms have unit test coverage before integration; semantic versioning with public API stability guarantee
execution_preference.rationale: SDK consumers depend on stable public API; breaking changes require major version bump

project.goal: npm SDK for browser and Node.js multiplayer state synchronisation
project.target_users: Game developers
project.timeline: Open-source release in 8 weeks

security.input_validation: All state mutations validated; malformed inputs rejected gracefully

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK feature set maps correctly to the needs of competitive multiplayer game developers: client-side prediction eliminates input latency perception, delta compression reduces bandwidth for mobile consumers, and rollback on correction minimises visible artifacts. The transport-agnostic adapter interface is essential for consumer flexibility.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The core algorithmic challenge is the rollback re-simulation: when the client receives a corrective snapshot from the server, it must efficiently re-apply all buffered unacknowledged inputs from the confirmed tick forward. The input_commands buffer design is the critical data structure. Delta encoding between consecutive snapshots using a structural diff approach (tracking changed fields by path) is more portable than binary bit-mask diffing across JS object types.
reasoner_reviews.security: Zero external runtime dependencies is a significant security benefit for an npm package; no transitive supply chain risk. The SDK should validate that consumer-provided state mutation functions do not mutate the canonical state object directly, as this would break snapshot integrity.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 60% delta compression target is achievable for typical shooter state (positions, orientations, health values) using structural field diffing. Benchmark results should be included in the README with a reference game state schema.

backend.service_design: Two entry points: ServerRoom (authoritative tick loop, snapshot generation, delta encoding, broadcast) and ClientConnection (prediction engine, input buffer, rollback, snapshot application). Both exported as named TypeScript classes from the package root.
backend.api_patterns: ServerRoom: constructor(options: ServerRoomOptions), room.addPlayer(id, connection), room.removePlayer(id), room.applyInput(playerId, input: InputCommand), room.tick() (advance state, generate snapshot, compute delta, broadcast). ClientConnection: constructor(options: ClientOptions), client.sendInput(input: InputCommand), client.applyServerSnapshot(snapshot: Snapshot, tick: number), client.predictTick() (local simulation step).
backend.failure_handling: ServerRoom.applyInput validates input schema against consumer-registered InputSchema; malformed inputs throw InputValidationError (caught by consumer). Client rollback handles missing snapshots in buffer by requesting a full state resync via configurable onResyncRequired callback.
backend.scaling_notes: Single ServerRoom instance is single-threaded; consumer manages multiple rooms via multiple instances. SDK does not manage room-to-process assignment; that is the consumer's server framework responsibility.
backend.backend_risks: Consumer providing a state mutation function with side effects (setTimeout, I/O) inside the tick loop will cause non-deterministic rollback behaviour; document clearly in API reference and add a SideEffectDetectedWarning in development mode.

data.entities: GameRoom (roomId, tick: number, authoritative_state: T, player_states: Map<playerId, PlayerState>), PlayerState (playerId, last_acknowledged_tick: number, input_buffer: InputCommand[]), InputCommand (playerId, tick: number, sequence: number, payload: unknown), Snapshot (tick: number, state: T, acknowledged_input_sequences: Map<playerId, number>), DeltaPatch (from_tick: number, to_tick: number, changed_paths: PathDelta[], created_paths: PathDelta[], deleted_paths: string[]), ReconciliationFrame (confirmed_tick: number, confirmed_state: T, unacknowledged_inputs: InputCommand[], rollback_applied: boolean)
data.schema_notes: All entities are generic over state type T; consumers provide their state schema. DeltaPatch uses JSON Pointer paths (RFC 6901) to identify changed fields, enabling structural diff without binary serialisation. TypeScript generics ensure consumer state type is consistent between server and client at compile time.
data.consistency_notes: Tick counter is the single sequencing primitive; both server and client use tick-aligned buffers. InputCommand.sequence provides per-player ordering within a tick for games with multiple inputs per frame.
