security.auth_design: No auth in SDK; consumer injects player identity via addPlayer(id, connection) after their own auth validation.
security.abuse_prevention: ServerRoom enforces a configurable maxInputsPerTick per player; inputs beyond this limit are dropped with an onInputDropped callback. Prevents malicious clients from flooding the input buffer and causing O(n) rollback cost.
security.security_risks: Consumer providing unvalidated player inputs directly to applyInput without their own auth check could allow session spoofing; document the responsibility boundary clearly in security section of README.

devops.deployment_topology: npm package published to npm registry under a scoped package name. No hosted services. GitHub repository with automated semantic-release on merge to main.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: TypeScript typecheck, Vitest unit tests (ServerRoom tick loop, delta encoding, rollback re-simulation), integration test (full round-trip: server tick, client prediction, correction receipt, rollback), build dual ESM/CJS outputs with tsup, bundle size check (guard < 30KB gzip), publish to npm on tagged release.
devops.devops_risks: npm package name squatting; register the scoped package name before public announcement.

ISS-001.title: DeltaPatch encoding does not handle nested array element reordering
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan uses JSON Pointer path diffing for DeltaPatch but this approach treats array reordering as a sequence of individual path changes, producing large patches when game state arrays are reordered (e.g., player list sort order changes). This can exceed the 60% delta compression target for states with frequently reordered arrays.

history.note: Raised during data model review; JSON Pointer diff naive for arrays

focus_issues.title: DeltaPatch encoding does not handle nested array element reordering
focus_issues.detail: JSON Pointer path diffing produces oversized patches for game state with reordered arrays, risking 60% compression target failure.

latest_recommendations: Adopt a hybrid delta strategy: use JSON Pointer diffs for object properties but represent array changes as move operations (LCS-based) or use a move+patch encoding to handle reordering without full re-enumeration

convergence_state.reason: High severity delta encoding issue unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Well-structured SDK architecture with strong TypeScript API design, but the delta encoding strategy has a correctness gap for arrays that could prevent meeting the 60% compression SLA.
strengths: Generic state type design ensures consumer compile-time safety
Transport-agnostic adapter interface correctly decouples WebSocket implementation
Input validation and maxInputsPerTick abuse prevention well-specified
concerns: JSON Pointer path diffing generates O(n) changes for array reordering, undermining delta compression goals
No benchmarking methodology specified for the 60% compression target
blocking_issues: Delta encoding strategy is insufficient for game state objects with frequently reordered arrays (e.g., player leaderboard, sorted entity lists)
recommendations: Use RFC 6902 JSON Patch move operation semantics for array element relocation, computed via LCS diff to minimise patch size
Add a benchmark suite in the test directory with a reference shooter state schema to verify 60% compression target

issue_updates.title: DeltaPatch encoding does not handle nested array element reordering
issue_updates.detail: data_model and api_design sections describe JSON Pointer path diffing without addressing array move operations

previous_plan.title: Multiplayer State Sync SDK v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: TypeScript SDK with ServerRoom authoritative tick loop and ClientConnection prediction engine; delta-compressed snapshots over WebSocket; zero external runtime dependencies.
previous_plan.data_model: GameRoom, PlayerState, InputCommand, Snapshot, DeltaPatch (JSON Pointer paths), ReconciliationFrame. Delta encoding uses JSON Pointer path diffing.
previous_plan.development_guidelines: TDD; all core algorithms unit-tested; semantic versioning.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for a TypeScript multiplayer state sync SDK. One high-severity issue: ISS-001 requires upgrading the DeltaPatch encoding from naive JSON Pointer path diffing to a hybrid strategy that handles array reordering with LCS-computed move operations (RFC 6902 JSON Patch move semantics). This change affects the data_model (DeltaPatch shape), api_design (encoding/decoding methods), and development_guidelines (benchmark suite requirement). The rest of the architecture is solid and unchanged.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced naive JSON Pointer path diffing with a hybrid delta strategy: object property changes use JSON Pointer paths as before; array changes use RFC 6902 JSON Patch move operations computed via Myers LCS diff algorithm. DeltaPatch now includes an array_ops field (MoveOp[]) alongside changed_paths. Added a benchmark suite in tests/benchmarks/ with a reference shooter game state schema (100 entities with positions, orientations, health values) that asserts >= 60% delta compression ratio at each release gate.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Delta patches for game states with reordered arrays produce move operations rather than O(n) path changes; 60% compression benchmark passes for reference shooter state schema

target_output.title: Multiplayer State Sync SDK
target_output.executive_summary: A TypeScript/JavaScript SDK providing authoritative server state synchronisation, client-side prediction, and delta-compressed snapshot delivery for browser-based and Node.js multiplayer games. Distributed as a zero-external-dependency npm package with dual ESM/CJS builds. The ServerRoom class manages the authoritative tick loop and snapshot generation; ClientConnection handles input prediction and rollback re-simulation. Delta patches use a hybrid encoder: JSON Pointer paths for object field changes and RFC 6902 move operations for array reordering, meeting a >= 60% compression target against a reference shooter game state schema.
target_output.architecture_overview: Library-only architecture with no hosted services. Two main class exports: ServerRoom (server-side, Node.js) and ClientConnection (client-side, browser or Node.js). The server tick loop advances authoritative state by applying validated input commands, generates a full snapshot of the new state, computes a DeltaPatch against the last acknowledged state per player using the hybrid encoder (object diffs via JSON Pointer, array reordering via Myers LCS move ops), and broadcasts compressed delta bytes to each player's WebSocket connection. Clients apply deltas to their local prediction state; on receiving a corrective snapshot (server tick > last-acknowledged), ClientConnection rolls back to the confirmed state and re-simulates all buffered unacknowledged inputs forward. Transport is abstracted behind an ITransportAdapter interface, allowing consumers to plug in any WebSocket library or custom transport.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: TypeScript 5.4 (strict mode). Runtime targets: Node.js 22 (server), browser ESM (client). Build: tsup generating dual ESM/CJS bundles. Delta encoding: custom Myers LCS implementation (zero dependencies) for array move op computation; JSON Pointer (RFC 6901) for object field paths. Serialisation: MessagePack via a bundled minimal encoder (no external dep) for binary snapshot compression. Testing: Vitest for unit and integration tests; benchmark suite using Vitest bench(). CI: GitHub Actions. Publishing: npm registry with scoped package name.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ServerRoom: configurable tick rate (default 20Hz), addPlayer/removePlayer lifecycle, applyInput with per-player input validation and maxInputsPerTick throttle, tick() which advances state, computes per-player DeltaPatch, and broadcasts. DeltaEncoder: hybrid encoder with Myers LCS for array move ops and JSON Pointer diffing for object fields; produces DeltaPatch with changed_paths, array_ops, deleted_paths. ClientConnection: sendInput dispatches to consumer WebSocket and records InputCommand in prediction buffer, predictTick() runs consumer state function for local simulation, applyServerSnapshot() triggers rollback if server tick is ahead of last-acknowledged client tick and re-simulates buffered inputs. Event hooks: onTickComplete, onClientPredictionError, onSnapshotDeltaRatio (for consumer telemetry).
target_output.system_components: ServerRoom class (authoritative state machine, tick scheduler, per-player input buffer, DeltaEncoder), ClientConnection class (prediction state, input buffer, rollback engine, ITransportAdapter binding), DeltaEncoder module (Myers LCS array move op computation, JSON Pointer object diffing, MessagePack binary output), ITransportAdapter interface (consumer implements send(playerId, bytes), onMessage(playerId, bytes), onDisconnect(playerId)), InputValidator module (consumer-registered schema validation), npm package entry points (server: ./dist/server.js, client: ./dist/client.js).
target_output.workflows: Server tick loop workflow: every 1/tickRate seconds, ServerRoom.tick() is called by the consumer's game loop. The engine applies all pending InputCommand entries from each player's buffer (in sequence order), advancing authoritative state via the consumer-registered stateReducer function. For each connected player, DeltaEncoder.compute(previousPlayerSnapshot, currentState) generates a DeltaPatch using JSON Pointer diffs for changed object fields and Myers LCS for changed array orderings. The patch is serialised to MessagePack bytes and sent via ITransportAdapter.send(playerId, bytes). Snapshot stored as player's last-acknowledged state. Client rollback workflow: when ClientConnection.applyServerSnapshot(snapshot, serverTick) is called and serverTick > localConfirmedTick, the client discards local prediction state from localConfirmedTick forward, restores authoritative state from the snapshot, then re-applies all InputCommand entries in the local buffer with sequence numbers >= serverTick in order, calling the consumer-registered clientStateReducer for each, rebuilding the predicted present state.
target_output.data_model: GameRoom<T> (roomId: string, currentTick: number, authoritativeState: T, playerStates: Map<string, PlayerState<T>>). PlayerState<T> (playerId: string, lastAcknowledgedTick: number, lastSentSnapshot: T | null, inputBuffer: InputCommand[]). InputCommand (playerId: string, tick: number, sequence: number, payload: unknown - validated by consumer InputSchema). Snapshot<T> (tick: number, state: T, acknowledgedInputSequences: Map<string, number>). DeltaPatch (fromTick: number, toTick: number, changedPaths: PathDelta[], arrayOps: ArrayMoveOp[], deletedPaths: string[]). PathDelta (path: string RFC6901, value: unknown). ArrayMoveOp (arrayPath: string RFC6901, fromIndex: number, toIndex: number) - computed via Myers LCS diff on array element identity keys. ReconciliationFrame<T> (confirmedTick: number, confirmedState: T, unacknowledgedInputs: InputCommand[], rollbackApplied: boolean).
target_output.api_design: Package exports: named exports from 'your-package/server' and 'your-package/client'. ServerRoom<T>: constructor({ tickRateHz, stateReducer, inputSchema, maxInputsPerTick }), room.addPlayer(playerId: string, transport: ITransportAdapter), room.removePlayer(playerId: string), room.applyInput(playerId: string, input: InputCommand): void | InputValidationError, room.tick(): TickResult. ClientConnection<T>: constructor({ clientStateReducer, onResyncRequired }), client.bindTransport(transport: ITransportAdapter), client.sendInput(input: InputCommand): void, client.predictTick(): T, client.applyServerSnapshot(snapshot: Snapshot<T>, tick: number): ReconciliationFrame<T>. DeltaEncoder: encode(prev: T, next: T): DeltaPatch, decode(base: T, patch: DeltaPatch): T. ITransportAdapter interface: send(playerId: string, data: Uint8Array): void, onMessage: (playerId: string, data: Uint8Array) => void, onDisconnect: (playerId: string) => void.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Input validation: all InputCommand payloads validated against consumer-registered Zod schema before applyInput processes them; malformed inputs throw InputValidationError with the offending payload redacted in the error message to prevent echo attacks. maxInputsPerTick per player enforced in ServerRoom; excess inputs dropped and onInputDropped callback fired. State mutation contract: consumer stateReducer must be a pure function; development builds emit SideEffectDetectedWarning via Proxy-based mutation detection on the state argument. No authentication in SDK; documented consumer responsibility boundary. Zero external runtime dependencies eliminates transitive supply chain risk.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Package distribution: tsup builds dual ESM output (dist/server.mjs, dist/client.mjs) and CJS fallbacks (dist/server.cjs, dist/client.cjs) with TypeScript declaration files. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: tsc --noEmit typecheck, Vitest unit tests (ServerRoom tick loop, DeltaEncoder object diff, DeltaEncoder array move op computation, ClientConnection rollback re-simulation), Vitest integration test (full server tick + client prediction + correction round-trip), benchmark suite asserting >= 60% delta compression on reference shooter state (100 entities), bundle size guard (< 30KB gzip total), npm publish on semver tag. Semantic versioning: major version bump for any public API change; changelog generated by conventional-commits.
target_output.observability: SDK does not emit telemetry directly; consumer wires event hooks. Available hooks: onTickComplete({ tick: number, tickDurationMs: number, connectedPlayers: number }), onSnapshotDeltaRatio({ fromTick: number, toTick: number, deltaBytes: number, fullStateBytes: number, ratio: number }), onClientPredictionError({ tick: number, predictedState: T, confirmedState: T, divergenceScore: number }), onInputDropped({ playerId: string, droppedCount: number }). Hook types exported for consumer TypeScript integration. Benchmark suite in tests/benchmarks/ provides reference metrics: tick loop throughput at 20Hz with 100 concurrent players, delta compression ratio for reference shooter state at 20 ticks/sec.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost; library distributed via npm. Benchmark targets ensure SDK performance: tick loop overhead < 1ms per tick for 100-player room on Node.js 22 LTS. Delta compression >= 60% for reference state reduces WebSocket bandwidth proportionally; at 20Hz with 100-byte full state, target is <40-byte average patch. Memory: PlayerState input buffer defaults to last 64 ticks; consumer configurable.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (4 weeks): ServerRoom tick loop, DeltaEncoder with hybrid Myers LCS + JSON Pointer encoding, basic ClientConnection with rollback. Vitest unit tests for all core modules. Benchmark suite with reference shooter state. Phase 2 (2 weeks): ITransportAdapter interface, ClientConnection.bindTransport, integration test for full round-trip with in-memory transport mock. GameRoom lifecycle (addPlayer, removePlayer). Phase 3 (2 weeks): Event hooks (onTickComplete, onSnapshotDeltaRatio, onClientPredictionError). tsup dual ESM/CJS build. npm publish pipeline. README with quick-start guide and benchmark results.
target_output.development_guidelines: TDD required for all core algorithm modules: DeltaEncoder.compute, DeltaEncoder.decode, ClientConnection rollback re-simulation. TypeScript strict mode; no any types in public API signatures. Pure function contract for consumer stateReducer enforced by Proxy mutation detection in development builds (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development'). Benchmark suite runs in CI; any PR that drops delta compression ratio below 60% or increases bundle size above 30KB gzip is blocked. Public API types in types/index.d.ts are the contract; internal implementation files not exported.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Myers LCS implementation correctness for complex nested array mutations. Mitigation: property-based tests using fast-check generating random state mutations and verifying encode/decode round-trip identity. Risk: MessagePack bundled encoder adds ~3KB to bundle but eliminates an external dependency; acceptable tradeoff given the zero-dep constraint. Tradeoff: per-player delta computation (O(players) encoder calls per tick) vs broadcast delta (one patch for all) chosen for correctness; broadcast delta requires all clients to have identical last-acknowledged state which is not guaranteed with variable network conditions. Tradeoff: in-memory PlayerState buffers limited to 64 ticks by default; games with very high tick rates (60Hz) may need larger buffers; consumer-configurable.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Myers LCS over patience diff for array move op computation due to better worst-case performance for game state array sizes (< 1000 elements). Confirmed MessagePack over JSON.stringify for snapshot serialisation to achieve binary compactness within zero-dep constraint. Chose Vitest over Jest for faster test execution in Node.js 22 ESM environment. Per-player delta confirmed over broadcast delta for correctness with variable network acknowledgement timing.

metadata.notes: Round 2 multiplayer state sync SDK; fixes delta encoding for array reordering with Myers LCS move operations.

profile.domain: C++ Anti-Cheat SDK for PC Game Developers

project_goal.value: C++ anti-cheat SDK for PC game developers that provides tamper detection, memory integrity checks, and encrypted telemetry reporting to a backend verification service, designed to be embedded into game binaries with minimal performance overhead
project_goal.rationale: Anti-cheat is a top player experience priority; partner studios need a drop-in SDK rather than building their own detection stack

target_users.value: Partner PC game development studios who embed the SDK into their game binaries and integrate with the backend verification service
target_users.rationale: B2B partner SDK consumed by game studios integrating into their build pipelines

project_class.rationale: C++ SDK distributed as a static/dynamic library plus a separate Rust-based backend verification service

capabilities.rationale: Rust verification service backend, ClickHouse analytics data, webhook integration with partner game backends

complexity_level.rationale: Memory integrity scanning at game runtime, mTLS telemetry reporting, high-throughput ClickHouse analytics, and ban propagation webhooks at AAA scale

risk_level.rationale: SDK runs in game process with elevated access; HMAC signing keys and violation event data are high-value targets; false positive bans are a severe player trust issue

data_sensitivity.rationale: Hardware fingerprint data contains device identifiers that may be personal data under GDPR in EU jurisdictions; violation records and cheat signature data are competitively sensitive

external_exposure.rationale: Backend verification service called by game clients of partner studios; partner studios receive ban webhooks; not exposed to end users directly

access_model.value: mTLS client certificates for SDK-to-service communication; partner studio API keys for webhook subscription management; no end-user authentication in SDK
access_model.rationale: mTLS ensures only SDK instances with valid certificates can report to the verification service; partner API keys scope webhook delivery

feature_scope.value: In-process memory region integrity scanning, process enumeration for known cheat tools, session key generation and HMAC signing, encrypted telemetry transmission over mTLS, backend violation classification and confidence scoring, ban record creation in ClickHouse, webhook delivery to partner backends, SDK version management and rollout control
feature_scope.rationale: Full anti-cheat pipeline from detection to enforcement

mvp_scope.value: Memory integrity scan, HMAC-signed telemetry reporting over mTLS, violation record creation in ClickHouse, ban record propagation via webhook
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum to provide value to first partner studio integration

security_baseline.value: mTLS with X.509 client certificates for all SDK-to-backend communication; HMAC-SHA256 session signing; telemetry encrypted with AES-256-GCM; certificate rotation without game patch required; no hardcoded keys in SDK binary
security_baseline.rationale: Anti-cheat SDK is a high-value target for reverse engineering; no static secrets in binary is mandatory

frontend_stack.value: No frontend; SDK is a C++20 library distributed as static and dynamic library variants
frontend_stack.rationale: library_sdk with no UI

backend_stack.value: C++ 20 (SDK core) + Rust with Axum (verification service)
backend_stack.rationale: C++ for game binary embedding; Rust for memory-safe high-throughput verification service handling telemetry at scale

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for violation events and ban records (high-write, analytical queries); Redis 7 for ban cache and SDK version rollout state
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse handles millions of integrity_reports inserts per day; Redis provides sub-millisecond ban lookups and version rollout flags

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate for verification service
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with auto-scaling for variable telemetry ingest load

privacy_retention_policy.value: hardware_fingerprints pseudonymised after 30 days (replace device identifiers with a one-way hash); violation_events retained 2 years for ban appeals; ban_records retained indefinitely; GDPR right-to-erasure supported for hardware_fingerprint pseudonymisation only
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR compliance for EU player hardware identifiers; ban records retained for integrity of enforcement history

future_scope.value: Kernel-mode driver extension for OS-level process monitoring, anomaly detection ML model for novel cheat pattern classification, partner dashboard for ban management and appeal tracking
future_scope.rationale: Progressive anti-cheat capability expansion roadmap

constraints.value: SDK scan overhead must not exceed 2ms per scan call; no dynamic memory allocation during scan hot path; partner game backends must receive ban webhook within 30 seconds of violation confirmation
constraints.rationale: Performance budget to avoid impacting game frame rate; 30-second ban propagation SLA for effective enforcement

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on verification service; custom metrics for telemetry ingestion rate, violation classification latency, webhook delivery success rate, and false positive appeal rate
observability_baseline.rationale: False positive rate monitoring is critical to partner trust

execution_preference.value: SDK releases versioned independently from backend service; partner studios opt-in to new SDK versions via rollout flags in Redis before forced upgrade
execution_preference.rationale: Avoid forcing partners to patch their games for every SDK update

compliance_context.value: GDPR: hardware fingerprint data constitutes personal data under GDPR for EU players; pseudonymisation after 30 days, right-to-erasure workflow required. SOC 2 Type II: violation records and ban decisions are security-sensitive; access controls and audit logging required.
compliance_context.rationale: EU player base requires GDPR compliance; partner contracts require SOC 2 attestation

project.goal: C++20 anti-cheat SDK with Rust verification backend for partner game studios
project.target_users: Partner PC game studios
project.timeline: First partner integration in 12 weeks

data.db: ClickHouse for violation events and ban records
data.cache: Redis 7 for ban cache and version rollout

reasoner_reviews.product: The 30-second ban webhook SLA is tight but achievable with async Kafka-like queue between violation confirmation and webhook dispatch. The SDK version rollout via Redis flags is the right pattern for zero-patch partner upgrades; ensure the rollout flag resolution adds negligible latency to the SDK handshake.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Rust Axum is an excellent fit for the verification service: async tokio runtime handles high-concurrency telemetry ingest without the thread-per-connection overhead, and Rust memory safety eliminates an entire class of vulnerabilities in a security-critical service. ClickHouse ReplacingMergeTree for ban_records ensures idempotent re-delivery of ban webhook triggers. The mTLS certificate rotation challenge is the key operational risk: partners need a certificate update mechanism that does not require a game binary patch.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS with partner-specific client certificate namespaces ensures telemetry from one partner cannot be attributed to another. Certificate pinning in the SDK binary is a security vs operability tradeoff: hard-pinning the backend certificate chain prevents MITM but requires an SDK update on certificate rotation; use a trust anchor approach pinning the intermediate CA rather than the leaf certificate. HMAC session key generation must use a hardware entropy source on the player PC, not a PRNG seeded from process ID.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 2ms scan overhead is achievable for memory region integrity checks using pre-allocated scan buffers (no heap allocation in hot path) and CRC32C hardware acceleration available on all modern x86-64 CPUs. Process enumeration is the most expensive operation; run on a background thread with results cached for 5 seconds to stay within budget.

backend.service_design: Rust Axum service with three route groups: /telemetry (POST mTLS-authenticated batch ingestion from SDK clients), /violations (internal: violation classification worker reads from Tokio MPSC channel, writes to ClickHouse), /webhooks (partner-facing: ban notification delivery with retry).
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/telemetry - SDK client sends AES-256-GCM encrypted, HMAC-signed batch of integrity_reports; server decrypts, verifies signature, deserialises, inserts into ClickHouse. POST /v1/webhooks/subscriptions - partner API key registers ban webhook endpoint URL. Internal async pipeline: telemetry ingest -> Tokio channel -> violation classifier -> ClickHouse write -> ban_records table -> Redis ban cache update -> webhook delivery task.
backend.failure_handling: Telemetry ingest: if ClickHouse insert fails, retry with exponential backoff x5 before dropping with Datadog error metric increment. Webhook delivery: retry with exponential backoff for 10 minutes; partner endpoint returning 4xx marks the endpoint as unhealthy for 1 hour; Datadog alert on sustained delivery failure rate > 5%.
backend.scaling_notes: ECS Fargate auto-scaling on CPU 60% target; telemetry ingest service stateless and horizontally scalable. ClickHouse cluster with 3 shards and 2 replicas per shard handles >10M integrity_report inserts per day.
backend.backend_risks: ClickHouse distributed table write amplification under sustained burst; mitigate with buffer tables (Buffer engine) to batch-insert integrity_reports in 5-second windows.

data.entities: integrity_reports (report_id UUID, sdk_version VARCHAR, partner_id UUID, player_fingerprint_hash VARCHAR, scan_type ENUM memory_region/process_enum/module_hash, result ENUM clean/suspicious/violated, confidence_score FLOAT, session_hmac VARCHAR, reported_at DateTime64, raw_scan_data_encrypted VARCHAR). violation_events (violation_id UUID, integrity_report_id UUID, player_fingerprint_hash VARCHAR, cheat_signature_id UUID FK, severity ENUM critical/high/medium, auto_ban BOOL, confirmed_at DateTime64). cheat_signatures (signature_id UUID, name VARCHAR, pattern_hash VARCHAR, auto_ban_threshold FLOAT, active BOOL, created_at DateTime64). ban_records (ban_id UUID, player_fingerprint_hash VARCHAR, partner_id UUID, violation_id UUID FK, ban_type ENUM permanent/temp_7d/temp_30d, issued_at DateTime64, expires_at Nullable(DateTime64), appeal_status ENUM none/pending/resolved). sdk_versions (version_id VARCHAR, release_date Date, rollout_percentage FLOAT, forced BOOL). player_hardware_fingerprints (fingerprint_id UUID, raw_device_hash VARCHAR, partner_id UUID, pseudonymised_at Nullable(DateTime64), collected_at DateTime64)
data.storage_design: ClickHouse: integrity_reports on ReplicatedMergeTree partitioned by toYYYYMM(reported_at); violation_events, ban_records on ReplicatedMergeTree; cheat_signatures on ReplicatedReplacingMergeTree (idempotent signature updates). Redis 7: ban cache keyed by player_fingerprint_hash with 5-minute TTL; sdk_version rollout flags keyed by partner_id and sdk_version.
data.retention_and_deletion: integrity_reports: TTL 90 days (ClickHouse TTL expression on reported_at). player_hardware_fingerprints: pseudonymisation job runs daily; sets pseudonymised_at and replaces raw_device_hash with HMAC-SHA256(device_hash, pseudonymisation_key) for records older than 30 days. GDPR erasure: sets raw_device_hash to NULL for matched records.

security.auth_design: mTLS: each partner studio issued an X.509 client certificate signed by an intermediate CA maintained by the anti-cheat platform. Partner certificates namespaced by partner_id in the Subject CN. SDK bundles the intermediate CA public cert for trust anchor pinning (not leaf cert pinning to allow certificate rotation without SDK update). Certificate rotation: new leaf cert signed by same intermediate CA; SDK trust anchor remains valid.
security.authorization_model: Telemetry endpoint authorises per partner_id extracted from mTLS CN; each batch attributed to that partner. Webhook subscription management requires partner API key (separate from mTLS). Internal violation classification and ban issuance have no external auth surface.
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager: intermediate CA private key, session HMAC signing key, ClickHouse password, Redis auth token. ECS tasks access secrets via IAM task role with Secrets Manager read-only policy. SDK session keys generated at runtime on player PC using hardware entropy (BCryptGenRandom on Windows); no static SDK secret.
security.security_risks: False positive ban from a buggy cheat_signature pattern is the highest partner-trust risk; mitigate with a confidence_score threshold gate (auto_ban only when confidence_score >= cheat_signature.auto_ban_threshold) and a human review queue for borderline cases in the future partner dashboard.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: telemetry-ingest service (4 vCPU, 8GB, 4-20 tasks with CPU auto-scaling), violation-classifier service (2 vCPU, 4GB, 2-8 tasks), webhook-dispatcher service (1 vCPU, 2GB, 2-6 tasks). ALB with mTLS passthrough to telemetry-ingest. ClickHouse cluster on EC2 (c6i.4xlarge x 6, 3 shards x 2 replicas). Redis Elasticache cluster mode.
devops.ci_cd_design: CircleCI: Rust cargo test and cargo clippy, C++ SDK unit tests with CTest (scan latency benchmarks asserting < 2ms), docker build Rust service image, push to ECR, ECS rolling deploy. SDK build: CMake for C++20 static and dynamic library artifacts; SDK release artifacts published to partner-facing S3 bucket with version prefix.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS rolling deploy with health check gate; automatic rollback on failed health checks. ClickHouse schema migrations via Flyway-compatible migration runner with forward-only constraint.
devops.devops_risks: ClickHouse EC2 node failure during high-ingest period; mitigate with 2-replica MergeTree replication; ZooKeeper ensemble (3 nodes) for ClickHouse coordination.

ISS-001.title: SDK certificate rotation requires game binary patch
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan used leaf certificate pinning in the SDK binary, meaning any certificate rotation requires partners to re-embed the SDK and patch their game binary. This creates a window where games with outdated certificates cannot report telemetry, blinding the system to ongoing cheating.

history.note: Raised in security review: leaf cert pinning is operationally incompatible with routine certificate rotation

ISS-002.title: GDPR pseudonymisation job not specified
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan stated hardware fingerprints are pseudonymised after 30 days but did not specify the job mechanism, schedule, or how GDPR right-to-erasure requests are handled in ClickHouse.

history.note: Compliance reviewer raised: GDPR mechanism must be explicit given confidential data sensitivity

focus_issues.title: SDK certificate rotation requires game binary patch
focus_issues.detail: Leaf cert pinning must be replaced with intermediate CA trust anchor pinning to enable certificate rotation without SDK update.

focus_issues.title: GDPR pseudonymisation job not specified
focus_issues.detail: Pseudonymisation schedule, mechanism, and GDPR erasure workflow for ClickHouse must be explicitly defined.

latest_recommendations: Replace leaf certificate pinning with intermediate CA trust anchor pinning in SDK; document certificate rotation runbook
Define a daily AWS Lambda or ECS scheduled task that pseudonymises hardware fingerprints older than 30 days in ClickHouse using an ALTER TABLE UPDATE mutation
