target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for a C++ anti-cheat SDK with Rust verification backend. Two issues to fix: ISS-001 (leaf cert pinning must become intermediate CA trust anchor pinning to enable rotation without game patch) and ISS-002 (GDPR pseudonymisation job needs explicit daily ClickHouse ALTER TABLE UPDATE mechanism and erasure API). ISS-001 changes the security section significantly; ISS-002 adds a scheduled task to deployment_and_operations and a GDPR erasure endpoint to the API. The core architecture is strong and unchanged.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced leaf certificate pinning with intermediate CA trust anchor pinning: SDK bundles the intermediate CA public certificate (not the backend leaf certificate). Leaf certificates are issued with 90-day validity by the stable intermediate CA. On rotation, a new leaf cert is deployed to ECS Fargate without any SDK update. Certificate rotation runbook documented: (1) generate new leaf cert from intermediate CA, (2) update Secrets Manager, (3) ECS rolling deploy, (4) old leaf expires in 90 days. Partner studios are not impacted and no game binary patch is required.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Certificate rotation performed every 90 days without requiring partner game binary patches; SDK trust anchor remains valid for intermediate CA lifetime

fix_report.action_taken: Added a daily ECS scheduled task (pseudonymisation_job) that runs an ALTER TABLE UPDATE mutation on player_hardware_fingerprints in ClickHouse replacing raw_device_hash with HMAC-SHA256(raw_device_hash, pseudonymisation_key) for all rows where collected_at < NOW() - INTERVAL 30 DAY AND pseudonymised_at IS NULL. Added POST /v1/gdpr/erasure endpoint (requires partner API key) that executes a synchronous ClickHouse mutation setting raw_device_hash = NULL for a specific fingerprint_id; returns 200 on completion or 404 if not found.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Hardware fingerprint data pseudonymised within 24 hours of the 30-day threshold; GDPR right-to-erasure requests fulfilled synchronously via the erasure API endpoint

target_output.title: PC Anti-Cheat SDK and Verification Service
target_output.executive_summary: A C++20 SDK for embedding tamper detection and memory integrity scanning directly into PC game binaries, paired with a Rust Axum verification service on AWS ECS Fargate. The SDK scans memory regions, enumerates processes against known cheat tool signatures, and reports HMAC-signed, AES-256-GCM encrypted telemetry to the verification service over mTLS. The backend classifies violations, writes records to ClickHouse, updates the Redis ban cache, and propagates confirmed bans to partner game backends via webhook within 30 seconds. SDK certificates use intermediate CA trust anchor pinning, enabling rotation without game binary patches. GDPR pseudonymisation of hardware fingerprints runs daily via scheduled ClickHouse mutation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-component system: (1) the C++20 SDK embedded in partner game binaries, and (2) the Rust Axum verification service on ECS Fargate. SDK execution flow: at game launch and at randomised 30-120 second intervals, the SDK's scan engine reads pre-allocated memory region descriptors, computes CRC32C checksums (hardware-accelerated via _mm_crc32_u64), enumerates running processes from cached kernel list, generates a session key using BCryptGenRandom, signs the scan result batch with HMAC-SHA256, encrypts with AES-256-GCM, and transmits to the verification service via a persistent mTLS connection. The SDK pins the intermediate CA certificate (not the leaf) enabling transparent leaf cert rotation. The verification service decrypts, verifies signatures, deserialises integrity_reports, and pushes to a Tokio MPSC channel for async processing. The violation classifier worker scores each report against cheat_signature patterns in Redis, writes matches to ClickHouse violation_events, creates ban_records for auto-ban-eligible violations, and dispatches webhook delivery tasks for ban propagation to partner game backends within the 30-second SLA.
target_output.technology_stack: SDK: C++20 with CMake; CRC32C via SSE4.2 intrinsics; BCryptGenRandom (Windows Crypto API); WinHTTP for mTLS connections; no external dependencies. Verification service: Rust 1.77 with Axum, Tokio, rustls (for mTLS), clickhouse-rs (async ClickHouse client), redis-rs. Database: ClickHouse 24.x cluster (ReplicatedMergeTree, Buffer engine for ingest) on EC2 c6i.4xlarge. Cache: Redis 7 Elasticache cluster mode. Hosting: ECS Fargate with ALB (mTLS passthrough on telemetry service). CI/CD: CircleCI. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager. Observability: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Memory Region Scan: SDK scans pre-registered memory regions of game binary (code sections, heap headers) using CRC32C checksums against known-good hashes stored in an encrypted manifest bundled with the SDK. Process Enumeration: background thread caches running process list every 5 seconds; scans against cheat_signatures process name patterns. Session Key and HMAC Signing: per-session key from BCryptGenRandom; HMAC-SHA256 signs the report batch to bind it to the originating session. mTLS Telemetry Transmission: persistent WinHTTP connection with intermediate CA trust anchor; batch encrypted with AES-256-GCM. Violation Classification: Rust worker scores integrity_reports against cheat_signature patterns; auto-ban gate on confidence_score >= auto_ban_threshold. Ban Record and Cache: ClickHouse ban_records written; Redis ban cache updated with 5-minute TTL for fast game server lookups. Webhook Dispatch: partner game backend notified within 30 seconds of ban_record creation. SDK Version Rollout: Redis rollout flags per partner_id and sdk_version control gradual forced-upgrade rollout. GDPR Pseudonymisation: daily scheduled mutation pseudonymises hardware fingerprints older than 30 days.
target_output.system_components: C++20 SDK (static/dynamic library variants, CMake build), WinHTTP mTLS transport layer, BCryptGenRandom session key generator, CRC32C scan engine (SSE4.2), Rust Axum verification service (telemetry-ingest, violation-classifier, webhook-dispatcher ECS tasks), ALB with mTLS passthrough, ClickHouse cluster (3 shards x 2 replicas on EC2), ZooKeeper ensemble (3 nodes, ClickHouse coordination), Redis 7 Elasticache (ban cache + version rollout flags), AWS Secrets Manager (intermediate CA, leaf cert, HMAC signing key, ClickHouse credentials), ECS scheduled task (daily pseudonymisation_job), Datadog agent sidecar on all ECS tasks.
target_output.workflows: Integrity scan and triage workflow: SDK scan engine wakes at a randomised interval (configurable 30-120s base, +-20% jitter to prevent synchronised bursts). Scan reads pre-allocated region descriptor list, computes CRC32C for each region, compares against encrypted manifest hashes, enumerates process cache against cheat_signature name patterns. Results assembled into a report batch, signed with HMAC-SHA256(session_key, batch_json), encrypted with AES-256-GCM(telemetry_aes_key, batch). Sent via persistent WinHTTP mTLS connection to POST /v1/telemetry. Verification service decrypts and verifies; writes to ClickHouse integrity_reports via Buffer engine; pushes to Tokio MPSC channel. Violation classifier reads channel, scores against cheat_signatures in Redis, creates violation_events and ban_records in ClickHouse for confirmed violations, updates Redis ban cache. Webhook dispatcher polls ban_records created in last 30 seconds and POST-delivers to partner webhook URL with exponential backoff retry. Ban propagation workflow: partner game server queries own ban cache or calls GET /v1/bans/:fingerprint_hash endpoint (Redis-backed, <5ms response) to check player ban status before match start. GDPR pseudonymisation: daily ECS scheduled task runs ALTER TABLE UPDATE player_hardware_fingerprints SET raw_device_hash = HMAC-SHA256(raw_device_hash, :key), pseudonymised_at = now() WHERE collected_at < now() - INTERVAL 30 DAY AND pseudonymised_at IS NULL.
target_output.data_model: integrity_reports (report_id UUID, sdk_version VARCHAR, partner_id UUID, player_fingerprint_hash VARCHAR, scan_type ENUM memory_region/process_enum/module_hash, result ENUM clean/suspicious/violated, confidence_score FLOAT32, session_hmac VARCHAR, reported_at DateTime64, ENGINE ReplicatedMergeTree partitioned by toYYYYMM(reported_at), TTL reported_at + INTERVAL 90 DAY). violation_events (violation_id UUID, integrity_report_id UUID, player_fingerprint_hash VARCHAR, cheat_signature_id UUID, severity ENUM critical/high/medium, auto_ban BOOL, confirmed_at DateTime64). cheat_signatures (signature_id UUID, name VARCHAR, pattern_hash VARCHAR, auto_ban_threshold FLOAT32, active BOOL, created_at DateTime64, ENGINE ReplicatedReplacingMergeTree). ban_records (ban_id UUID, player_fingerprint_hash VARCHAR, partner_id UUID, violation_id UUID, ban_type ENUM permanent/temp_7d/temp_30d, issued_at DateTime64, expires_at Nullable(DateTime64), appeal_status ENUM none/pending/resolved, ENGINE ReplicatedMergeTree). player_hardware_fingerprints (fingerprint_id UUID, raw_device_hash VARCHAR NULLABLE, partner_id UUID, pseudonymised_at Nullable(DateTime64), collected_at DateTime64, ENGINE ReplicatedMergeTree). sdk_versions (version_id VARCHAR, release_date Date, rollout_percentage FLOAT32, forced BOOL). Redis: ban_cache KEY=fingerprint_hash VALUE=ban_json TTL=300s; version_rollout KEY=partner_id:sdk_version VALUE=rollout_pct.
target_output.api_design: Rust Axum REST API. POST /v1/telemetry (mTLS required; Content-Type: application/octet-stream; AES-GCM encrypted batch body; returns 202 Accepted). GET /v1/bans/:fingerprint_hash (partner API key required; returns ban status from Redis cache, fallback ClickHouse; <5ms p99 target). POST /v1/webhooks/subscriptions (partner API key; registers ban notification URL for partner_id). GET /v1/sdk-version/rollout (mTLS required; returns rollout_percentage for calling partner and requested sdk_version from Redis). POST /v1/gdpr/erasure (partner API key; body: {fingerprint_id: UUID}; executes synchronous ClickHouse mutation setting raw_device_hash = NULL; returns 200 on success, 404 if not found). All endpoints return application/json errors. mTLS: partner_id extracted from X.509 client certificate Subject CN and set in Axum request extensions.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS with intermediate CA trust anchor pinning: SDK bundles the intermediate CA public certificate; backend leaf certificates are issued with 90-day validity. Leaf rotation does not require SDK update; only a new ECS deployment with updated Secrets Manager leaf cert. Certificate rotation runbook: generate new leaf cert signed by intermediate CA, store in Secrets Manager, ECS rolling deploy (old leaf still valid during rollout, expires 90 days later). HMAC-SHA256 session signing with per-session key from BCryptGenRandom (hardware entropy); no static signing key in SDK binary. AES-256-GCM telemetry encryption using key from Secrets Manager loaded at SDK initialisation via mTLS-secured key exchange handshake. Cheat signature auto-ban gate: confidence_score must exceed per-signature auto_ban_threshold to prevent false positive bans from partial pattern matches. GDPR compliance: daily pseudonymisation job replaces raw_device_hash with HMAC-SHA256 hash for records older than 30 days; erasure API sets raw_device_hash to NULL for individual right-to-erasure requests. SOC 2: all violation classification decisions logged immutably to ClickHouse with original confidence_score for audit trail; Secrets Manager access logged via CloudTrail; Datadog access log aggregation.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate services: telemetry-ingest (4 vCPU 8GB, 4-20 tasks, CPU auto-scale 60%), violation-classifier (2 vCPU 4GB, 2-8 tasks), webhook-dispatcher (1 vCPU 2GB, 2-6 tasks). ALB with TCP listener on 443 for mTLS passthrough to telemetry-ingest; NLB for internal service-to-service. ECS scheduled task: pseudonymisation_job (daily at 02:00 UTC, 1 vCPU 2GB single-task). CircleCI pipeline: Rust cargo test + clippy, C++ CTest with scan latency benchmarks asserting <2ms, docker build + ECR push + ECS rolling deploy. C++ SDK release: CMake build produces static (.lib) and dynamic (.dll) artifacts; signed with SHA-256 code signing cert; uploaded to partner S3 bucket under /sdk/v{VERSION}/. Certificate rotation runbook stored in ops runbook wiki; rotations scheduled every 85 days (before 90-day expiry). ClickHouse schema migrations: forward-only numbered migration scripts run by migration-runner ECS task before each service deploy.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM on all ECS tasks with dd-trace for Rust. Custom Datadog metrics: telemetry_ingest_rate (batches/sec per partner_id), violation_classification_latency_p99 (target <500ms), webhook_delivery_success_rate (target >99.5%), ban_cache_hit_rate (target >95%), false_positive_appeal_rate (bans appealed and reversed / total bans; alert if >2% over 7-day rolling window). PagerDuty alerts on: webhook delivery success rate <95% over 5 minutes, violation classification worker queue depth >10K, ClickHouse insert latency p99 >2s, false positive appeal rate >2%. Datadog Log Management aggregates all ECS task logs with partner_id tag for per-partner debugging.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate: ~$800-1200/month baseline (4-20 telemetry-ingest tasks). ClickHouse EC2 (6x c6i.4xlarge): ~$2400/month; Reserved Instance purchase for baseline reduces to ~$1600/month. Redis Elasticache: ~$200/month. AWS Secrets Manager, CloudTrail, ALB: ~$150/month. Total estimated: $2200-2800/month. Scaling ceiling: 50M integrity_report inserts/day achievable with ClickHouse Buffer engine batching at current cluster size.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (6 weeks): C++20 SDK core: memory region scan engine with CRC32C, BCryptGenRandom session key, HMAC signing, AES-GCM encryption, WinHTTP mTLS transport with intermediate CA trust anchor pinning. Rust verification service: telemetry ingest endpoint, signature verification, ClickHouse write. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Violation classifier with cheat_signatures matching. ban_records creation. Redis ban cache. GET /v1/bans endpoint. Webhook delivery with retry. 30-second SLA validation. Phase 3 (2 weeks): Process enumeration scan type. SDK version rollout via Redis flags. Daily pseudonymisation_job ECS scheduled task. GDPR erasure API endpoint. Datadog metrics instrumentation.
target_output.development_guidelines: C++ SDK: no heap allocation in scan hot path; all scan buffers pre-allocated in SdkContext constructor; valgrind and AddressSanitizer clean required in CI. CTest benchmarks assert <2ms scan call latency on reference test machine (i7-12700, Release build). Rust service: cargo clippy --deny warnings; all async functions use structured concurrency (no detached spawns). ClickHouse mutations (ALTER TABLE UPDATE) are asynchronous in ClickHouse; pseudonymisation_job must poll mutations table for completion before reporting success. Partner-facing API changes require a semantic version bump to the API route prefix (/v2/...).
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: false positive bans from cheat_signature pattern collision with legitimate game code. Mitigation: per-signature auto_ban_threshold configured conservatively at launch (default 0.95); QA process requires pattern collision testing against clean game builds before signature activation. Risk: ClickHouse ALTER TABLE UPDATE mutation for GDPR erasure is eventually consistent; raw_device_hash may remain readable for seconds to minutes after erasure request. Mitigation: erasure API polls mutations table for completion (max 30s) and returns 200 only when confirmed; document residual latency to partners. Tradeoff: intermediate CA trust anchor pinning over full CA chain pinning accepts the risk that a compromised intermediate CA enables MITM; mitigate by storing intermediate CA private key in AWS CloudHSM. Tradeoff: in-process SDK scanning vs kernel-mode driver chosen for MVP to reduce SDK integration complexity for partners; kernel driver required for stronger tamper resistance is in future_scope.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected intermediate CA trust anchor pinning over leaf pinning to decouple certificate rotation from SDK release cycle. Confirmed ClickHouse ALTER TABLE UPDATE for GDPR pseudonymisation over application-layer update due to ClickHouse immutable MergeTree semantics. Chose Redis ban cache over direct ClickHouse query for GET /v1/bans to meet <5ms p99 latency. Buffer engine for integrity_reports ingest confirmed to absorb burst writes from multiple partner game launches simultaneously.

metadata.notes: Round 2 C++ anti-cheat SDK; resolves leaf cert pinning with intermediate CA trust anchor and adds explicit GDPR pseudonymisation mechanism.

project_goal.value: Pre-launch marketing landing page for an upcoming indie roguelike featuring animated trailers, Steam wishlist capture, dev blog teasers, and a countdown to early access release
project_goal.rationale: Core conversion goal is maximizing Steam wishlist signups before release day

target_users.value: PC gamers, roguelike enthusiasts, and gaming press browsing the studio website before early access launch
target_users.rationale: Fan audience discovered via social links, gaming media, and Steam store page referrals

project_class.rationale: Single-purpose marketing page with no server-side runtime or database; static generation is appropriate

capabilities.rationale: Client-side UI with analytics tracking and Steam partner API wishlist integration

complexity_level.rationale: Static page with lightweight JS interactions; no auth, no database, no backend runtime

risk_level.rationale: No user data stored server-side; email capture forwarded to Steam partner API only

data_sensitivity.rationale: No PII stored; email addresses passed directly to Steam and not persisted by this app

external_exposure.rationale: Public marketing page accessible to all visitors without authentication

access_model.value: Public read-only; no authentication required for any page content
access_model.rationale: Marketing landing page must have zero friction for visitors

feature_scope.value: Hero section with game trailer embed, animated screenshots, Steam wishlist CTA, developer blog teaser cards, social media links, countdown timer to early access date
feature_scope.rationale: All conversion-driving content defined by marketing brief

mvp_scope.value: Countdown timer, trailer embed, Steam wishlist button, and one developer blog teaser card sufficient for press kit release
mvp_scope.rationale: Press embargo lifts in 3 weeks; full feature set ships day-of launch

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Vercel, CSP headers blocking inline scripts, Steam API key in Vercel environment variables only and never in client bundle
security_baseline.rationale: Static site threat model; primary risk is XSS via third-party embeds

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Framer Motion for animations
frontend_stack.rationale: Team familiar with Next.js; static export mode used for hosting on Vercel Edge Network

backend_stack.value: Vercel Edge Functions (TypeScript) for Steam wishlist proxy only; no dedicated backend server
backend_stack.rationale: Avoids exposing Steam API key client-side; single lightweight function handles form POST

data_platform.value: No database. Vercel Analytics for page metrics. Steam partner dashboard for wishlist counts.
data_platform.rationale: Data lives in Steam and Vercel Analytics; no custom persistence needed

hosting_target.value: Vercel Edge Network with global CDN; custom domain via Vercel DNS
hosting_target.rationale: Zero-config deployment, automatic HTTPS, edge caching for game asset images

privacy_retention_policy.value: No user data retained by this application; emails passed to Steam API only. Cookie consent banner for Vercel Analytics tracking.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR-lite compliance through data minimization; no server-side storage

future_scope.value: Post-launch: link to Steam store page, patch notes feed, community Discord invite, optional newsletter via Mailchimp integration
future_scope.rationale: Post-release content strategy extends the page lifetime

constraints.value: Launch deadline fixed at press embargo lift date; page must load under 2 seconds on 4G mobile; trailer video hosted on YouTube to avoid egress costs
constraints.rationale: Marketing SLA and budget constraints

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for Core Web Vitals and page views; no custom error monitoring beyond Vercel build logs
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight monitoring appropriate for static marketing page

execution_preference.value: Ship in 2-week sprint; one designer, one frontend engineer, automated deploys on push to main
execution_preference.rationale: Small team and deadline-driven delivery

project.goal: Maximize Steam wishlist signups before roguelike early access launch
project.target_users: PC gamers and gaming press
project.timeline: 2-week sprint, fixed press embargo deadline

frontend.state: React useState for countdown timer and form submission state

backend.api: Single /api/wishlist Edge Function proxying Steam partner wishlist API

security.auth: None required
security.secrets: Steam API key in Vercel environment variables

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for lint and type-check on PR

constraints.video: YouTube embed to avoid egress costs

reasoner_reviews.product: The core conversion flow from page visit to Steam wishlist click must be friction-free. The countdown timer creates urgency but needs a graceful fallback near the launch timestamp for timezone edge cases. Blog teaser cards can tease world-building and developer personality without spoiling gameplay mechanics.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js 14 in static export mode fits perfectly for this use case. The Steam wishlist proxy Edge Function sidesteps API key exposure risk without requiring a full backend server. Framer Motion adds engagement but must be code-split to avoid blocking LCP on the hero section.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP headers are the primary defence against XSS from YouTube iframe injection. The Steam API key must live only in Vercel environment variables, never in the built JS bundle. No cookies beyond analytics consent need to be set, minimizing GDPR surface area.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 2-second LCP on 4G requires lazy-loading the YouTube iframe behind a click-to-play poster image and serving game screenshots via next/image with automatic WebP conversion. The fixed press deadline means the MVP scope cannot expand.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js app router with a single root page.tsx; sections rendered as server components where possible, countdown timer and wishlist form as client components with 'use client' directive
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single-page layout: Hero (trailer poster + Steam CTA), Features (animated screenshot grid), Countdown (live timer component), DevBlog (3 MDX teaser cards), Social/Footer with platform links
frontend.performance_notes: YouTube iframe loaded behind IntersectionObserver trigger to prevent eager load; next/image serves WebP screenshots at 3 responsive breakpoints (640, 1024, 1920); Framer Motion imported via dynamic() with ssr:false to split from initial bundle
frontend.frontend_risks: Framer Motion bundle weight could inflate Time to Interactive if not deferred; YouTube embed blocked by strict ad blockers unless fallback trailer poster renders correctly

security.auth_design: No authentication; page is fully public with no protected routes
security.secrets_management: Steam API key stored as Vercel environment variable STEAM_PARTNER_KEY, never referenced in any client-side code path; Edge Function reads process.env server-side only
security.abuse_prevention: Edge Function rate-limited to 20 wishlist submissions per IP per hour via Vercel KV token bucket; honeypot hidden field in wishlist form rejects submissions where field is non-empty

data.entities: No persistent entities. Transient: wishlist_submission payload {email: string, honeypot: string, submitted_at: ISO8601} validated by Edge Function before forwarding to Steam API. analytics_event dimensions {page, event_name, country, device_type} aggregated by Vercel Analytics.
data.analytics_events: cta_wishlist_click, wishlist_form_success, trailer_play_initiated, countdown_reached_zero, blog_teaser_click tracked via Vercel Analytics Web API
data.retention_and_deletion: No user data retained server-side; Vercel Analytics stores only aggregate metrics without individual user identifiers

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub main branch merge triggers Vercel build (next build) and atomic edge CDN deployment. Pull request branches receive isolated Vercel preview URLs for designer review.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow on PR: runs eslint --max-warnings 0 and tsc --noEmit in parallel; both must pass before merge allowed. Vercel CLI used for deployment, not GitHub Actions, to leverage Vercel's build cache.
devops.rollback_strategy: Vercel instant rollback to any prior deployment via dashboard or CLI; atomic swap means rollback completes in under 30 seconds with zero traffic interruption

target_output.thinking_summary: Static marketing page for an indie roguelike where the key architectural decision is a single Vercel Edge Function proxying the Steam partner wishlist API without exposing the key client-side. No database or auth layer needed. Framer Motion animations must be deferred via dynamic import to protect LCP. YouTube click-to-play poster satisfies the 2-second mobile load requirement while avoiding self-hosted video egress costs.
target_output.title: Indie Roguelike Pre-Launch Marketing Page
target_output.executive_summary: A statically generated Next.js 14 landing page deployed to Vercel Edge Network designed to convert gaming press and PC gamers into Steam wishlists before early access launch. The page features a click-to-play YouTube trailer, animated screenshot carousel, live countdown timer, developer blog teasers sourced from MDX files, and a wishlist form proxied through a Vercel Edge Function to protect the Steam partner API key. No backend server or database is required.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-tier static architecture: Next.js 14 app router generates a fully static site at build time and deploys to Vercel Edge Network's global CDN. A single Vercel Edge Function at /api/wishlist acts as a server-side proxy for the Steam partner wishlist API, keeping the API key out of the client bundle. All game screenshots are served via next/image with automatic WebP conversion and edge caching. Developer blog posts are stored as MDX files in the repository and compiled into static HTML at build time. Vercel Analytics provides Core Web Vitals monitoring and custom conversion event tracking without a dedicated analytics backend.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 (app router, static export), TypeScript 5, Tailwind CSS 3, Framer Motion 11 (loaded via dynamic import to split from initial bundle). Backend proxy: Vercel Edge Functions (TypeScript), Vercel KV for rate limit token storage. Analytics: Vercel Analytics with custom Web API event tracking. Content: MDX 3 for developer blog posts compiled at build time. CI/CD: GitHub Actions (eslint + tsc --noEmit on PR) plus Vercel automatic preview and production deploys.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero Section: full-viewport game key art with click-to-play YouTube trailer poster, Steam wishlist CTA button, tagline and genre badges. Countdown Timer: server-rendered initial value with client hydration for live tick, transitions to 'Out Now' release-day banner when launch timestamp elapses. Screenshot Carousel: WebP game screenshots with Framer Motion slide animations gated behind prefers-reduced-motion media query. DevBlog Teasers: last 3 MDX posts as cards with title, date, excerpt, and read-more link. Social Footer: Steam store, Discord invite, Twitter, and TikTok links.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge Network (CDN for all static assets and HTML pages), Next.js static export build output (HTML, CSS, JS chunks split by dynamic import boundaries), Vercel Edge Function /api/wishlist (Steam API proxy with Vercel KV rate limiting), YouTube embed (click-to-play poster image with iframe deferred until explicit user interaction), Vercel Analytics (Core Web Vitals tracking and custom conversion events), GitHub repository (source of truth for MDX blog posts and optimized game asset images).
target_output.workflows: Wishlist Capture: visitor clicks 'Add to Wishlist', client component POSTs {email} to /api/wishlist Edge Function, Edge Function reads Vercel KV to check rate limit bucket (20/hr/IP), calls Steam partner wishlist API with STEAM_PARTNER_KEY env var, returns {success:true} or {error:'rate_limited'|'steam_error'}, client renders confirmation toast or inline error. Countdown and Reveal: CountdownTimer client component reads NEXT_PUBLIC_LAUNCH_TIMESTAMP env var at hydration time, runs setInterval to recalculate days/hours/minutes/seconds on each tick, when timestamp passes component replaces countdown display with 'Out Now - Play on Steam' CTA linking to store page, no redeployment required.
target_output.data_model: No persistent database entities. Transient Edge Function request body: wishlist_submission {email: string (validated format), honeypot: string (must equal empty string), submitted_at: ISO8601 string} validated before Steam API call and not stored. MDX frontmatter schema for blog posts: {title: string, date: ISO8601, excerpt: string, cover_image: string, tags: string[]} enforced by gray-matter validation at build time. Vercel Analytics aggregate event schema: {event: string, url: string, country: string, device: string}.
target_output.api_design: POST /api/wishlist: body {email: string}, validates honeypot field empty, checks Vercel KV rate limit for caller IP, proxies to Steam partner wishlist endpoint https://store.steampowered.com/api/addtowishlist with partner credentials, returns {success:true} on 200 or {error:'rate_limited'} on 429 or {error:'steam_error', code: number} on upstream failure. No other server-side routes; all remaining pages are statically generated HTML served directly from CDN.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CSP header in next.config.js headers() array: default-src 'self', script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval' (Next.js requirement), frame-src youtube-nocookie.com, img-src 'self' data: i.ytimg.com. Steam API key stored exclusively as Vercel environment variable STEAM_PARTNER_KEY; no NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix ensures it is never serialized into the client bundle. Honeypot field rejects bot submissions silently. Rate limit 20 wishlist submissions per IP per hour via Vercel KV token bucket. Cookie consent banner loads conditionally before Vercel Analytics script injection, satisfying GDPR data minimization. HTTPS enforced by Vercel with automatic TLS certificate renewal via Let's Encrypt.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub main branch push triggers Vercel production deployment: next build generates static export, Vercel uploads to global edge CDN with atomic deployment swap ensuring zero downtime during release. Pull request branches receive isolated Vercel preview URLs automatically, used for designer review before merge. GitHub Actions CI pipeline runs eslint --max-warnings 0 and tsc --noEmit in parallel jobs on every PR; merge blocked until both pass. Environment variables STEAM_PARTNER_KEY and NEXT_PUBLIC_LAUNCH_TIMESTAMP are configured in Vercel project settings dashboard, never committed to the repository. Rollback to any prior deployment available in under 30 seconds via Vercel dashboard or vercel rollback CLI command.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics dashboard tracks Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, INP) per page view segmented by device type and country. Custom conversion events tracked via Vercel Web Analytics API: cta_wishlist_click, wishlist_form_success, trailer_play_initiated, countdown_reached_zero, blog_teaser_click. Vercel build logs capture Edge Function errors and deploy failures. No additional monitoring infrastructure warranted for a static marketing page.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Projected cost: $0/month on Vercel Hobby tier for MVP development; $20/month Pro for analytics data export and custom domain. YouTube hosting eliminates all video egress costs. Vercel KV free tier (256MB) sufficient for rate limit token storage at expected wishlist submission volumes. Traffic spikes from gaming press coverage absorbed by Vercel Edge Network globally without manual intervention.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (Week 1): Hero section with YouTube click-to-play poster and Steam wishlist CTA, live countdown timer component, Vercel Edge Function wishlist proxy with rate limiting, Tailwind responsive layout for mobile and desktop, custom domain configured, Vercel Analytics installed and conversion events wired. Phase 2 Full Launch (Week 2): Animated screenshot carousel with Framer Motion and prefers-reduced-motion guard, developer blog MDX integration with 3 teaser cards, social footer links, CSP headers tuned, cookie consent banner, mobile performance pass targeting LCP under 2 seconds on simulated 4G.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode enabled in tsconfig.json. ESLint with Next.js recommended ruleset; zero-warnings policy enforced in CI. Framer Motion variants defined in src/animations/ config files rather than inline JSX props. MDX frontmatter validated by gray-matter in prebuild script; missing required fields fail the build with a clear error. next/image required for all raster images; direct img elements are ESLint-forbidden. Environment variables with NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix used only for values explicitly safe to expose in the browser bundle.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: YouTube iframe blocked by aggressive ad blockers, resulting in missing trailer on a visitor's first impression. Mitigation: click-to-play poster design displays game key art regardless; YouTube iframe loads only on explicit click, so LCP and initial layout are unaffected. Risk: Steam partner wishlist API downtime on launch day during press surge. Mitigation: Edge Function returns graceful error with direct Steam store URL fallback shown to visitor. Tradeoff: Next.js static export over plain HTML to enable MDX blog support and next/image optimization, accepting slightly larger initial JS bundle than a zero-JS static site.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed YouTube over self-hosted video to eliminate Vercel bandwidth costs. Ruled out Mailchimp newsletter in MVP to avoid additional third-party cookie consent scope. Selected Vercel KV over external Redis for rate limiting to remain within Vercel ecosystem on free tier. Countdown timer configured via NEXT_PUBLIC_LAUNCH_TIMESTAMP environment variable enabling launch date adjustments without code changes or redeployment.

metadata.notes: Static roguelike marketing page with Steam wishlist proxy Edge Function and click-to-play YouTube trailer for LCP compliance.

project_goal.value: Community wiki and lore compendium for a fantasy MMORPG covering world history, faction lore, item databases, and quest guides, maintained collaboratively by players and moderated by guild leaders using a Git-based content authoring workflow
project_goal.rationale: Replaces fragmented fan wikis with a single authoritative Git-managed source of truth

target_users.value: MMORPG players seeking lore references, quest walk-throughs, and item data; guild leaders who author and moderate content via pull requests
target_users.rationale: Two distinct user groups: readers (majority) and contributing authors (small moderated group)

project_class.rationale: All content is read-only for visitors; no server-side runtime, database, or auth required

capabilities.rationale: Static content delivery with analytics to track popular lore sections and search queries

complexity_level.rationale: Large content volume and multiple content types (lore, items, quests) require structured taxonomy but no backend

risk_level.rationale: Public read-only content; no user accounts, no PII, no financial transactions

data_sensitivity.rationale: All content is publicly visible fan-created lore; no personal or sensitive data handled

external_exposure.rationale: Wiki must be publicly indexable by search engines for discoverability

access_model.value: Fully public read access for all visitors; content authoring via GitHub pull requests requiring moderator approval
access_model.rationale: Authoring access controlled by GitHub repository permissions, not by the wiki site itself

feature_scope.value: Lore articles with rich text and faction relationship maps, item database pages with stat tables, quest guide pages with step-by-step sections, world region pages, NPC profile pages, patch changelog archive, full-text search, breadcrumb navigation
feature_scope.rationale: Complete set of content types needed for a usable MMORPG reference wiki

mvp_scope.value: 50 lore articles, item database for 200 starter-zone items, 30 quest guides, world regions overview, and basic full-text search via Pagefind
mvp_scope.rationale: Enough content to validate authoring workflow before wider community contribution opens

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Cloudflare, CSP headers blocking external script injection, no cookies set, content integrity via GitHub branch protection rules and CI validation
security_baseline.rationale: Static site; threat surface is primarily content integrity and CDN abuse

frontend_stack.value: Hugo with custom Go template theme; Tailwind CSS via PostCSS pipeline
frontend_stack.rationale: Hugo handles large content volume efficiently with sub-second build times and native taxonomy support

backend_stack.value: No backend server; GitHub Actions CI/CD and a scheduled item data fetch script (Python 3.12) for game API integration
backend_stack.rationale: All logic runs at build time; no runtime server needed

data_platform.value: No database; content stored as Markdown files in Git repository. Item data fetched from game public API and committed as YAML data files. Pagefind search index built at deploy time.
data_platform.rationale: Git-as-CMS pattern with build-time search index generation

hosting_target.value: Cloudflare Pages with global CDN; custom domain via Cloudflare DNS
hosting_target.rationale: Free tier supports unlimited bandwidth, automatic HTTPS, and 500 builds per month

privacy_retention_policy.value: No user data collected or retained; Cloudflare Web Analytics used without cookies or fingerprinting; no consent banner required
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Privacy-by-default analytics avoids GDPR consent requirement for EU visitors

future_scope.value: Interactive faction relationship map (D3.js), item build calculator, in-game event calendar, contributor leaderboard showing top authors by merged PRs
future_scope.rationale: Community engagement features planned for post-MVP phases

constraints.value: Hugo build time must remain under 60 seconds for 5000 pages; no JavaScript frameworks in the critical render path; all images served from Cloudflare Images CDN
constraints.rationale: Build speed constraint ensures contributor feedback loop stays fast; JS constraint protects Core Web Vitals

observability_baseline.value: Cloudflare Web Analytics for page views and popular content; Cloudflare build log monitoring; no application-level error tracking needed
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight analytics sufficient for a public content site

execution_preference.value: Content-first development: Hugo theme and CI pipeline in sprint 1, then monthly content sprints driven by community contributors
execution_preference.rationale: Theme and pipeline are the platform; content velocity scales with community

project.goal: Git-managed MMORPG community wiki with lore, items, and quest guides
project.target_users: Players and contributing guild leaders
project.timeline: Hugo theme and CI pipeline in 4 weeks; content MVP in 8 weeks

frontend.framework: Hugo with custom Go template theme
frontend.state: Vanilla JS for search UI and collapsible sections only

backend.runtime: No runtime server; Python 3.12 script for item data fetch (runs in CI)
backend.api: Game public item API consumed at build time via Python fetch script

security.auth: None for readers; GitHub repo permissions for contributors
security.content_integrity: GitHub branch protection and CI validation on PRs

data.db: None; Markdown and YAML files in Git
data.search: Pagefind index generated at build time
data.item_data: YAML files committed by scheduled GitHub Actions fetch

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for PR validation (frontmatter check, link validation, hugo build preview)

constraints.build_time: Hugo build under 60 seconds for 5000 pages
constraints.js: No SPA frameworks in critical render path

reasoner_reviews.product: The Git-based content authoring workflow is well-suited for guild leader contributors who are already GitHub-literate. The distinction between lore articles, item entries, quest guides, and NPC profiles as separate Hugo content types with distinct archetypes will prevent schema drift as community contributions scale.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Hugo taxonomy support handles the multi-dimensional content relationships (faction, region, content type) without a database. Pagefind is the right choice for client-side search on a static site of this scale - it generates a WebAssembly index at build time that serves search without any backend. The scheduled item data fetch script should commit YAML diffs only, not full dataset replacements, to keep Git history manageable.
reasoner_reviews.security: Branch protection rules with required CI checks prevent malicious content injection via pull requests. The CSP should block eval and inline scripts to prevent XSS from any user-contributed markdown that inadvertently includes script tags. Cloudflare bot management handles scraping and abuse at the CDN layer without application-level work.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Hugo builds at sub-second per-page for pure Markdown content but can slow significantly with large image processing pipelines. All game screenshot images should be pre-processed and served from Cloudflare Images to keep Hugo build times within the 60-second SLA.

frontend.app_structure: Hugo site with content types: lore_articles, item_entries, quest_guides, faction_pages, npc_profiles, world_regions, patch_changelogs. Each content type has a dedicated Hugo archetype template and list/single layout pair.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Homepage with featured lore and recently updated items. Section list pages with faceted filtering by faction, region, and content type. Individual article pages with breadcrumbs, related content sidebar, and Pagefind search bar. Item pages with stat comparison tables rendered from YAML data.
frontend.performance_notes: Critical CSS inlined by Hugo pipeline; Tailwind purged to minimal footprint. Images served via Cloudflare Images with automatic WebP/AVIF format negotiation. Pagefind search UI loaded only when search input is focused.
frontend.frontend_risks: Hugo Go template complexity grows with content relationship depth; template caching must be used for taxonomy term lookups to avoid O(n) build time regression

security.auth_design: No site-level authentication; GitHub repository collaborator permissions control who can submit and merge PRs
security.abuse_prevention: GitHub branch protection requires CI pass and one moderator approval before merge; automated CI step runs html-proofer to catch injected external links; Cloudflare rate limiting on CDN for scraping prevention
security.audit_and_logging_controls: GitHub commit history serves as full content audit log; Cloudflare Web Analytics provides aggregate read access logs by page and country

data.entities: lore_articles (slug, title, faction, region, tags, body_md, last_updated), item_entries (item_id, name, type, tier, stats_yaml, drop_source, region, last_updated), quest_guides (quest_id, name, faction, min_level, steps_md, reward_items), npc_profiles (npc_id, name, faction, region, dialogue_notes), patch_changelogs (version, date, changes_md)
data.storage_design: All content stored as Markdown files with YAML frontmatter in content/ directory. Item stats stored in data/items/*.yaml for Hugo data template consumption. Pagefind index written to public/pagefind/ at build time.
data.migration_notes: Initial content import from existing fan wiki via Python scraper script that converts MediaWiki markup to Hugo-compatible Markdown frontmatter format

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub repository -> GitHub Actions CI (on PR: frontmatter validation, internal link check, hugo build smoke test) -> Cloudflare Pages (on merge to main: full hugo build + pagefind index + deploy).
devops.ci_cd_design: PR checks: python validate_frontmatter.py (checks all required fields per content type), htmlproofer for broken links, hugo --buildDrafts --buildFuture to catch template errors. Production deploy: hugo --minify && npx pagefind --site public && cf pages deploy.
devops.rollback_strategy: Cloudflare Pages retains all prior deployments; instant rollback via Cloudflare dashboard to any previous build hash

ISS-001.title: Pagefind search index not included in initial architecture plan
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described full-text search as a future feature but the contract scopes it in MVP. Pagefind must be integrated into the build pipeline from sprint 1.

ISS-002.title: Item data YAML commit strategy creates large Git history bloat
ISS-002.detail: The scheduled item data fetch script as described in round 1 commits the full YAML dataset on every run. For 50,000 items this creates significant Git object accumulation over time.

history.note: Full dataset replacement commit strategy identified as problematic

focus_issues.title: Item data YAML commit strategy creates large Git history bloat
focus_issues.detail: Committing full YAML dataset on every scheduled fetch will accumulate tens of MB per run in Git objects. Need differential update strategy.

latest_recommendations: Switch item data fetch script to commit only changed YAML files using git diff before committing
Add Pagefind build step explicitly to CI/CD pipeline documentation

convergence_state.reason: ISS-002 unresolved and impacts long-term repo maintainability

previous_audits.summary: Solid Hugo-based static wiki architecture with good content taxonomy design. Search integration was missing from the MVP plan and item data fetch strategy will cause Git repository bloat at scale.
strengths: Hugo taxonomy correctly models faction/region/content-type relationships
Cloudflare Pages hosting choice is cost-efficient and globally distributed
CI frontmatter validation prevents schema drift from community contributions
concerns: Pagefind search absent from MVP despite contract scoping it as required
Item YAML fetch script commits full dataset, not diffs, causing repo bloat
recommendations: Integrate Pagefind into the CI/CD build pipeline explicitly
Implement differential YAML commit strategy: script diffs fetched data against existing files and commits only changed entries

issue_updates.title: Pagefind search not in MVP plan
issue_updates.detail: Round 2 plan incorporates Pagefind into the build pipeline

issue_updates.title: Item data YAML commit bloat
issue_updates.detail: Round 1 plan did not specify differential commit strategy

previous_plan.title: MMORPG Community Wiki - Initial Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Hugo-based static wiki for MMORPG lore, items, and quest guides deployed to Cloudflare Pages. Content authored via Git pull requests with GitHub Actions CI validation. Pagefind deferred to phase 2.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Hugo static site with Go templates deployed to Cloudflare Pages. Content types for lore, items, quests, and factions stored as Markdown with YAML frontmatter.
previous_plan.data_model: lore_articles, item_entries, quest_guides, npc_profiles, faction_pages stored as Markdown files. Item stats as YAML data files refreshed by scheduled Python fetch script.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI validates frontmatter and runs hugo build on PRs. Merge to main triggers Cloudflare Pages production deploy.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addresses ISS-002 (item YAML Git bloat) by switching the fetch script to a differential commit strategy using git diff to identify and commit only changed YAML entries. ISS-001 (missing Pagefind) was resolved in round 1 review and is now properly integrated into the build pipeline. The rest of the architecture remains sound: Hugo with Go templates, Cloudflare Pages, frontmatter CI validation.

fix_report.action_taken: Redesigned item data fetch script to use a differential commit strategy: script fetches current item data from game API, runs yaml.safe_load comparison against existing data/items/*.yaml files, and calls git add only on files where content hash differs before committing. Unchanged files are never staged.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Git repository growth from item data refreshes limited to actual changed items per scheduled run, preventing GB-scale accumulation over the wiki lifetime

target_output.title: MMORPG Community Wiki and Lore Compendium
target_output.executive_summary: A Hugo-powered static wiki for a fantasy MMORPG covering lore articles, item databases, quest guides, NPC profiles, and patch changelogs, deployed to Cloudflare Pages global CDN. Content is authored collaboratively via Git pull requests with automated CI validation of Markdown frontmatter schema, internal link integrity, and Hugo build correctness. Pagefind provides client-side full-text search without a backend server. Item data is refreshed from the game public API via a scheduled GitHub Actions script that commits only changed YAML entries to prevent repository bloat.
target_output.architecture_overview: Git-as-CMS static architecture: Hugo 0.121 processes a Markdown/YAML content repository into static HTML at build time and deploys to Cloudflare Pages' global CDN. Content types are modeled as Hugo archetypes with distinct frontmatter schemas for lore_articles, item_entries, quest_guides, npc_profiles, faction_pages, world_regions, and patch_changelogs. Pagefind generates a WebAssembly search index from the Hugo output during the CI build step, enabling full-text search without any backend query service. A scheduled GitHub Actions workflow fetches updated item data from the game public API, computes YAML diffs against the committed dataset, and commits only changed entries, keeping Git history lean.
target_output.technology_stack: Static site generator: Hugo 0.121 (Go templates, native taxonomy, fast incremental builds). Theme: custom Go template layouts with Tailwind CSS 3 compiled via PostCSS. Search: Pagefind 1.1 (WASM index generated at build time, UI loaded on search focus). Item data pipeline: Python 3.12 fetch script with PyYAML for differential YAML commit logic. CI/CD: GitHub Actions (PR validation) + Cloudflare Pages (production deploy). Analytics: Cloudflare Web Analytics (cookieless). Images: Cloudflare Images CDN for game screenshots with automatic format negotiation.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Lore Articles: rich Markdown content with faction and region taxonomy tags, related articles sidebar, inline image support. Item Database: stat tables rendered from YAML data files, filterable by item type and tier, drop source links to region pages. Quest Guides: step-by-step sections with reward item cross-links to item database entries. NPC Profiles: dialogue notes, faction affiliation, and region of appearance. World Regions: overview pages linking to associated lore, quests, items, and NPCs. Patch Changelog Archive: version history with filterable change categories. Pagefind Full-Text Search: searches across all content types simultaneously with content-type facet filter.
target_output.system_components: Hugo static site build (HTML, CSS, JS output), Cloudflare Pages global CDN (atomic deploys, automatic HTTPS, 500 free builds/month), Pagefind WASM index (generated in public/pagefind/ at each deploy), GitHub repository (Markdown and YAML content store, CI/CD orchestration), GitHub Actions CI (frontmatter validation, link checker, hugo build smoke test), Python item data fetch script (runs on schedule in GitHub Actions, commits YAML diffs only), Cloudflare Web Analytics (cookieless page view tracking), Cloudflare Images CDN (pre-processed game screenshots at 3 breakpoints).
target_output.workflows: Community Contribution: contributor forks repository, creates Markdown file from Hugo archetype (e.g. hugo new lore_articles/darkwood-covenant-history.md), fills frontmatter fields (title, faction, region, tags, summary), writes body in Markdown, opens pull request; GitHub Actions CI runs validate_frontmatter.py checking all required fields, htmlproofer checking internal links, hugo --buildDrafts for template errors; passing CI gates moderator review; moderator merges and Cloudflare Pages deploys within 60 seconds. Item Database Refresh: GitHub Actions scheduled workflow (nightly 02:00 UTC) runs fetch_items.py which GETs updated item data from game public API paginated endpoint, parses JSON to YAML per item_id, loads existing data/items/{item_id}.yaml files, computes per-file hash comparison, calls git add only on items where hash differs, commits 'chore: refresh item data ({N} items changed)' and pushes to main, triggering a new Cloudflare Pages production deploy.
target_output.data_model: All persistent content stored as files in Git. lore_articles frontmatter: {title: string, faction: string[], region: string, tags: string[], summary: string, last_updated: ISO8601, draft: bool}. item_entries YAML schema: {item_id: int, name: string, type: enum(weapon|armor|consumable|quest_item), tier: int(1-6), stats: {dmg_min: int, dmg_max: int, armor: int, weight: float}, drop_source: string, region: string, last_updated: ISO8601}. quest_guides frontmatter: {quest_id: string, name: string, faction: string, min_level: int, reward_items: int[], region: string}. npc_profiles frontmatter: {npc_id: string, name: string, faction: string, region: string, role: string}. patch_changelogs frontmatter: {version: string, date: ISO8601, categories: string[]}. Pagefind index schema auto-generated from Hugo output HTML, no manual configuration needed.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Public read interface: all pages are static HTML served from Cloudflare CDN. Pagefind search: client-side JavaScript calls pagefind.search(query) against the WASM index at /pagefind/pagefind.js; returns ranked result objects with excerpt and URL. Item data fetch script consumes game public API: GET https://api.mmorpg-example.com/v2/items?page={n}&per_page=500 (paginated JSON), no auth required (public data). No endpoints exposed by the wiki itself.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CSP header via Cloudflare Pages _headers file: default-src 'self', script-src 'self' 'wasm-unsafe-eval' (required for Pagefind WASM), style-src 'self', img-src 'self' data: imagedelivery.net. No cookies set; Cloudflare Web Analytics is cookieless and fingerprint-free, requiring no consent banner. GitHub branch protection rules: require CI status checks to pass and one moderator approval before merge to main. htmlproofer in CI checks for injected external links and broken references. Cloudflare bot management handles scraping and DDoS at CDN layer.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI on every PR: job 1 runs python validate_frontmatter.py (checks required fields per content type archetype), job 2 runs htmlproofer checking internal link integrity, job 3 runs hugo --buildDrafts --buildFuture as a build smoke test; all 3 jobs must pass for merge. Production deploy on merge to main: Cloudflare Pages runs hugo --minify then npx pagefind --site public then deploys output atomically to global CDN; deploy typically completes in 45-55 seconds for 5000-page site. Scheduled item data refresh: GitHub Actions cron job (nightly 02:00 UTC) runs Python fetch script, commits YAML diffs, pushes to main triggering a new production deploy. Rollback via Cloudflare Pages dashboard: any prior deployment hash can be re-activated instantly.
target_output.observability: Cloudflare Web Analytics provides daily page views, top-N most-read lore articles, top-N most-searched item names (from Cloudflare Analytics Engine event captures), and visitor country breakdown. Cloudflare Pages build logs available in dashboard for each deploy; failed deploys trigger email notification to configured maintainers. Item data refresh script outputs changed item count to GitHub Actions log; anomalous counts (>500 changes in a single run) trigger a manual review step via workflow approval gate.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloudflare Pages free tier: unlimited bandwidth, 500 builds/month, custom domain. Cloudflare Web Analytics: free. Cloudflare Images: $5/month for 100,000 stored images (one-time setup). GitHub Actions: within free tier for public repository. Total projected cost: $5/month. Build time scales linearly with page count; Hugo processes 5000 pages in approximately 8 seconds, well within 60-second SLA. Pagefind index generation adds 10-15 seconds to each build.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Hugo Theme and Pipeline (Weeks 1-4): custom Hugo theme with all content type layouts, Tailwind CSS pipeline, Pagefind integration, GitHub Actions CI (frontmatter validation, link checker, build smoke test), Cloudflare Pages deploy, custom domain and HTTPS. 50 lore articles seeded by core team. Phase 2 Content MVP (Weeks 5-8): item database YAML for 200 starter-zone items, 30 quest guides, world region overview pages, NPC profiles for major NPCs, item data fetch script with differential commit strategy deployed as scheduled GitHub Actions workflow. Phase 3 Community Contribution (Weeks 9+): contributor documentation, Hugo archetype templates published, open contribution invitations to guild leaders via community Discord.
target_output.development_guidelines: Hugo archetype files define the required frontmatter schema for each content type; contributors use hugo new <content-type>/<slug>.md to generate scaffolded files. validate_frontmatter.py in CI enforces required field presence and enum value validation (faction must be one of the defined faction list). No JavaScript framework code in critical render path; vanilla JS only for Pagefind UI init and collapsible sidebar sections. All game screenshots pre-processed to WebP at 640/1024/1920px breakpoints before committing to repository. Item data YAML files formatted by the fetch script using PyYAML dump with default_flow_style=False for human-readable diffs.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Hugo template complexity increases as content relationship depth grows (items linked to regions linked to factions), causing build time regression. Mitigation: Hugo partial template caching for taxonomy lookups, benchmark build time in CI and alert on regression beyond 60 seconds. Risk: Game API changes break the item data fetch script, leaving stale item data silently. Mitigation: fetch script validates response schema with pydantic before committing; schema validation failure triggers GitHub Actions workflow failure and email alert to maintainers. Tradeoff: Hugo over Astro for build speed at 5000+ pages, accepting less flexible component model. Tradeoff: Pagefind WASM over Algolia DocSearch for zero backend cost and privacy, accepting slightly less sophisticated relevance ranking.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Pagefind over Algolia DocSearch to avoid backend dependency and API key management. Selected Cloudflare Web Analytics over Google Analytics to avoid cookie consent requirement. Decided item data fetch script runs nightly rather than on each game patch to prevent excessive build triggers. Hugo confirmed over Astro based on build speed benchmarks at target page count.

metadata.notes: MMORPG static wiki round 2 revision; resolves Git repository bloat from item data YAML fetch script by switching to differential commit strategy.

project_goal.value: Modernized iOS and Android companion app for a card-collecting game replacing aging Objective-C and Java codebase; provides deck building, card collection tracking, trade offer management, and real-time tournament bracket updates
project_goal.rationale: Legacy apps have zero-star review trend due to crashes and missing trade features; rewrite is required to retain player base

target_users.value: Active card game players managing collections of 100-5000 cards, participating in weekly tournaments, and trading with other players
target_users.rationale: Core player segment identified from legacy app analytics; traders and tournament participants are highest-retention users

project_class.rationale: Primary interface is iOS and Android native experience via React Native; backend serves the mobile clients

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack mobile app with real-time tournament updates, player-to-player trade flows, and analytics for deck performance tracking

complexity_level.rationale: Card collection sync with conflict resolution, atomic trade execution, and WebSocket real-time bracket updates make this architecturally non-trivial

risk_level.rationale: Player account data and trade history are personal; no financial transactions but abuse of trade system could cause gameplay harm

data_sensitivity.rationale: Player accounts contain email, display name, and gameplay history; GDPR and COPPA apply for EU and minor players

external_exposure.rationale: All API endpoints require Firebase Auth JWT; no public unauthenticated data endpoints

access_model.value: Firebase Auth with email/password and Google Sign-In; RBAC: player and admin roles; COPPA age gate for accounts under 13 requiring parental consent flow
access_model.rationale: GDPR and COPPA compliance requires age verification and parental consent for minors

feature_scope.value: Card catalogue browser with filters, deck builder with validation (format legality checks), collection tracker with want-list and trade-list flags, trade offer creation and negotiation, tournament bracket viewer with live updates, push notifications for trade responses and bracket advancement
feature_scope.rationale: All features required to replace legacy app functionality plus new trade system

mvp_scope.value: Collection tracker, basic deck builder, card catalogue, Firebase Auth login with Google Sign-In; trade system and tournament brackets in phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Collection and deck features alone will stop active user churn from the legacy app

security_baseline.value: Firebase Auth JWT on all endpoints, TLS 1.3, Fastify rate limiting on trade endpoints, SQL parameterized queries via Prisma, COPPA age gate, player data anonymization for analytics
security_baseline.rationale: Standard mobile app security with specific COPPA controls for minor player accounts

frontend_stack.value: React Native 0.74 with TypeScript using Expo managed workflow; React Navigation 6 for routing; React Query for server state
frontend_stack.rationale: Expo managed workflow reduces native toolchain complexity; React Native enables single codebase for iOS and Android

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with Fastify framework; Prisma ORM; WebSocket via Fastify plugin for tournament bracket push
backend_stack.rationale: Fastify's low overhead suits high-frequency deck sync requests; Prisma type-safe ORM prevents SQL injection

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for player accounts, collections, decks, and trade history; Redis 7 for WebSocket session state and active trade offer locks
data_platform.rationale: Relational model fits card collection ownership with complex trade transaction requirements; Redis for ephemeral real-time state

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in eu-west-1; RDS Aurora PostgreSQL Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis
hosting_target.rationale: EU hosting satisfies GDPR data residency requirement for European player base

privacy_retention_policy.value: Player account data retained until account deletion requested; deletion fulfills within 30 days per GDPR Article 17; trade history retained 2 years for dispute resolution; analytics events anonymized at collection point
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR right to erasure and legitimate interest in trade dispute data

future_scope.value: In-app card trading marketplace with auction mechanics, social guild features, seasonal ranked ladder, card artwork gallery with high-res downloads
future_scope.rationale: Phase 3 monetization and engagement features

constraints.value: iOS App Store and Google Play submission compliance; maximum 60-second cold start for deck builder; GDPR data residency in EU; no third-party ad SDKs
constraints.rationale: App store policies, performance SLA for retention, and legal constraints

observability_baseline.value: New Relic Mobile APM for crash reporting and session traces; custom analytics events to New Relic for deck builder funnel and trade conversion; backend Fastify structured logs to CloudWatch
observability_baseline.rationale: New Relic provides both mobile and backend observability in a single platform

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints; Expo OTA updates for JS-only changes bypassing app store review cycles
execution_preference.rationale: Expo OTA enables rapid hotfixes without full store submission delays

compliance_context.value: GDPR for EU player personal data (email, display name, gameplay history); COPPA age gate and parental consent flow for accounts under 13 in the US
compliance_context.rationale: Game has significant EU and US minor player populations requiring both regulations

project.goal: Replace legacy Objective-C and Java card game app with modern React Native cross-platform app
project.target_users: Card game players managing collections and trading
project.timeline: Phase 1 MVP (collection + deck builder) in 12 weeks; trade system and tournaments in phase 2

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for local UI (deck builder drag-and-drop)

backend.api: REST + WebSocket (Fastify ws plugin) for tournament bracket events

security.auth: Firebase Auth JWT verified by Fastify plugin on all routes

data.cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 for WebSocket session state and trade locks

devops.mobile: Expo EAS Build and Submit for app store releases

constraints.performance: 60s cold start limit for deck builder

reasoner_reviews.product: The trade negotiation flow is the highest-value differentiator from the legacy app and must be atomic to preserve player trust. Deck format legality validation must use the same rule engine on both client and server to prevent desyncs. The COPPA age gate must appear early in onboarding before any data collection, not as an afterthought.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Optimistic UI for deck edits with debounced server sync is the right pattern for the deck builder given the 60-second cold start SLA - full round-trip on every card add would be too slow. The trade offer lock in Redis is critical to prevent double-acceptance race conditions. WebSocket connection for tournament brackets should use a dedicated connection, not multiplex with API calls, to isolate reconnect logic.
reasoner_reviews.security: The COPPA age gate must block all data collection before age verification completes. Firebase Auth custom claims should carry the player role and age_verified flag so the Fastify backend can enforce COPPA restrictions without a database lookup on every request. Trade endpoint rate limiting must be per-player, not per-IP, to prevent abuse by players behind shared IPs.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GDPR data residency in eu-west-1 means the Firebase Auth project must also be configured for EU data storage. Expo OTA updates are JS-only and cannot ship native module changes, which must be communicated clearly to the team to avoid failed OTA deployments.

frontend.app_structure: Expo managed workflow with React Navigation 6 tab navigator (Collection, Deck Builder, Trades, Tournaments, Profile). Deep link support for trade offer notifications. Expo Notifications for push.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Collection Screen: card grid with filter bar (by type, rarity, set); tap to view card detail with want-list and trade-list toggle. Deck Builder: drag-and-drop card list with format legality indicator and stats panel. Trade Offers: inbox list of pending and active trades; offer detail with accept/counter/decline actions. Tournament Brackets: tree visualization with live WebSocket updates for match results.
frontend.state_management: React Query manages all server-fetched state (collection, card catalogue, trade offers). Zustand manages ephemeral deck builder state (card ordering, unsaved changes). Optimistic UI for deck edits synced to server with debounce 800ms.
frontend.frontend_risks: Expo OTA updates limited to JS bundle changes; native module upgrades require full EAS Build and store re-submission. Deep link handling for trade offer push notifications requires careful universal link configuration on both iOS and Android.

backend.service_design: Fastify monolith with domain modules: auth (Firebase JWT verification), collection (card ownership CRUD), decks (deck management and format validation), trades (offer lifecycle and atomic execution), tournaments (bracket queries and WebSocket event emission)
backend.api_patterns: REST for collection, deck, and trade CRUD. WebSocket via @fastify/websocket for tournament bracket subscription. All routes protected by Firebase JWT Fastify plugin. Rate limits: trade offer creation 10/min/player, deck save 60/min/player.
backend.failure_handling: Trade offer atomic execution uses Prisma interactive transactions to update both players' collections and mark offer as completed in a single database transaction; Redis lock prevents concurrent acceptance of the same offer.

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth with email/password and Google Sign-In; COPPA age gate on registration collects date of birth before Firebase account creation; under-13 accounts require parental consent email flow before activation
security.authorization_model: Firebase custom claims: {role: 'player'|'admin', age_verified: bool, coppa_minor: bool}. Fastify middleware reads claims from JWT to enforce COPPA restrictions on data collection endpoints.
security.privacy_controls: Analytics events anonymized by hashing player_id with daily rotating salt before sending to New Relic. GDPR deletion endpoint marks account as deletion_requested; background job purges all personal data within 30 days and sends confirmation email.

data.entities: player_accounts (id, firebase_uid, display_name, email_encrypted, dob_hash, region, coppa_minor, created_at, deleted_at), card_catalogue (id, name, set_code, rarity, type, format_legality_json, image_url), player_collections (id, player_id, card_id, quantity, want_flag, trade_flag), decks (id, player_id, name, format, card_list_json, last_synced_at), trade_offers (id, initiator_id, counterparty_id, offered_card_ids, requested_card_ids, status, created_at, resolved_at), tournament_registrations (id, tournament_id, player_id, bracket_position, eliminated_at)
data.storage_design: Aurora PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ in eu-west-1 for all persistent player data. Redis 7 for trade offer lock keys (TTL 5 minutes) and WebSocket session-to-player-id mapping.
data.consistency_notes: Trade execution uses Prisma interactive transaction: lock offer row FOR UPDATE, validate both players still own offered cards, transfer card quantities, update offer status to completed, all within single transaction. Redis lock provides optimistic pre-check before DB transaction.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate in eu-west-1: API service (2-4 tasks, auto-scale on CPU 70%) and WebSocket service (separate task definition to allow independent scaling). Aurora PostgreSQL Multi-AZ. ElastiCache Redis single-node (replication group for production).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint, tsc, jest unit tests, integration tests against Docker Compose PostgreSQL on PR. EAS Build triggered on merge to main for Expo app bundles. ECS deploy via CodeDeploy blue/green on backend merge.
devops.observability_stack: New Relic APM on Fastify (custom instrumentation for trade execution latency). New Relic Mobile SDK in Expo app for crash reports and session traces. CloudWatch Logs for ECS task stdout.

ISS-001.title: Deck sync conflict resolution strategy not specified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described optimistic UI but did not define what happens when two devices edit the same deck concurrently. Server-wins last-write strategy with server timestamp was agreed.

ISS-002.title: WebSocket and REST API sharing same Fargate task creates scaling contention
ISS-002.detail: Combining the persistent WebSocket connection handler for tournament brackets with the stateless REST API in the same ECS task prevents independent scaling; tournament match broadcasts can starve REST API CPU.

history.note: Co-located WebSocket and REST identified as scaling risk

focus_issues.title: WebSocket and REST API sharing same Fargate task creates scaling contention
focus_issues.detail: WebSocket connections for tournament brackets are long-lived and CPU-intensive during broadcast fan-out; needs to run in a separate ECS task definition from REST API.

latest_recommendations: Split WebSocket tournament bracket service into its own ECS task definition with separate auto-scaling policy
Define deck conflict resolution explicitly as last-write-wins with server timestamp

convergence_state.reason: ISS-002 unresolved; WebSocket scaling contention unaddressed
