project.goal: Build a scalable esports tournament platform with bracket management, registration, and prize payouts
project.target_users: Organizers, participants, spectators, and streaming overlay consumers
project.timeline: Beta in 10 weeks, public launch in 16 weeks

frontend.state: TanStack Query for server state; Zustand for bracket editor local state

backend.api: REST with OpenAPI 3.0; WebSocket for live bracket events

security.auth: Auth0 with RBAC

data.cache: Redis 7 for bracket state cache and Pub/Sub overlay events

constraints.overlay_slo: Bracket embed API < 50ms P99 for 10k concurrent reads

reasoner_reviews.product: The tournament platform scope is well-delineated. Single-elimination for MVP is the right constraint -- round-robin adds significant bracket management complexity. The streaming overlay API is a differentiator that should be treated as a first-class public API with its own rate limiting tier.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js SSR for public tournament pages is the correct choice for SEO and initial load performance on high-traffic streams. Redis Pub/Sub for bracket events is a good fit for overlay fan-out without WebSocket connections from Fastify to thousands of overlay consumers. Bracket generation idempotency can be implemented via a deterministic seed hash -- if the same participants are submitted twice, the same bracket structure is produced.
reasoner_reviews.security: Stripe webhook signature verification (HMAC-SHA256 with Stripe secret) must be validated before processing any payout event. Auth0 RBAC must prevent participants from reporting match scores on brackets they are not seeded in. Prize payout initiation should require organizer MFA re-authentication.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 10k concurrent overlay reads can be served with Redis GET on cached bracket JSON plus CDN caching of the /api/v1/tournaments/:id/bracket endpoint response with 5-second TTL. This avoids PostgreSQL read amplification during peak stream events.

backend.service_design: Fastify monolith with domain modules: TournamentModule (bracket logic, registration), MatchModule (score reporting, seeding), PrizeModule (Stripe Connect payout orchestration), OverlayModule (public bracket read API), WebSocketModule (bracket change events)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful at /api/v1 with OpenAPI 3.0; WebSocket at /ws/tournaments/:id/bracket for live bracket events; public bracket embed API at /api/v1/tournaments/:id/bracket with API key auth and CDN caching
backend.business_modules: Bracket generation: deterministic single-elimination tree from seeded participant list using a stable sort by seed ranking, stored as adjacency list in bracket_rounds table; idempotency via bracket_seed_hash unique constraint. Score reporting: submitter must be the seeded player or organizer; score locked after organizer confirmation. Prize payout: Stripe Connect transfer initiated only after tournament status=completed and organizer KYC verified.
backend.failure_handling: Stripe webhook retry handling with idempotency key on transfer events; bracket generation wrapped in PostgreSQL transaction with rollback on constraint violation; WebSocket client disconnect handled with exponential backoff reconnect

frontend.app_structure: Next.js App Router with server components for public tournament pages; client components for organizer dashboard and bracket editor
frontend.pages_and_flows: Public: /tournaments/:id (bracket view, live results), /tournaments/:id/embed (streaming overlay iframe). Authenticated: /dashboard (organizer tournament list), /tournaments/new (creation wizard), /tournaments/:id/manage (registration, bracket control, match reporting), /tournaments/:id/prizes (pool definition and payout)
frontend.state_management: TanStack Query for tournament data, participant lists, and match results; Zustand for bracket editor drag-and-drop seeding state; WebSocket subscription hooked into TanStack Query cache for live bracket updates
frontend.accessibility_notes: Bracket tables include ARIA role=grid and keyboard navigation for accessibility compliance; public pages pass WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios

security.auth_design: Auth0 universal login with RBAC claims; organizer accounts require email verification; prize payout actions trigger Auth0 step-up MFA challenge
security.authorization_model: Organizer: full CRUD on own tournaments; Participant: read own registration, submit match scores for own seeded matches; Spectator (anonymous): read public tournament pages and overlay API; Admin: global tournament management
security.secrets_management: Stripe secret key and webhook signing secret in AWS Secrets Manager; Auth0 client secret in Secrets Manager; 30-day rotation policy
security.abuse_prevention: Bracket embed API rate limited at 1000 req/min per API key via Redis token bucket; score reporting requires exactly one submission per match slot per authorized player, duplicate submissions rejected with 409

data.entities: tournaments (id UUID PK, organizer_id FK, title, format ENUM single_elimination/round_robin, status ENUM registration/seeding/in_progress/completed, max_participants INT, prize_pool_usd NUMERIC, bracket_seed_hash TEXT, created_at). participants (id UUID PK, tournament_id FK, player_account_id FK, seed_position SMALLINT, registration_status ENUM registered/confirmed/disqualified, stripe_account_id, registered_at). bracket_rounds (id UUID PK, tournament_id FK, round_number SMALLINT, match_slot SMALLINT, player_a_id FK, player_b_id FK, winner_id FK, score_a SMALLINT, score_b SMALLINT, status ENUM pending/in_progress/reported/confirmed). prize_pools (id UUID PK, tournament_id FK, placement SMALLINT, amount_usd NUMERIC, recipient_participant_id FK, stripe_transfer_id, paid_at). streaming_overlays (id UUID PK, tournament_id FK, api_key TEXT UNIQUE, created_by FK, created_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 primary with read replica for public bracket queries; Redis 7 for bracket JSON cache (5-second TTL) and Pub/Sub channel per tournament for live score events
data.retention_and_deletion: Participant PII deleted on GDPR erasure request; prize financial records archived to S3 at 7 years; tournament bracket results retained indefinitely as public sports records

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: Next.js frontend service (2-8 tasks auto-scaling on request rate), Fastify API service (2-10 tasks on CPU 60%), Redis ElastiCache cluster (2 nodes), RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ. CloudFront CDN in front of Next.js for public tournament pages and overlay API caching.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint (ESLint), type-check (tsc --noEmit), unit tests (Vitest), integration tests against test DB, Docker build and ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. Branch protection requires CI green and 1 approval.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM tracing across Next.js server components and Fastify handlers; custom metrics: active_tournaments_count, match_reports_per_minute, overlay_api_rps, stripe_payout_success_rate, bracket_generation_duration_ms
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS rolling update with minimum healthy percent 100 during deploy; automatic rollback triggered by Datadog monitor on error rate > 2% within 5 minutes of deploy

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass tournament platform with real-time bracket updates and Stripe Connect prize payouts. Key decisions: Redis Pub/Sub for overlay fan-out avoids direct WebSocket scaling problems; bracket generation as deterministic idempotent seed-hash operation; CloudFront caching on overlay API endpoint satisfies 10k concurrent read SLO; Auth0 step-up MFA for prize payout initiation satisfies financial action elevation requirement.
target_output.title: Esports Tournament Platform Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale fullstack tournament management platform built with Next.js 14 and Node.js Fastify on AWS ECS Fargate. Supports single-elimination and round-robin bracket generation, participant registration with seeding, real-time match score reporting, live bracket WebSocket events for streaming overlays, and Stripe Connect prize pool disbursements with KYC gating. Designed to handle hundreds of simultaneous tournaments with 10k concurrent overlay API reads during peak streaming events.
target_output.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 App Router frontend served via CloudFront CDN -- server components for SEO-optimized public tournament pages, client components for the organizer dashboard and bracket editor. Fastify REST API on ECS Fargate handles tournament lifecycle, match reporting, and prize orchestration. Redis 7 Pub/Sub propagates bracket change events from the API to WebSocket subscribers, decoupling write load from push fan-out. PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ stores all bracket state, participant registrations, and financial records. The public bracket embed API endpoint is cached at CloudFront with a 5-second TTL to absorb overlay consumer traffic without PostgreSQL read pressure. Auth0 provides RBAC-enforced authentication; Stripe Connect handles organizer KYC and prize payout transfers.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, TanStack Query, Zustand. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify with TypeBox, OpenAPI 3.0. Database: PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ, Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster. Auth: Auth0 with RBAC and step-up MFA. Payments: Stripe Connect. CDN: CloudFront. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB, ECR, Secrets Manager. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Observability: Datadog APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Tournament Creation: organizer configures format (single-elimination/round-robin), registration window, max participants, prize pool amounts per placement. Participant Registration: public registration form, Auth0 login required, seeding assignment by organizer or automatic ELO-based seeding. Bracket Generation: deterministic bracket tree generated from seeded participant list with idempotency via seed hash, published as PostgreSQL adjacency list and cached in Redis. Match Reporting: seeded players submit scores for their match slot; organizer confirms to lock results; bracket automatically advances winner. Live Overlay API: public bracket JSON at /api/v1/tournaments/:id/bracket with API key, cached 5 seconds at CloudFront; WebSocket stream for incremental bracket events. Prize Payout: organizer reviews final standings, triggers Stripe Connect transfers to participant Stripe accounts, requires KYC verification and step-up MFA.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront CDN: caches public tournament pages and overlay API responses. ALB: routes traffic to Next.js and Fastify Fargate services. Next.js App (2-8 Fargate tasks): SSR public pages, client-side organizer dashboard. Fastify API (2-10 Fargate tasks): REST tournament API, WebSocket bracket events, Stripe webhook receiver. Redis ElastiCache (2 nodes): bracket JSON cache, Pub/Sub per-tournament channel, API key rate limiting token buckets. PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ: tournaments, participants, bracket_rounds, prize_pools, streaming_overlays tables. AWS Secrets Manager: Auth0 secrets, Stripe keys, DB credentials. GitHub Actions + ECR: CI and image registry.
target_output.workflows: Bracket Generation: organizer closes registration and clicks 'Generate Bracket'; Fastify computes a stable seed sort of confirmed participants, generates the single-elimination tree as bracket_rounds rows in a PostgreSQL transaction, computes bracket_seed_hash and stores as UNIQUE constraint; if re-run with same participants, hash matches and generation is a no-op (idempotent); bracket JSON written to Redis cache; WebSocket bracket_generated event published to tournament Pub/Sub channel. Match Score Reporting: when a match is in_progress, both seeded players can submit scores via POST /api/v1/matches/:id/report with their Auth0 JWT; second submission auto-confirms if scores agree, or flags for organizer review if disputed; organizer confirms winner via PATCH /api/v1/matches/:id/confirm; bracket_rounds row updated, next round match slot populated, Redis cache invalidated, Pub/Sub event emitted. Prize Disbursement: after tournament status=completed, organizer navigates to /tournaments/:id/prizes; platform validates all participant stripe_account_id values are KYC-verified via Stripe API; organizer authenticates with step-up MFA; POST /api/v1/tournaments/:id/prizes/disburse creates Stripe Connect transfer_group, initiates individual transfers per placement, records stripe_transfer_id on prize_pools rows; Stripe webhook confirms transfers and marks paid_at.
target_output.data_model: tournaments (id UUID PK, organizer_id FK, title TEXT, game_title TEXT, format ENUM single_elimination/round_robin, status ENUM registration/seeding/in_progress/completed, max_participants INT, prize_pool_usd NUMERIC, bracket_seed_hash TEXT UNIQUE, registration_closes_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at). participants (id UUID PK, tournament_id FK, player_account_id FK, display_name TEXT, seed_position SMALLINT, registration_status ENUM registered/confirmed/disqualified, stripe_account_id TEXT, registered_at). bracket_rounds (id UUID PK, tournament_id FK, round_number SMALLINT, match_slot SMALLINT, player_a_id FK, player_b_id FK, winner_id FK, score_a SMALLINT, score_b SMALLINT, reported_by_a_at TIMESTAMPTZ, reported_by_b_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM pending/in_progress/reported/disputed/confirmed). prize_pools (id UUID PK, tournament_id FK, placement SMALLINT, amount_usd NUMERIC, recipient_participant_id FK, stripe_transfer_id TEXT, stripe_transfer_group TEXT, paid_at TIMESTAMPTZ). streaming_overlays (id UUID PK, tournament_id FK, api_key TEXT UNIQUE, created_by FK, request_count BIGINT DEFAULT 0, created_at).
target_output.api_design: REST at /api/v1 authenticated via Auth0 Bearer JWT unless noted. POST /tournaments (organizer: create tournament). GET /tournaments/:id (public: tournament metadata). POST /tournaments/:id/participants (participant: register). GET /tournaments/:id/participants (organizer: list with seeding). POST /tournaments/:id/bracket/generate (organizer: idempotent bracket generation). GET /tournaments/:id/bracket (public with API key: returns bracket JSON, CDN cached 5s). PATCH /matches/:id/report (participant: submit score). PATCH /matches/:id/confirm (organizer: confirm winner). POST /tournaments/:id/prizes/disburse (organizer + MFA: trigger Stripe Connect transfers). POST /webhooks/stripe (raw body, Stripe-Signature header verification). WebSocket: /ws/tournaments/:id/bracket (any authenticated user: subscribe to bracket events). Rate limits: overlay API 1000 req/min per API key; score report 10 req/min per participant.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth: Auth0 universal login, email-verified accounts required for organizers and participants; anonymous access for public bracket pages. RBAC claims in JWT: organizer (owns tournament), participant (registered in tournament), admin (global). Prize disbursement requires Auth0 step-up MFA challenge via /authorize with acr_values=http://schemas.openid.net/pape/policies/2007/06/multi-factor. Stripe: webhook signature verified with Stripe-Signature HMAC-SHA256 before any payout processing; Stripe Connect KYC status checked via Stripe API before initiating transfers. GDPR: participant PII deletion on erasure request; financial records (prize_pools with stripe_transfer_id) exempted from deletion under financial record retention obligation, pseudonymized after 7 years. COPPA: participants under 13 require parental consent before tournament registration. API key scoped to single tournament ID, rate-limited via Redis token bucket at 1000 req/min. CSRF protection via SameSite=Strict cookie on organizer session tokens.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate with two services: next-app (min 2, max 8 tasks, scale on ALB request count) and fastify-api (min 2, max 10 tasks, scale on CPU 60%). GitHub Actions pipeline: lint + tsc + Vitest unit tests + integration tests against ephemeral PostgreSQL container, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling update (min healthy 100%). Environments: dev (single task each, shared Redis), staging (2 tasks each, dedicated Redis and RDS), production (auto-scaling, Multi-AZ RDS, ElastiCache cluster mode). Secrets Manager stores Auth0 client secret, Stripe secret and webhook signing secret, DB password with 30-day rotation. CloudFront distribution with 5-second TTL cache on /api/v1/tournaments/*/bracket; cache invalidated on bracket update via CloudFront invalidation API call from Fastify.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces Next.js server component render times and Fastify route handlers including bracket generation and Stripe payout duration. Custom metrics: active_tournaments_count (gauge), match_reports_per_minute (rate), overlay_api_rps (rate by tournament_id), stripe_payout_success_rate (ratio), bracket_generation_duration_ms (histogram), websocket_subscriber_count (gauge). PagerDuty alerts: API error rate > 1%, overlay API P99 > 200ms, Stripe webhook processing failure, bracket generation > 5 seconds. Structured JSON logs with trace_id, tournament_id, and organizer_id for cross-service correlation.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $3,200/month at 500 active tournaments: ECS Fargate (4 avg tasks each service) ~$800, RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.large ~$900, ElastiCache 2-node ~$400, CloudFront (~50TB transfer for overlay traffic) ~$600, Secrets Manager and misc ~$500. Scales to $6,500/month at 2000 tournaments and 10k peak overlay CCU. CloudFront caching prevents linear database scaling with overlay consumers. Redis cluster mode activated above 10GB bracket cache size.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Beta (10 weeks): single-elimination bracket generation with idempotency, participant registration, manual match score reporting with organizer confirmation, public bracket page and overlay embed API, Auth0 RBAC, Stripe Connect payout with KYC gate, Datadog observability, staging and production environments. Phase 2 Public Launch (6 weeks): round-robin bracket format, automatic ELO-based seeding, WebSocket live bracket events, bracket change push to overlay subscribers, GDPR erasure tooling, COPPA consent gate, organizer analytics dashboard (match completion rate, average tournament duration). Phase 3 Scale (4 weeks): team tournament support (participants as teams), sponsor management module, tournament embed widget with branding customization, multi-game ELO rating service.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode enforced for both Next.js and Fastify codebases. Fastify route handlers validated via TypeBox schemas -- no raw req.body access. All bracket state mutations must occur within PostgreSQL transactions. Vitest integration tests cover bracket generation idempotency, score report authorization (participant cannot report other players' matches), and Stripe webhook signature rejection. PR requires green CI and 1 senior engineer approval. Feature flags (LaunchDarkly) gate round-robin format and team tournament features. OpenAPI spec generated from TypeBox schemas via fastify-swagger and kept in sync with frontend API client types.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis Pub/Sub message loss if a subscriber disconnects mid-event during high-volume match reporting. Mitigation: WebSocket clients reconnect and fetch current bracket state via REST on reconnect, treating WebSocket as an optimization not a source of truth. Risk: Stripe Connect KYC failure for international participants prevents prize disbursement. Mitigation: organizer notified 48 hours before tournament end if any prize recipients have unverified accounts; manual bank transfer workflow documented as fallback. Tradeoff: CloudFront 5-second cache TTL on overlay API means score updates take up to 5 seconds to appear on streaming overlays -- acceptable latency for the use case versus eliminating PostgreSQL read amplification at 10k CCU.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Bracket idempotency: deterministic seed hash computed from sorted participant IDs with UNIQUE constraint prevents duplicate bracket rows on re-run. Overlay API scaling: CloudFront 5-second cache with Redis fallback satisfies 10k concurrent read SLO without PostgreSQL scaling. Stripe KYC failure handling: organizer notification 48h before end with documented manual fallback. WebSocket fan-out: Redis Pub/Sub per tournament decouples score writes from subscriber count.

metadata.notes: First-pass esports tournament platform with idempotent bracket generation, Redis Pub/Sub overlay fan-out, and Stripe Connect KYC-gated prize payouts.

project_goal.value: Real-time mobile trivia and quiz battle app for iOS and Android where players compete in live 1-vs-1 and multiplayer room matches with synchronized question delivery, server-authoritative countdowns, instant scoring, and global leaderboards
project_goal.rationale: Core product vision validated in competitive market research

target_users.rationale: Primary addressable audience defined in product strategy

project.goal: Live trivia battle mobile app with 1-vs-1 and room matches, IAP, and global leaderboards
project.target_users: Casual and competitive trivia players globally aged 16-35
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks, v1.0 in 20 weeks

frontend.state: Zustand for local UI state, React Query for REST, Socket.IO client for match events

backend.framework: Fastify for HTTP API, Socket.IO for match server
backend.api: REST (OpenAPI 3.0) + WebSocket (Socket.IO) combined on same Node process per region

security.auth: Firebase Auth (email, Google, Apple sign-in)
security.match_trust: Server-authoritative scoring; client submits answers, server evaluates timing and correctness

data.cache: Redis 7 (ElastiCache) for match state and leaderboard sorted sets

open_questions.question_content: Who owns question set authoring and quality review pipeline

confirmed_decisions.iap: App Store StoreKit 2 + Google Play Billing Library v6
confirmed_decisions.leaderboard_backend: Redis ZREVRANGEBYSCORE for global rank queries

reasoner_reviews.product: The 1-vs-1 real-time match is the core engagement loop and must be polished before multiplayer rooms ship. Server-authoritative scoring is non-negotiable for competitive integrity; the client must never compute final scores. IAP for premium question packs is a reasonable early monetization strategy assuming sufficient free content volume to retain non-paying players.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Running Fastify HTTP and Socket.IO on the same Node process per region reduces operational complexity but requires careful separation of match lifecycle state (in Redis) from persistent data (in PostgreSQL). Match rooms should be Redis-keyed with TTL to auto-expire abandoned sessions. The multi-region ECS topology with a shared RDS read replica in eu-west-1 should be validated against GDPR data residency requirements -- EU player data should not transit us-east-1.
reasoner_reviews.security: Server-authoritative scoring prevents the most common cheating vector. Additionally, answer submission rate limiting per socket connection prevents flooding exploits. IAP receipt validation must occur server-side before content is unlocked -- never trust client-side purchase confirmation. Firebase Auth tokens must be validated on every Socket.IO handshake, not just on HTTP login.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The Expo managed workflow constraint is important to maintain for team velocity; ensure no native modules are pulled in that require ejecting. The 150ms P99 Socket.IO latency requirement is achievable within a single AWS region but cross-region players will need routing to the nearest regional endpoint.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The question content pipeline is an unresolved dependency -- without a quality question backlog the match experience will degrade quickly after launch. This must be staffed as a separate workstream before the MVP launch date.

backend.service_design: Single Node.js 22 process per ECS task running Fastify (HTTP REST on port 3000) and Socket.IO (WebSocket on same port via http server upgrade). Match lifecycle managed in Redis: quiz_room:{room_id} hash with player list, question index, and scores; TTL 30 minutes to auto-expire abandoned matches.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful OpenAPI 3.0 at /api/v1 for account, leaderboard, and IAP endpoints. Socket.IO namespaces: /match for live game events. Match events: 'room:join', 'match:start', 'question:delivered', 'answer:submit', 'round:result', 'match:end'.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly leaderboard snapshot job persists top-1000 global rankings from Redis to PostgreSQL for historical analytics. Weekly question set popularity report aggregated from answer_submissions table.
backend.failure_handling: Socket.IO reconnection with match state rehydration from Redis on reconnect within 60-second window. If Redis match key has expired, client receives 'match:expired' event and is returned to lobby.

frontend.app_structure: Expo managed React Native app with React Navigation v6 stack and tab navigators. Core screens: Auth (Firebase login), Lobby (matchmaking), Match (live question/answer), Results, Leaderboard, Profile, Store (IAP).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Matchmaking: player taps 'Find Match', Zustand match slice transitions through states (idle -> searching -> found -> countdown -> active -> results). Match screen renders server-pushed question with countdown ring animation and locks answer input after server-authoritative cutoff event.
frontend.performance_notes: Question screens use React.memo and avoid re-renders during countdown. Socket.IO event handlers registered once at match mount; cleaned up on unmatch. Images for question packs lazy-loaded with Expo Image.

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth idToken validated on every Fastify request via firebase-admin SDK middleware. Socket.IO auth middleware validates idToken on handshake and stores decoded uid on socket.data.
security.authorization_model: Players can only submit answers to match rooms they have joined; room membership checked against Redis quiz_room:{room_id} player list on every answer event.
security.abuse_prevention: Answer submission rate limit: max 1 answer per question per socket connection enforced server-side. IP-based connection rate limiting via fastify-rate-limit on /api/v1/match/join.

data.entities: player_accounts (id UUID PK, firebase_uid, display_name, avatar_url, created_at, region), quiz_rooms (id UUID PK, mode ENUM solo/duel/room, status ENUM waiting/active/completed, question_set_id FK, started_at, ended_at), match_sessions (id UUID PK, room_id FK, player_id FK, score INTEGER, rank INTEGER, completed_at), answer_submissions (id UUID PK, session_id FK, question_index INTEGER, answer_index SMALLINT, is_correct BOOLEAN, latency_ms INTEGER, submitted_at), question_sets (id UUID PK, title, category, difficulty, is_premium BOOLEAN, created_at), iap_purchases (id UUID PK, player_id FK, product_id, platform ENUM ios/android, receipt_hash, verified_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ in each region for persistent data. Redis 7 ElastiCache for active match state (quiz_room: hashes), session JWTs, and global leaderboard sorted set (leaderboard:global ZADD by total_score).
data.analytics_events: match_started, question_delivered, answer_submitted (with is_correct and latency_ms), match_completed (with final scores), iap_initiated, iap_verified, leaderboard_viewed

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate service per region (us-east-1, eu-west-1) behind an Application Load Balancer with sticky sessions for Socket.IO. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ primary in us-east-1 with read replica in eu-west-1. ElastiCache Redis cluster per region.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR merge to main, run Jest unit tests (80% coverage gate), build Docker image, push to ECR, deploy to staging ECS via rolling update. On tag, promote staging image to production with blue-green switch.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM agent sidecar on each ECS task. Custom Socket.IO event metrics forwarded to Datadog via StatsD. PagerDuty alerting on match error rate > 0.5% or Socket.IO connection refused rate > 1%.

target_output.thinking_summary: Building a real-time trivia battle app requires a server-authoritative match engine above all else -- client scores must never be trusted. Socket.IO with Redis-backed match state is the right pattern for handling reconnects and multi-player room synchronization. Multi-region ECS with region-local Redis satisfies both latency and GDPR data residency requirements. IAP receipt validation must be fully server-side before any content unlock.
target_output.title: QuizBattle -- Real-Time Mobile Trivia Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A real-time mobile trivia battle app built with React Native (Expo) on iOS and Android, backed by a Fastify + Socket.IO Node.js server on AWS ECS Fargate deployed in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Server-authoritative scoring, Redis-backed match state, and Firebase Auth provide a cheat-resistant competitive experience. In-app purchases for premium question packs are validated server-side via App Store and Play Store APIs. GDPR compliance is achieved through regional data isolation and a right-to-erasure API.
target_output.architecture_overview: React Native Expo client connects to the regional ECS Fastify service over HTTPS and upgrades to Socket.IO WebSocket for match events. All match scoring is computed server-side: the client submits answer index and the server evaluates correctness and timing before broadcasting round results. Active match state (player list, current question index, scores) lives in Redis hashes with 30-minute TTL. PostgreSQL stores persistent player accounts, question sets, match history, and IAP purchase records. Global leaderboard rankings are maintained as Redis sorted sets (ZADD by total_score) with nightly snapshots to PostgreSQL. Regional ECS clusters in us-east-1 and eu-west-1 ensure EU players' data is processed within EU infrastructure to satisfy GDPR Article 46.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native 0.74, TypeScript, Expo managed workflow, React Navigation v6, Zustand, React Query, Socket.IO client, NativeWind. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify 4, Socket.IO 4, firebase-admin SDK, fastify-rate-limit. Database: PostgreSQL 16 (RDS Multi-AZ), Redis 7 (ElastiCache cluster). Cloud: AWS ECS Fargate, ALB with sticky sessions, RDS, ElastiCache, ECR, CloudWatch. Auth: Firebase Auth. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Datadog APM + StatsD. Crash reporting: Datadog RUM for mobile.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Live Match Engine: server broadcasts synchronized question start with authoritative countdown timer; clients submit answers within time window; server evaluates correctness and latency bonus; round results broadcasted to all participants after cutoff. Matchmaking: 1-vs-1 quick match via Redis queue (BRPOP from matchmaking:{category} list); public rooms with shareable join codes. Question Pack IAP: premium question sets unlocked after server-side receipt validation; StoreKit 2 on iOS, Play Billing v6 on Android. Global Leaderboard: Redis ZREVRANGEBYSCORE queries for rank and percentile; per-category leaderboards. Player Profiles: win rate, total matches, top categories, achievement badges.
target_output.system_components: React Native Expo client (iOS + Android), AWS Application Load Balancer (sticky sessions for Socket.IO), ECS Fargate service (Fastify + Socket.IO Node process), ElastiCache Redis 7 (match state + leaderboard sorted sets + session cache), RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (player data + question sets + IAP records), Firebase Auth (identity provider), Apple App Store Receipt Validation API, Google Play Developer API (IAP verification), Datadog APM + StatsD agent, GitHub Actions CI/CD.
target_output.workflows: Live 1-vs-1 Match: player opens app and taps 'Quick Match'; client calls POST /api/v1/match/join with category preference; server pushes player into Redis matchmaking queue; when two players are paired, server creates quiz_room hash in Redis, generates a match session for each player in PostgreSQL, and emits 'match:start' to both sockets; server broadcasts 'question:delivered' events at tick intervals; players emit 'answer:submit' with answer_index; server validates against correct_answer, computes latency_ms, updates Redis scores, and broadcasts 'round:result'; after final question, server writes match_sessions scores to PostgreSQL, computes rankings, updates leaderboard ZADD, and emits 'match:end' with full results payload. Premium Pack Purchase: player taps pack in Store screen; Expo IAP initiates native purchase flow; on purchase completion, client receives platform receipt; client posts receipt to POST /api/v1/iap/verify; server validates receipt against Apple or Google IAP API, records verified iap_purchase in PostgreSQL, and returns unlocked question_set_ids; client Zustand store updates available packs.
target_output.data_model: player_accounts (id UUID PK, firebase_uid TEXT UNIQUE, display_name TEXT, avatar_url TEXT, region TEXT, total_score INTEGER, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). quiz_rooms (id UUID PK, mode TEXT CHECK IN (duel, room), status TEXT CHECK IN (waiting, active, completed), question_set_id UUID FK, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ). match_sessions (id UUID PK, room_id UUID FK, player_id UUID FK, score INTEGER, rank SMALLINT, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). answer_submissions (id UUID PK, session_id UUID FK, question_index SMALLINT, answer_index SMALLINT, is_correct BOOLEAN, latency_ms INTEGER, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). question_sets (id UUID PK, title TEXT, category TEXT, difficulty TEXT, is_premium BOOLEAN, question_count INTEGER). iap_purchases (id UUID PK, player_id UUID FK, product_id TEXT, platform TEXT CHECK IN (ios, android), receipt_hash TEXT UNIQUE, verified_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: REST at /api/v1: POST /auth/verify (Firebase token exchange), GET /players/:id (profile), POST /match/join (enter matchmaking queue), GET /match/:id/results (post-match summary), GET /leaderboard?category=&limit=100 (ranked list), POST /iap/verify (receipt validation + content unlock), GET /question-sets (available packs for player), DELETE /players/:id (GDPR erasure). Socket.IO on /match namespace: client emits 'answer:submit' {room_id, question_index, answer_index}; server emits 'question:delivered' {question, options, deadline_ts}, 'round:result' {correct_index, scores}, 'match:end' {final_scores, ranks}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Firebase idToken validated on every Fastify middleware invocation and every Socket.IO handshake via firebase-admin verifyIdToken. Server-authoritative scoring: server holds correct answers in Redis match hash; clients never receive answer keys. IAP receipt validation: POST to Apple verifyReceipt or Google purchases.products.get API before any content unlock; receipt_hash stored to prevent replay. GDPR: DELETE /players/:id triggers cascade deletion of player_accounts, match_sessions, answer_submissions, and iap_purchases; erasure job queued asynchronously with confirmation. COPPA: age gate on registration; accounts flagging under-13 blocked from account creation with notice to parent email. Rate limiting: 1 answer per question per socket; 10 match join requests per minute per IP.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate service in us-east-1 (primary) and eu-west-1 (EU-isolated). Each region has its own ALB, ECS service (2-8 tasks auto-scaling on Socket.IO connection count), ElastiCache Redis cluster, and RDS read replica. GitHub Actions pipeline: on PR merge, Jest tests (80% gate), Docker build, push to ECR, deploy to staging with rolling update; on release tag, blue-green production deploy per region. ALB sticky sessions via AWSALB cookie ensure Socket.IO connections remain on the same ECS task. ECS task CPU 70% scale-out trigger with 5-minute cooldown. Secrets managed via AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces all Fastify HTTP requests and Socket.IO event handlers with trace IDs. Custom StatsD metrics: match.active_count (gauge), question.delivered_latency_ms (histogram), answer.submission_rate (counter), iap.verification_success_rate (gauge). Datadog dashboard: active matches, Socket.IO connected clients per region, match error rate, IAP funnel. PagerDuty P1 alerts: match error rate > 0.5%, Socket.IO connection timeout rate > 1%, RDS connection pool exhaustion.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $800/month at 5,000 DAU: 2 ECS tasks per region ($180), RDS Multi-AZ db.t4g.medium per region ($280), ElastiCache r7g.large per region ($240), ALB and data transfer ($100). Auto-scaling to 8 tasks per region handles 30,000 concurrent Socket.IO connections. Redis sorted set leaderboard operations are O(log N) and scale to millions of entries without schema changes. Reserved instance pricing for RDS reduces costs ~40% at steady-state traffic.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): Firebase Auth login, 1-vs-1 live match with 3 built-in question sets, server-authoritative scoring, global leaderboard, basic player profiles, ECS single-region (us-east-1), GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 v1.0 (8 weeks): public multiplayer rooms (2-8 players), IAP for premium question packs with App Store + Play Store validation, GDPR erasure endpoint, eu-west-1 regional deployment, Datadog APM instrumentation. Phase 3: tournament brackets, question creator tools, subscription tier, web client.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across React Native and Node.js codebases. Server answer evaluation logic unit-tested with Jest; Socket.IO match lifecycle integration-tested with socket.io-client in test environment. No client-side score computation permitted -- all scoring logic lives in backend match_engine module. All Socket.IO event handlers validate room membership against Redis before processing. GDPR erasure endpoint covered by integration test verifying cascade deletion. Expo SDK upgrades require full regression on iOS TestFlight and Android internal track before production release.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Socket.IO sticky session requirement means ECS task failure drops active matches. Mitigation: Redis match state allows reconnection rehydration within 60 seconds; implement graceful task drain with ECS connection draining before scale-in. Risk: Firebase Auth dependency -- outage prevents all logins. Mitigation: guest session tokens for match play only; full account features degrade gracefully. Tradeoff: single Node.js process for HTTP + Socket.IO per task simplifies deployment but means CPU-bound work (question evaluation at high concurrency) competes with WebSocket I/O; mitigate with worker_threads for CPU-intensive batch operations at load. Risk: COPPA age gate relies on self-reported date of birth. Mitigation: flag accounts under 13, block IAP, notify parent email -- consistent with COPPA safe harbor requirements.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed server-authoritative scoring model -- client never receives correct answers before match end. Selected Redis ZADD sorted sets over PostgreSQL for leaderboard to achieve O(log N) rank queries. GDPR erasure implemented as async job with 72-hour SLA matching Article 17 obligation. IAP validation is fully server-side with receipt_hash deduplication to prevent replay attacks.

metadata.notes: Real-time mobile trivia battle app with server-authoritative match engine, Socket.IO multi-region, Firebase Auth, and IAP validation.

profile.domain: Tabletop RPG Campaign Management Desktop App

project_goal.value: Cross-platform desktop application for tabletop RPG game masters to design and run interactive campaign sessions, including dynamic encounter maps, NPC dialogue trees, initiative trackers, and a locally stored campaign asset library
project_goal.rationale: Replacing fragmented spreadsheet and text file workflows with an integrated offline-first GM tool

target_users.value: Tabletop RPG game masters managing campaigns with 3-6 players across multiple sessions
target_users.rationale: Single user category; no multi-tenant or server-side user management required

project_class.rationale: Offline-first local application with no cloud backend; Tauri provides cross-platform native packaging

capabilities.rationale: Local UI, Rust command handlers, and embedded SQLite storage; no auth, no cloud, no payments

complexity_level.rationale: Complex UI interactions (encounter map canvas, dialogue tree editor) but straightforward local data model

risk_level.rationale: No network exposure; no sensitive personal data; data loss risk mitigated by export and backup features

data_sensitivity.rationale: Campaign content is user-generated creative content with no PII or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: No network server; all data stored locally; optional export for sharing is file-based

access_model.value: Single-user local application; no authentication required; OS filesystem permissions protect campaign files
access_model.rationale: Desktop app with no multi-user access model

feature_scope.value: Encounter map canvas with token placement and grid overlay, NPC library with dialogue tree editor, initiative tracker with HP and status tracking, campaign asset library (images, notes, handouts), session log recorder, campaign save and export
feature_scope.rationale: Core GM workflow features for session preparation and runtime management

mvp_scope.value: Campaign creation, NPC management with basic stats, encounter map with token drag-and-drop, initiative tracker, campaign save/load to SQLite file
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for playtesting with real GM users before adding dialogue trees and handout library

security_baseline.value: No network endpoints; Tauri CSP preventing eval and inline scripts; campaign export files are unencrypted ZIP archives
security_baseline.rationale: Local-only app; security concerns are XSS-in-webview and supply chain, not network attacks

frontend_stack.value: Tauri 2 with React 18 and TypeScript; Konva.js for encounter map canvas rendering
frontend_stack.rationale: Tauri provides native desktop shell with webview; React for component-based UI; Konva for GPU-accelerated canvas

backend_stack.value: Rust Tauri backend commands for file I/O, SQLite operations, and OS integration via tauri-plugin-sql
backend_stack.rationale: Rust provides safe native performance for file operations and embedded SQLite without external dependencies

data_platform.value: SQLite embedded via tauri-plugin-sql; single .campaign file per campaign stored on user filesystem
data_platform.rationale: Embedded SQLite eliminates external database dependency; file-per-campaign enables easy backup and sharing

hosting_target.value: Local desktop binary distributed via GitHub Releases; auto-update via Tauri updater plugin
hosting_target.rationale: No cloud hosting; GitHub Releases provides simple distribution; Tauri updater for version management

privacy_retention_policy.value: All data stored locally on user device; no telemetry collected; campaign files persist until user deletes them
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Local-only app with no data collection or server-side storage

future_scope.value: Cloud sync for campaign files via user-provided storage (Dropbox, iCloud), multiplayer map view for remote sessions via WebRTC
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP features dependent on core local functionality being stable

constraints.value: Must support Windows 10, macOS 12, and Ubuntu 22.04; app binary size under 30MB; no Electron (performance requirement)
constraints.rationale: Cross-platform requirement with performance constraint ruling out Electron in favor of Tauri

observability_baseline.value: Sentry desktop SDK for crash reporting; local debug log file in app data directory; no remote telemetry
observability_baseline.rationale: Crash reports to Sentry for developer diagnostics; all other observability is local

execution_preference.value: Kanban with weekly milestones; single developer team with monthly beta releases
execution_preference.rationale: Small team; Kanban suits continuous feature delivery without sprint commitments

project.goal: Desktop GM tool for tabletop RPG campaign management and session running
project.target_users: Tabletop RPG game masters
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks, v1.0 in 20 weeks

backend.file_ops: Tauri file dialog and FS plugin

security.webview: Tauri CSP blocking eval and inline scripts

reasoner_reviews.product: The GM tool scope is well-defined for tabletop RPG workflows. Encounter map canvas and initiative tracker are the highest-value features for actual session play. Dialogue tree editor is powerful but risky for MVP scope; should validate simpler NPC stat blocks first.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Tauri 2 with Rust backend commands is the right choice for local-only desktop. tauri-plugin-sql handles SQLite migrations cleanly. Konva.js is proven for interactive canvas scenarios. The single .campaign SQLite file per campaign is elegant for portability but needs a clear schema migration strategy for app updates.
reasoner_reviews.security: Tauri CSP configuration must block eval() and inline script injection in the webview. The Tauri updater must use signed bundles to prevent supply chain attacks via fake update endpoints. Campaign export ZIP files should warn users that they contain all campaign content unencrypted.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 30MB binary constraint is achievable with Tauri 2 which averages 8-15MB for React apps. Windows, macOS, and Linux cross-compilation via GitHub Actions matrix is well-supported by Tauri's build system.

backend.service_design: Rust Tauri commands organized by domain: campaign_commands (create, open, save, export), encounter_commands (map operations, token management), npc_commands (CRUD, initiative), asset_commands (image import, handout storage). Each command invokes tauri-plugin-sql for SQLite operations.
backend.api_patterns: Tauri IPC invoke() calls from React frontend to Rust command handlers; all commands return Result<T, String> serialized as JSON; no HTTP server or REST API
backend.failure_handling: Rust commands return structured error types serialized to frontend; SQLite transactions with rollback on partial encounter state writes; campaign auto-save every 60 seconds to prevent data loss

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with React Router for view navigation: CampaignHub, EncounterMap, NPCLibrary, InitiativeTracker, SessionLog, Settings
frontend.pages_and_flows: Campaign creation wizard (name, system, session notes), Encounter designer (Konva canvas with token layer, grid overlay, fog-of-war toggle), Initiative tracker (sorted HP/status list with drag reordering), NPC sheet editor (stats, abilities, dialogue node list)
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for active campaign session state (current encounter, initiative order, active NPC); React Query for Tauri IPC calls with optimistic updates for token drag operations
frontend.performance_notes: Konva canvas uses requestAnimationFrame for token drag rendering; encounter maps cached as Konva JSON snapshots to avoid re-render on tab switch

security.auth_design: No authentication required; single-user local app
security.secrets_management: No secrets; Tauri updater signing keys stored in GitHub Actions secrets for CI release pipeline only
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Local debug log file in OS app data directory with structured JSON entries; Sentry crash reports include stack traces but no campaign content

data.entities: campaigns (id, name, game_system, created_at, last_opened_at), encounters (id, campaign_id, name, map_json, status ENUM draft/active/completed), npcs (id, campaign_id, name, hp_max, hp_current, ac, stats_json, notes), dialogue_trees (id, npc_id, root_node_id, nodes_json), initiative_orders (id, encounter_id, combatant_id, combatant_type, initiative, hp_current, conditions_json), asset_library_items (id, campaign_id, type ENUM image/handout/note, filename, local_path)
data.storage_design: Single SQLite file per campaign at {appDataDir}/campaigns/{campaign_id}.campaign; schema migrations via embedded SQL scripts on app startup; asset files stored in {appDataDir}/campaigns/{campaign_id}/assets/
data.migration_notes: Schema version stored in campaigns table metadata_json; on open, compare schema_version to app version and run pending migration scripts in order before loading campaign

devops.deployment_topology: No server deployment; GitHub Actions matrix build for win-x64, mac-universal, linux-x64 using tauri-action; signed binaries uploaded to GitHub Release assets
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: cargo test, cargo clippy, npm run build, tauri build --target per platform, upload release assets; beta channel tag triggers pre-release
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous GitHub Release binaries remain downloadable; Tauri updater rollback by downloading previous version tag; SQLite schema rollback not supported so migrations must be additive-only

ISS-001.title: Encounter map state not persisted between tab switches
ISS-001.detail: The round 1 plan stores encounter map state only in React component state. Switching tabs unmounts Konva canvas and loses token positions. SQLite auto-save every 60 seconds does not address immediate tab-switch loss.

ISS-002.title: SQLite schema migration strategy missing from data model
ISS-002.detail: The round 1 plan does not specify how SQLite schema migrations are applied when the app updates. Without a migration strategy, users who open old .campaign files with a new app version will encounter schema mismatch errors.

focus_issues.title: Encounter map state not persisted between tab switches
focus_issues.detail: Konva canvas unmounts on tab switch losing token positions; auto-save interval does not cover immediate navigation loss

focus_issues.title: SQLite schema migration strategy missing from data model
focus_issues.detail: No migration plan for opening old .campaign files with new app versions; additive-only constraint not documented

latest_recommendations: Persist Konva canvas state to Zustand on every token drag-end event so tab navigation does not lose encounter positions
Document additive-only SQLite migration constraint and implement schema_version check on campaign open
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Tabletop RPG GM Desktop Tool - Initial Architecture

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues with no fix actions in round 1 plan

previous_audits.summary: Solid desktop app architecture with appropriate Tauri choices but two implementation gaps reduce plan quality: encounter map state loss on tab navigation and missing SQLite migration strategy for campaign file versioning.
strengths: Tauri 2 + Rust backend command design is idiomatic and matches local-only requirement
Konva.js canvas choice is well-justified for token drag performance
Single .campaign SQLite file per campaign is elegant for portability
concerns: Encounter map Konva canvas loses token state on React component unmount (tab switch)
No SQLite schema migration strategy means old campaign files will break on app updates
blocking_issues: ISS-001: encounter map state loss on tab switch is a user-facing data loss bug in a primary feature
recommendations: Serialize Konva canvas stage JSON to Zustand on every token drag-end and encounter tab-leave event; reload from Zustand on tab-enter

Implement schema_version field in campaigns metadata_json; run embedded migration SQL scripts on campaign open when schema_version < app version; document additive-only constraint for all future schema changes

issue_updates.title: Encounter map state not persisted between tab switches
issue_updates.detail: Plan stores canvas state in React component only; tab unmount destroys state before 60s auto-save fires

issue_updates.title: SQLite schema migration strategy missing
issue_updates.detail: data_model section lists entities but no migration lifecycle described

previous_plan.title: Tabletop RPG GM Desktop Tool - Initial Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A cross-platform Tauri 2 desktop application for tabletop RPG game masters storing all campaign data locally in per-campaign SQLite files, with React 18 frontend for encounter map canvas, NPC management, and initiative tracking.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Tauri 2 shell with React 18 webview frontend communicating via IPC invoke() to Rust backend commands. SQLite embedded storage via tauri-plugin-sql. No network services.
previous_plan.data_model: campaigns, encounters, npcs, dialogue_trees, initiative_orders, asset_library_items all stored in per-campaign SQLite file at appDataDir.
previous_plan.workflows: Encounter map: GM places tokens on Konva canvas, configures NPC stats, launches initiative tracker. Campaign save: SQLite auto-save every 60 seconds.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revision round 2 for the Tauri-based TTRPG GM desktop app. Two issues from round 1 need resolution: encounter map Konva canvas state being lost on tab navigation (ISS-001), and missing SQLite schema migration strategy for campaign file versioning (ISS-002). The fix for ISS-001 is to serialize Konva stage JSON into Zustand on every drag-end and tab-leave, and rehydrate on tab-enter. The fix for ISS-002 is to implement a schema_version check on campaign open with embedded additive-only migration scripts. This is a flawed quality row: the plan resolves ISS-001 but introduces a new inconsistency by describing a cloud sync architecture that contradicts the local_only external_exposure constraint.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Konva stage JSON serialization to Zustand encounterMapSlice on every token drag-end and EncounterMap component unmount; rehydration from Zustand on component mount before Konva stage creation; auto-save now also triggers on navigation away from encounter tab
fix_report.expected_outcome: Token positions and encounter state persist across tab navigation without data loss; 60-second auto-save to SQLite remains as secondary durability mechanism

fix_report.action_taken: Added schema_version INTEGER to campaigns metadata; app startup reads version from opened .campaign file, runs pending additive-only migration SQL scripts from embedded migrations/ directory in order, updates schema_version; documented additive-only constraint in development_guidelines
fix_report.expected_outcome: Old .campaign files open correctly with new app versions; schema_version mismatch triggers migration before load; breaking migrations prohibited by guideline

target_output.title: Tabletop RPG GM Desktop Tool Architecture - Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: A cross-platform Tauri 2 desktop application for tabletop RPG game masters storing all campaign data locally in per-campaign SQLite files. Revision 2 resolves encounter map state persistence via Zustand serialization and adds a schema migration lifecycle for campaign file versioning. The app runs entirely offline with no server components, distributing via GitHub Releases with Tauri auto-updater.
target_output.architecture_overview: Tauri 2 native shell hosting a React 18 webview frontend connected via IPC invoke() to Rust backend commands. React frontend manages encounter map state in a Zustand encounterMapSlice that serializes Konva canvas JSON on every significant state change, ensuring token positions survive tab navigation. Rust backend commands handle SQLite operations via tauri-plugin-sql, file I/O via Tauri FS plugin, and OS integration for file dialogs and app data directory management. No network server or cloud services. Optional cloud sync added via user-configured third-party storage webhooks — this sync module communicates with external cloud endpoints, providing real-time campaign backup to AWS S3 or Google Drive even for local_only deployments.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Tauri 2, React 18, TypeScript, Konva.js for encounter canvas, Zustand for session state, React Router v6. Backend: Rust with tauri-plugin-sql (SQLite), tauri-plugin-fs, tauri-plugin-dialog, tauri-plugin-updater. Data: SQLite embedded per-campaign file. Observability: Sentry desktop SDK for crash reporting. CI: GitHub Actions with tauri-action for cross-platform builds. Distribution: GitHub Releases with signed binaries.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Campaign Hub: create, open, and manage multiple .campaign files; recent campaigns list with last-opened metadata. Encounter Map: Konva canvas with draggable token layer, configurable grid overlay, fog-of-war toggle; token state persisted to Zustand on every drag-end and component unmount. NPC Library: CRUD for NPC stat blocks with HP, AC, ability scores, notes; linked dialogue tree nodes. Initiative Tracker: sorted combatant list with HP current/max, status condition badges, drag reordering, round counter. Session Log: timestamped event entries editable per session. Campaign Export: ZIP archive of SQLite file and assets directory.
target_output.system_components: Tauri 2 application shell (native window manager, auto-updater, tray icon), React 18 webview rendered inside Tauri shell, Zustand encounterMapSlice (serialized Konva stage JSON, token positions, fog state), Rust IPC command handlers (campaign_commands, encounter_commands, npc_commands, asset_commands), tauri-plugin-sql SQLite layer (schema migrations on open, CRUD operations), Tauri FS and dialog plugins for file operations, Sentry desktop SDK for crash telemetry, optional cloud sync webhook module.
target_output.workflows: Encounter Map Persistence: GM drags token on Konva canvas; drag-end handler serializes full stage JSON via Konva.Stage.toJSON() into Zustand encounterMapSlice; on tab navigation away, Tauri IPC encounter_save_state command writes Zustand state to SQLite encounters.map_json column; on tab return, React mounts Konva stage from Zustand serialized JSON ensuring no position loss. Campaign Schema Migration: app reads schema_version from opened .campaign SQLite file; compares to app CURRENT_SCHEMA_VERSION constant; if version is behind, executes pending migration scripts from embedded migrations/0002_add_conditions.sql, migrations/0003_add_dialogue_trees.sql in order within a single SQLite transaction; updates schema_version on success; if migration fails, campaign opens in read-only mode with error dialog.
target_output.data_model: campaigns (id TEXT PK, name TEXT, game_system TEXT, schema_version INTEGER DEFAULT 1, metadata_json TEXT, created_at TEXT, last_opened_at TEXT). encounters (id TEXT PK, campaign_id TEXT FK, name TEXT, map_json TEXT, status TEXT CHECK IN draft/active/completed, created_at TEXT). npcs (id TEXT PK, campaign_id TEXT FK, name TEXT, hp_max INTEGER, hp_current INTEGER, ac INTEGER, stats_json TEXT, notes TEXT). dialogue_trees (id TEXT PK, npc_id TEXT FK, root_node_id TEXT, nodes_json TEXT). initiative_orders (id TEXT PK, encounter_id TEXT FK, combatant_id TEXT, combatant_type TEXT, initiative INTEGER, hp_current INTEGER, conditions_json TEXT). asset_library_items (id TEXT PK, campaign_id TEXT FK, asset_type TEXT, filename TEXT, local_path TEXT). Schema migrations: additive-only SQL scripts embedded in app binary at migrations/{version}_{name}.sql.
target_output.api_design: Tauri IPC commands (invoke from frontend): campaign_create(name, game_system) -> CampaignMeta, campaign_open(path) -> CampaignData (triggers schema migration), campaign_save(campaign_id, data) -> void, encounter_save_state(encounter_id, map_json) -> void, encounter_load(encounter_id) -> EncounterData, npc_upsert(npc_data) -> NpcId, initiative_update(order_id, hp, conditions) -> void, asset_import(campaign_id, file_path) -> AssetMeta, campaign_export(campaign_id, dest_path) -> void. All commands return Result<T, AppError> serialized as {ok: T} or {error: string}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Tauri CSP configured in tauri.conf.json: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; no eval() or inline scripts permitted. Tauri updater uses Ed25519 signed bundles; public key embedded in app binary; fake update endpoint attack mitigated by signature verification. Campaign export ZIP files contain no encrypted data; users warned in export dialog. Sentry crash reports scrubbed to exclude campaign content filenames. No network attack surface for local-only operation.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions matrix build: ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest using tauri-action v0.5; cargo test and cargo clippy --deny warnings run before build; npm run build bundles React app; tauri build produces platform installers (.msi, .dmg, .AppImage). Release pipeline: git tag v{semver} triggers release workflow; signed binaries uploaded to GitHub Release assets; Tauri updater JSON manifest updated at releases/latest endpoint. Beta channel: pre-release tags trigger beta release with opt-in updater channel. No server deployment required.
target_output.observability: Sentry desktop SDK initialized with DSN from app config; captures panics in Rust commands via sentry-panic integration and unhandled JS errors in React webview. Local debug log file at {appDataDir}/logs/debug.log with structured JSON entries for IPC command invocations and SQLite migration events. No remote metrics or telemetry beyond Sentry crash reports. Error rate monitoring via Sentry release tracking per app version.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No server infrastructure costs. GitHub Actions free tier covers CI builds for open-source repo. GitHub Releases storage for binary artifacts (approximately 90MB per release across 3 platforms). Sentry free tier supports up to 5000 crash events per month. Distribution scales to any number of users without infrastructure cost increase.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Campaign CRUD with SQLite per-file storage, NPC library with stat blocks, encounter map with Konva token drag-and-drop and Zustand persistence fix (ISS-001), initiative tracker, schema migration lifecycle (ISS-002), GitHub Actions CI, Sentry integration. Phase 2 (6 weeks): NPC dialogue tree editor with node graph UI, asset library with image import, session log with export, campaign ZIP export. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Tauri auto-updater with signed bundles and beta channel, fog-of-war encounter map layer, handout viewer with PDF support.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust commands must return typed Result<T, AppError>; no unwrap() in production Tauri command handlers. SQLite schema migrations must be additive-only (no column renames or table drops); each migration in a numbered SQL file; tested against minimum supported schema version in CI. Konva state changes trigger Zustand update on drag-end, not on every mouse-move event (performance constraint). React components accessing Tauri IPC use typed invoke wrappers in src/ipc/ module.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Konva canvas performance degradation with >200 tokens on large encounter maps. Mitigation: token culling for off-screen sprites; Konva layer separation for static grid and dynamic token layers. Risk: SQLite file corruption on crash during auto-save. Mitigation: WAL mode enabled; auto-save writes to .campaign.tmp then renames atomically. Tradeoff: single .campaign SQLite file per campaign is portable and backup-friendly but prevents concurrent multi-device editing without the future cloud sync feature. Risk: Tauri webview rendering inconsistencies across OS versions. Mitigation: visual regression CI tests using Playwright desktop.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Konva.js over Pixi.js for encounter canvas based on simpler React integration via react-konva. Confirmed tauri-plugin-sql over better-sqlite3 npm package since Rust-native SQLite avoids Node dependency in desktop binary. SQLite WAL mode enabled by default for crash-safe auto-save. Additive-only migration constraint documented and enforced via CI lint check on migration files.

metadata.notes: Flawed row: plan introduces optional cloud sync module that contradicts local_only external_exposure contract; resolves ISS-001 and ISS-002 but adds architecture inconsistency.

project_goal.value: Decompose a monolithic matchmaker for a competitive online shooter into separate skill estimation, lobby assembly, and connection negotiation microservices, with an ML model predicting match quality scores to minimize wait time and skill imbalance
project_goal.rationale: Monolithic matchmaker has become a scaling bottleneck and prevents independent iteration on skill model vs lobby logic

target_users.value: Game clients submitting matchmaking requests and the game platform engineering team operating the system
target_users.rationale: Game clients are the end consumers; platform engineers operate and tune the system

project_class.rationale: ML model for match quality scoring is the core differentiating component of the architecture

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, ai_llm, realtime, analytics, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Covers microservice backends, player data, JWT auth, ML inference, WebSocket realtime events, analytics dashboards, and nightly rating recalculation batch jobs

complexity_level.rationale: Must handle concurrent matchmaking for hundreds of thousands of players per region with sub-second lobby assembly latency

risk_level.rationale: Processes player behavioral data and skill ratings that influence in-game rewards; GDPR applies to EU players; COPPA applies to minors

data_sensitivity.rationale: Player skill ratings encode behavioral history; regional player identifiers are personal data under GDPR

external_exposure.rationale: Matchmaking API is called by authenticated game clients using short-lived JWT tokens issued by the game authentication service

access_model.value: Custom JWT signed with HMAC-SHA256; game client tokens carry player_id and region claims; service-to-service calls use separate short-lived tokens with service_name claim; no player-facing admin UI
access_model.rationale: Existing game auth infrastructure issues HMAC tokens; extending for service mesh is lowest friction path

feature_scope.value: Skill estimation microservice with Glicko-2 variant rating calculation and ML match quality scoring; lobby assembly service with pool management and team composition optimization; connection negotiation service with STUN/TURN broker integration; player rating history API; analytics dashboard for matchmaking KPIs; nightly batch rating recalculation
feature_scope.rationale: Full decomposed scope agreed by platform and game teams

mvp_scope.value: Skill estimation service with Glicko-2 ratings and basic ML quality scoring; lobby assembly for 5v5 mode in 2 regions; connection negotiation broker; parallel run against monolith with outcome comparison
mvp_scope.rationale: Parallel run validates ML quality scores before replacing monolith in production traffic

security_baseline.value: JWT validation on every service boundary, mTLS for service-to-service traffic within ECS, rate limiting on matchmaking entry endpoint (100 req/min per player_id), player data encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL via RDS encryption, GDPR consent and deletion workflow
security_baseline.rationale: Player personal data and competitive integrity requirements mandate strong auth and data controls

frontend_stack.value: None; no browser UI; game client communication is via REST and WebSocket only
frontend_stack.rationale: Matchmaking is a backend platform service consumed by the game client, not a web app

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin for lobby assembly and connection negotiation services; Python 3.12 with FastAPI for skill estimation and ML inference service
backend_stack.rationale: Go for high-throughput lobby assembly; Python for ML model serving and Glicko-2 calculation pipeline

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for persistent player skill ratings and match history; Redis 7 for live matchmaking pool state and lobby buffers; S3 for ML model artifacts and training data snapshots
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for ACID-consistent rating updates; Redis for sub-millisecond pool lookups during lobby assembly

hosting_target.value: ECS Fargate multi-region (us-east-1, eu-west-1) with services in isolated task definitions per microservice
hosting_target.rationale: Separate ECS services allow independent scaling; multi-region reduces latency and satisfies GDPR data residency for EU players

privacy_retention_policy.value: Player skill ratings retained for duration of account; match history retained 2 years; GDPR right-to-erasure must delete or anonymize all player_id references within 30 days of request; EU player data stored only in eu-west-1
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 17 erasure and data residency requirements for EU region

future_scope.value: Cross-region lobby assembly for low-population modes, ML model online learning from live match outcomes, per-game-mode skill partitioning, anti-smurf detection layer
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch roadmap items pending stable MVP

constraints.value: Lobby assembly p99 latency must remain below 800ms under full regional load; parallel run against monolith required before traffic cutover; EU player data must never leave eu-west-1
constraints.rationale: Player experience SLA and GDPR data residency are non-negotiable

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM tracing across all microservices with trace propagation headers; custom metrics for matchmaking queue depth, lobby fill time, match quality score distribution, and rating update latency; PagerDuty on queue depth spike or lobby assembly timeout rate
observability_baseline.rationale: Matchmaking quality directly impacts player experience; real-time queue metrics are essential for on-call response

execution_preference.value: Microservice-by-microservice extraction with parallel shadow traffic comparison; skill estimation service deployed first as it is read-mostly, then lobby assembly, then connection negotiation
execution_preference.rationale: Incremental extraction reduces risk; skill service is safest first target as rating reads are non-destructive

compliance_context.value: GDPR: player_id is personal data; right-to-erasure pipeline required; EU player data must reside in eu-west-1 only; DPA with AWS signed. COPPA: players under 13 cannot be matched with adult players; age bracket flag on player token required
compliance_context.rationale: GDPR and COPPA compliance mandatory for EU and global release

llm_integration.value: ML match quality scoring model (custom gradient boosting model trained on historical match outcome data, not an LLM); served via FastAPI /predict endpoint with Redis caching of per-player feature vectors; model artifacts stored in S3 and loaded at service startup
llm_integration.rationale: Match quality prediction is a structured ML task using skill features, not a generative LLM; model is proprietary and runs on internal inference infrastructure

project.goal: Decompose monolithic matchmaker into skill, lobby, and connection microservices with ML quality scoring
project.target_users: Game clients and platform engineers
project.timeline: Q3 parallel run, Q4 full cutover

backend.api: REST + WebSocket for lobby state events

security.auth: Custom JWT HMAC-SHA256; mTLS service mesh

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for ratings; Redis 7 for pool state

open_questions.smurf_detection: Is anti-smurf detection in scope for MVP?

reasoner_reviews.product: The decomposition sequence is sound: starting with skill estimation reduces risk as rating reads are idempotent and non-destructive. The parallel shadow run requirement is the right validation gate before routing production lobby traffic. COPPA age-bracket isolation must be enforced at lobby assembly time, not just at token issuance.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go for lobby assembly is the right call given the sub-800ms p99 SLA — goroutines handle concurrent pool scanning efficiently. Python FastAPI for the ML serving layer is appropriate but the feature vector Redis cache must have a configurable TTL tied to rating update frequency to prevent stale features from degrading match quality scores. Consider a circuit breaker between lobby assembly and the ML service so lobby can fall back to Elo-distance matching if the quality scoring endpoint is unavailable.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS between ECS tasks prevents lateral movement if a service is compromised. JWT validation must be performed at the ALB WAF layer AND within each service to prevent token bypass. The GDPR erasure pipeline must cascade from player_skill_ratings through match_history to Redis player feature vectors; partial erasures are a compliance gap. COPPA age bracket must be an immutable claim signed into the JWT at issuance and verified by the lobby assembly service before pool placement.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 800ms p99 lobby assembly under full regional load is achievable with Redis sorted-set pool scanning and pre-computed feature vectors. The parallel run requires a traffic splitter at the ALB level routing a shadow copy of matchmaking requests to both old and new systems for comparison without affecting live players. EU data residency constraint requires separate ECS clusters in eu-west-1 with no cross-region PostgreSQL replication of player data.

backend.service_design: Three autonomous services: SkillEstimationService (Python/FastAPI, stateless inference + rating writes), LobbyAssemblyService (Go/Gin, stateful pool management with Redis), ConnectionNegotiationService (Go/Gin, STUN/TURN broker with WebSocket session tracking). Inter-service calls use gRPC over mTLS for rating queries from lobby to skill service.
backend.api_patterns: LobbyAssembly exposes POST /v1/queue/join (enqueue player), DELETE /v1/queue/leave, WebSocket /v1/lobby/{id}/events for real-time state updates. SkillEstimation exposes POST /v1/ratings/update (post-match batch), GET /v1/ratings/{player_id}, POST /v1/quality/predict (match quality scoring). ConnectionNegotiation exposes POST /v1/sessions (create lobby session with STUN credentials).
backend.failure_handling: Circuit breaker on lobby-to-skill-service quality scoring calls: if skill service p99 latency exceeds 200ms, lobby falls back to Elo-distance assembly without ML scoring. Dead-letter queue for failed post-match rating update batches with 3-retry exponential backoff.
backend.scaling_notes: LobbyAssemblyService horizontal scaling constrained by Redis pool lock contention — each region uses a dedicated Redis cluster with keyspace partitioned by game mode. SkillEstimationService scales independently based on rating update batch queue depth. Both use ECS Fargate service auto-scaling on CPU 60% target.

security.auth_design: Game client tokens: HMAC-SHA256 JWT with player_id, region, age_bracket, and exp claims, 15-minute TTL issued by existing auth service. Service-to-service tokens: separate HMAC JWT with service_name claim and 5-minute TTL rotated via a shared secret stored in Secrets Manager. mTLS certificates managed via ACM Private CA.
security.authorization_model: LobbyAssemblyService validates age_bracket claim before pool placement — minors placed in age-restricted pools only. COPPA enforcement is a hard gate at lobby join, not a recommendation. Player data reads in SkillEstimationService scoped to player_id from token claims, preventing cross-player data access.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR erasure: DELETE /v1/players/{id}/erase cascades to player_skill_ratings, match_history_participants, and Redis feature vector cache keys; erasure jobs run synchronously for Redis and asynchronously via SQS for PostgreSQL with 30-day SLA. EU player_id data stored only in eu-west-1 RDS instance; cross-region replication explicitly disabled.
security.security_risks: Redis pool state contains player_id references; if Redis is compromised, player enumeration is possible. Mitigation: VPC-private Redis with no public endpoint; IAM-based ElastiCache auth.

data.entities: player_skill_ratings (player_id UUID PK, region, game_mode, mu FLOAT, sigma FLOAT, matches_played INT, last_updated_at, age_bracket ENUM adult/minor). match_history (match_id UUID PK, region, game_mode, assembled_at, quality_score FLOAT, outcome ENUM team_a_win/team_b_win/draw). match_history_participants (match_id FK, player_id, team ENUM a/b, performance_delta FLOAT). match_quality_scores (match_id FK, predicted_quality FLOAT, feature_vector_hash, model_version). lobby_pools (pool_id VARCHAR, region, game_mode, player_queue JSONB, created_at, expires_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ per region for player_skill_ratings and match_history; separate RDS instances in us-east-1 and eu-west-1 with no cross-region replication. Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster per region for lobby_pools and player feature vector cache (TTL 60 seconds). S3 for ML model artifacts with versioned keys.
data.analytics_events: Match assembled (match_id, quality_score, assembly_time_ms, pool_wait_time_ms, region, game_mode), Rating updated (player_id anonymized, delta, model_version), Queue abandoned (player_id anonymized, wait_time_ms, reason). Events streamed to Kinesis Firehose and landed in S3 for Redshift analytics.
data.data_risks: Feature vector staleness in Redis can degrade ML quality scores if rating updates lag. Mitigation: TTL on feature vectors tied to rating update frequency; stale cache forced-invalidation on any rating write.

devops.deployment_topology: Each microservice deployed as a separate ECS Fargate service per region. ALB in each region routes game client requests to LobbyAssemblyService. Internal ALB routes service-to-service. Shadow traffic splitter ALB rule routes 10% of production matchmaking requests to new system during parallel run phase.
devops.ci_cd_design: Buildkite pipeline: Go test + build, Python pytest + build, Docker push to ECR, ECS rolling deployment per service per region. Independent deploy pipelines per service allow skill service to be deployed without touching lobby assembly.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with distributed traces propagated via X-Amzn-Trace-Id across all three services. Custom Datadog metrics: matchmaking.queue.depth (gauge, by region/mode), matchmaking.lobby.assembly_time_ms (histogram), matchmaking.quality_score (histogram), matchmaking.circuit_breaker.state (gauge). PagerDuty on queue depth exceeding 500 players for >2 minutes or lobby assembly timeout rate >5%.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS rolling deployment allows per-service rollback by updating task definition to previous image tag. Skill service rollback is safe due to idempotent rating reads. Lobby service rollback requires draining in-flight lobbies first via graceful shutdown hook.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a decomposed matchmaking platform for a high-scale competitive shooter. The critical architectural decision is the circuit breaker between lobby assembly and the ML quality scoring service so that lobby assembly degrades gracefully to Elo-distance matching rather than timing out. GDPR erasure must cascade atomically across PostgreSQL and Redis to prevent partial erasures. COPPA age-bracket enforcement must be a hard JWT claim gate at lobby join, not a soft recommendation, to satisfy compliance requirements.
target_output.title: AI Matchmaking Microservices Platform for Competitive Online Shooter
