target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale microservices decomposition of a monolithic matchmaker into three independently deployable services — SkillEstimationService (Python/FastAPI), LobbyAssemblyService (Go/Gin), and ConnectionNegotiationService (Go/Gin) — with a gradient-boosting ML model predicting match quality scores to minimize skill imbalance and wait time. Deployed on ECS Fargate in us-east-1 and eu-west-1 with Redis pool state, GDPR-compliant erasure, and COPPA age-bracket isolation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-service architecture on ECS Fargate with each service independently scalable and deployable. Game clients authenticate with HMAC-JWT and connect to LobbyAssemblyService via ALB. Lobby service scans Redis sorted-set player pools by skill rating proximity, calls SkillEstimationService via gRPC for ML-predicted match quality scores, and assembles optimal team compositions within the 800ms p99 SLA. A circuit breaker on the ML quality call falls back to pure Elo-distance matching if SkillEstimation exceeds 200ms p99. ConnectionNegotiationService issues STUN/TURN credentials and manages WebSocket session state for assembled lobbies. Post-match telemetry drives nightly Glicko-2 batch recalculations that update player_skill_ratings in PostgreSQL. GDPR erasure pipeline cascades from PostgreSQL through Redis feature vectors via SQS. EU player data confined to eu-west-1 with no cross-region replication.
target_output.technology_stack: SkillEstimationService: Python 3.12, FastAPI, scikit-learn gradient boosting model, Redis feature vector cache. LobbyAssemblyService: Go 1.22, Gin, gRPC client (grpc-go), Redis 7 sorted sets for pool state. ConnectionNegotiationService: Go 1.22, Gin, gorilla/websocket, coturn STUN/TURN integration. Data: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ per region, Redis 7 ElastiCache per region, S3 for model artifacts. Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB with WAF, ACM Private CA for mTLS, Secrets Manager. Observability: Datadog APM, Kinesis Firehose, Redshift. CI/CD: Buildkite.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Skill Estimation: Glicko-2 variant rating storage per player per game mode per region; ML match quality predictor using 14-feature vector (mu, sigma, game_mode, region, time_of_day, recent_win_rate, disconnect_rate); POST /v1/ratings/update for post-match batch updates; GET /v1/ratings/{player_id} for lobby read. Lobby Assembly: player queue management in Redis sorted sets keyed by skill rating; team composition optimizer for 5v5 and 10v10 modes; ML quality threshold gate (reject lobbies below 0.65 quality score); WebSocket push for lobby_formed and player_added events. Connection Negotiation: STUN credential issuance with 5-minute TTL; WebSocket session tracker for lobby readiness signals; lobby timeout handler at 120 seconds. Analytics: match quality score distribution, assembly time histograms, queue abandonment rate by region and mode.
target_output.system_components: ALB with WAF (external game client traffic per region). SkillEstimationService ECS tasks (Python/FastAPI, stateless, scales on CPU). LobbyAssemblyService ECS tasks (Go/Gin, scales on custom metric queue_depth). ConnectionNegotiationService ECS tasks (Go/Gin, scales on active WebSocket connections). Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster per region (pool state and feature vector cache). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ per region (player_skill_ratings, match_history). S3 buckets for ML model artifacts and match telemetry archive. SQS queues for post-match rating update batches and GDPR erasure jobs. Kinesis Firehose for analytics event streaming. ACM Private CA issuing mTLS certificates for inter-service communication.
target_output.workflows: Lobby Assembly: game client sends POST /v1/queue/join with HMAC-JWT; LobbyAssemblyService validates token, extracts age_bracket claim for COPPA pool routing, adds player to Redis sorted set keyed by game_mode and age_bracket; background goroutines continuously scan pool for candidate groups within skill sigma threshold; candidate lobby sent to SkillEstimationService POST /v1/quality/predict; if quality_score >= 0.65 lobby is formed and ConnectionNegotiationService is called for STUN credentials; WebSocket push notifies all players. Post-Match Rating Update: game server posts match outcome and per-player performance deltas to SQS; nightly batch job dequeues, computes Glicko-2 rating updates using new_mu and new_sigma, writes to player_skill_ratings in PostgreSQL, invalidates player feature vector keys in Redis. GDPR Erasure: player submits deletion request; erasure job synchronously deletes Redis feature vector cache keys for player_id, then enqueues SQS message for PostgreSQL cascade (player_skill_ratings anonymize player_id to anonymous_{uuid}, match_history_participants soft-delete) within 30-day SLA.
target_output.data_model: player_skill_ratings (player_id UUID PK, region VARCHAR, game_mode VARCHAR, mu FLOAT NOT NULL, sigma FLOAT NOT NULL, matches_played INT, last_rating_update_at TIMESTAMPTZ, age_bracket ENUM adult/minor, erased BOOLEAN DEFAULT false). match_history (match_id UUID PK, region, game_mode, assembled_at, quality_score FLOAT, team_a_players UUID[], team_b_players UUID[], outcome ENUM team_a_win/team_b_win/draw, assembly_time_ms INT). match_quality_scores (match_id UUID FK, predicted_quality FLOAT, feature_vector_hash VARCHAR, model_version VARCHAR, scored_at). lobby_pools Redis key pattern: pool:{region}:{game_mode}:{age_bracket} sorted set with player_id members scored by mu. player_feature_vectors Redis hash: feature:{player_id} with TTL 60 seconds, fields: mu, sigma, recent_win_rate, disconnect_rate, region, age_bracket.
target_output.api_design: LobbyAssemblyService: POST /v1/queue/join {game_mode, region} — enqueue player, returns queue_position. DELETE /v1/queue/leave — dequeue player. GET /v1/queue/status — current wait estimate. WS /v1/lobby/{lobby_id}/events — push lobby_formed, player_added, lobby_cancelled events. SkillEstimationService: GET /v1/ratings/{player_id}?game_mode={mode}&region={region} — returns mu, sigma, matches_played. POST /v1/quality/predict {players: [{player_id, team}], game_mode, region} — returns quality_score FLOAT, confidence FLOAT. POST /v1/ratings/update (internal SQS trigger) — processes batch rating updates. ConnectionNegotiationService: POST /v1/sessions {lobby_id, player_ids[]} — returns STUN credentials per player. WS /v1/sessions/{lobby_id}/ready — readiness signaling. All endpoints require Authorization: Bearer {jwt}; rate limit 100 req/min per player_id at ALB WAF.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Authentication: HMAC-SHA256 JWT validated at ALB WAF and inside each service handler; tokens carry player_id, region, age_bracket, exp claims. Service mesh: mTLS via ACM Private CA certificates for all ECS inter-service gRPC calls. COPPA: age_bracket claim is an immutable signed JWT field; LobbyAssemblyService rejects join requests from minor-flagged players to adult pools at the Redis insertion step. GDPR: erasure pipeline replaces player_id with anonymous_{uuid} in PostgreSQL and deletes Redis keys synchronously; EU player data stored only in eu-west-1 RDS with cross-region replication disabled; GDPR consent recorded at account creation before any rating data is stored. Secrets: ML model signing key, JWT shared secrets, and DB credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with 30-day rotation. Network: RDS and ElastiCache in private VPC subnets with no public endpoints.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Three independent ECS Fargate services per region (us-east-1, eu-west-1), each with their own task definition, ECR repository, and Buildkite pipeline. ALB listener rules: external player traffic to LobbyAssemblyService, internal ALB for service-to-service gRPC. During parallel run phase, shadow ALB rule routes 10% of production matchmaking requests to new system; match quality and assembly time metrics compared against monolith in Datadog. Graceful shutdown: LobbyAssemblyService drains in-flight lobbies on SIGTERM before ECS marks task unhealthy. ECS auto-scaling: LobbyAssembly on matchmaking.queue.depth metric; SkillEstimation on CPU 60% target. RDS Multi-AZ failover automatic with 60-second RTO. Redis ElastiCache Multi-AZ with automatic failover. Rollback: update ECS task definition to previous image tag per service; lobby service rollback requires pre-drain.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM distributed traces across all three services with X-Amzn-Trace-Id propagation; spans capture queue.join, quality.predict, lobby.assemble, and session.create operations. Custom metrics emitted to Datadog: matchmaking.queue.depth (gauge by region and game_mode), matchmaking.lobby.assembly_time_ms (histogram), matchmaking.quality_score.distribution (histogram), matchmaking.circuit_breaker.state (gauge, 0=closed/1=open). Kinesis Firehose delivers analytics events to S3 and Redshift for post-match quality analysis and model performance tracking. PagerDuty alerts: queue depth >500 for 2 minutes, lobby assembly timeout rate >5%, SkillEstimation p99 latency >300ms, circuit breaker open for >5 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate per-region: LobbyAssembly 2-10 tasks ($300-1500/month), SkillEstimation 1-4 tasks ($150-600/month), ConnectionNegotiation 1-6 tasks ($150-900/month). RDS Multi-AZ per region: db.r6g.large ($400/month per region). ElastiCache r6g.large per region ($250/month). Shadow traffic comparison phase adds ~15% cost overhead for 4-week parallel run window. ML model retraining on SageMaker estimated $200/month for weekly batch jobs. Total per-region operational cost: ~$1500/month at mid-scale, scaling linearly with player pool size.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Skill Estimation Extract (6 weeks): Deploy SkillEstimationService with Glicko-2 ratings and ML quality predictor in us-east-1 dev. Train initial quality model on 6 months of historical match outcome data. Verify rating parity with monolith on shadow reads. Deploy to staging and production in read-only mode alongside monolith. Phase 2 — Lobby Assembly Extract (6 weeks): Deploy LobbyAssemblyService connecting to SkillEstimationService. Run 10% shadow traffic split in us-east-1 production. Compare assembly time and quality score distributions against monolith. Tune circuit breaker thresholds. Expand to 100% traffic in us-east-1. Phase 3 — Connection and EU Region (4 weeks): Deploy ConnectionNegotiationService. Validate COPPA pool isolation in staging with synthetic minor-flagged test accounts. Deploy all three services in eu-west-1 with separate RDS instance. Verify GDPR erasure pipeline end-to-end. Decommission monolith matchmaker. Phase 4 — Additional Modes and Rating Refinement (ongoing): Extend skill partitioning to remaining game modes, implement online model learning from live match outcomes.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go services use standard library HTTP and grpc-go; no framework magic for request routing. Python service uses FastAPI dependency injection for JWT validation; model loaded once at startup and cached in memory. Buildkite pipeline enforces Go test with race detector enabled, Python pytest with >80% coverage on rating calculation logic. All service-to-service gRPC calls include a deadline of 200ms. Feature vector cache keys must include model_version suffix so model rollout invalidates stale features. No player_id values in application logs — use anonymized match_id for log correlation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ML quality scoring service outage during lobby assembly causes assembly failures. Mitigation: circuit breaker falls back to Elo-distance assembly; fallback path tested weekly via chaos engineering. Risk: Redis pool state lost on ElastiCache node failure causes in-flight queue entries to be dropped. Mitigation: players receive WebSocket disconnect event and are instructed to re-queue; ElastiCache Multi-AZ reduces failure window. Tradeoff: gRPC between lobby and skill service over REST reduces serialization overhead at high throughput but requires mTLS certificate rotation management. Tradeoff: per-region PostgreSQL instances (no cross-region replication) eliminate cross-region data egress risk for GDPR compliance but require separate schema migrations per region to be coordinated in CI.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Glicko-2 variant over TrueSkill for rating algorithm due to open-source availability and simpler implementation without license constraints. Selected Redis sorted sets over PostgreSQL for live pool state because sorted set ZRANGEBYSCORE scans are O(log N + M) and fit within 800ms p99 SLA. COPPA enforcement placed at lobby join rather than token validation layer so each service independently enforces age-bracket isolation without depending on upstream service configuration. Anti-smurf detection deferred to post-launch roadmap pending live data collection.

metadata.notes: High-scale matchmaking decomposition with ML quality scoring, GDPR erasure, and COPPA age-bracket isolation.

profile.domain: Game Key Distribution and License Management

project_goal.value: Secure game key distribution and license management platform for indie publishers to sell and revoke digital game keys across storefronts and bundles, with fraud detection for key reselling abuse and secure key redemption flows.
project_goal.rationale: Core business need for indie publishers managing multi-storefront key inventory

target_users.value: Indie game publishers (primary), their end customers redeeming keys, and internal ops staff managing fraud cases
target_users.rationale: Three user personas with distinct workflows identified in discovery

project_class.rationale: Publisher dashboard, admin panel, and public redemption API all require both frontend and backend

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, payments, analytics, admin_panel, public_api
capabilities.rationale: Full capability surface required for publisher billing, key management, and fraud analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Cryptographic key generation, fraud detection rules, Stripe billing, and publisher API surface constitute advanced complexity

risk_level.rationale: Financial transactions and key theft/reselling fraud represent high operational and financial risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: Publisher payouts and payout reports contain financial data; key redemption may be linked to payment identity

external_exposure.rationale: Key redemption endpoint is publicly accessible; publisher dashboard authenticated but internet-facing

access_model.value: Custom JWT with TOTP MFA for publishers; API key for storefront integrations; internal admin role for ops team; anonymous for public key redemption endpoint
access_model.rationale: Publishers need MFA for sensitive key batch operations; storefronts need API keys for automated distribution

feature_scope.value: Game license definition, cryptographic key batch generation, key export as CSV, storefront API for distribution partners, key redemption with fraud checking, key revocation, publisher analytics dashboard, fraud case review admin panel, payout reports
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for launch covering all publisher and ops workflows

mvp_scope.value: Single publisher onboarding, key batch generation and CSV export, manual key redemption, IP and velocity-based fraud flagging, basic publisher analytics
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for first publisher cohort

security_baseline.value: AES-256 encrypted key storage at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, TOTP MFA for publisher accounts, rate limiting on redemption endpoint, HMAC-signed API keys for storefront integrations, append-only redemption audit log
security_baseline.rationale: Key theft and reselling fraud demand strong cryptographic controls and audit trails

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for fast publisher dashboard initial load; React for dynamic key management tables

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: Python team expertise; FastAPI for async I/O under batch generation load

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with Redis 7
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for key inventory and audit records; Redis for redemption rate limiting and fraud rule evaluation cache

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on Hetzner
hosting_target.rationale: Cost-sensitive indie publisher audience requires low-overhead hosting; publisher prefers European data residency

privacy_retention_policy.value: Customer redemption records retained 7 years for financial regulations; publisher account data deleted on account closure; fraud signals anonymized after 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial record retention obligations with GDPR-compliant publisher data deletion

future_scope.value: Automated storefront API integrations (GOG, Humble), bundle management module, publisher revenue share analytics, advanced ML-based fraud scoring
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap for platform growth

constraints.value: Key generation must produce cryptographically random tokens (CSPRNG); bare-metal Kubernetes limits managed service use; no third-party fraud-as-a-service to keep customer data on-premises; key export CSV must be downloadable only once per batch
constraints.rationale: Security requirements for key randomness and data sovereignty constraints from publisher

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus with custom metrics for key redemption rate, fraud flag rate, and batch generation duration; Sentry for application errors
observability_baseline.rationale: Self-hosted observability stack aligned with Hetzner bare-metal deployment philosophy

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with security review gates before each new capability ships
execution_preference.rationale: Security-first delivery pace appropriate for financial and key distribution platform

compliance_context.value: GDPR for EU customer data in redemption records; PCI-DSS scoping review for publisher billing flows; SOC 2 Type II target for publisher trust; financial record retention obligations
compliance_context.rationale: Financial data sensitivity and publisher trust requirements trigger compliance obligations

project.goal: Build a secure key distribution and fraud-detection platform for indie game publishers
project.target_users: Publishers, end-customer redeemers, ops fraud reviewers
project.timeline: Beta in 10 weeks, launch in 18 weeks

security.auth: Custom JWT with TOTP MFA
security.keys: CSPRNG generation; AES-256 encrypted at rest

data.cache: Redis 7 for rate limiting and fraud evaluation cache

reasoner_reviews.product: The platform scope is tightly focused on the key distribution problem. One-time CSV download is a good security constraint that prevents bulk key exposure via repeated downloads. The fraud flagging system should be rule-based for MVP with enough hooks for ML scoring in Phase 2.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: CSPRNG key generation using Python secrets.token_hex(16) with AES-256 encryption at rest before PostgreSQL storage is the correct approach. Redis token bucket for redemption rate limiting is simple and effective at Hetzner scale. The append-only audit log should use a PostgreSQL trigger to prevent DELETE operations rather than relying on application-layer enforcement alone.
reasoner_reviews.security: TOTP MFA for publisher accounts must be enforced on key batch generation and CSV download operations specifically, not just login. API keys for storefront integrations should be HMAC-SHA256 signed and stored only as their hash in the database -- never in plaintext. One-time CSV download must be enforced via a download_token with single-use TTL in Redis.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Bare-metal Kubernetes on Hetzner constrains managed database services. Recommend self-managed PostgreSQL 16 with streaming replication to a standby node, Patroni for automatic failover, and daily logical backups to Hetzner Object Storage. No pgbouncer required at this scale but connection limits should be configured conservatively.

backend.service_design: FastAPI monolith with domain routers: /licenses (game license CRUD), /batches (key generation, export), /redemption (public key redemption endpoint), /fraud (case management, rule evaluation), /publishers (account management, API key issuance), /admin (ops fraud review and override)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful at /api/v1 with OpenAPI 3.0; rate limiting on /redemption via Redis token bucket (10 req/min per IP); HMAC-signed API keys for storefront integrations authenticated via Authorization: ApiKey header
backend.business_modules: Key generation: secrets.token_hex(16) per key, AES-256-GCM encrypted with per-publisher key derived from master key via HKDF before PostgreSQL INSERT; batch CSV assembled in memory and written to one-time Redis download token (TTL 300s). Fraud evaluation: rules engine checks IP velocity (> 5 redemptions per IP per hour), device fingerprint mismatch, account age < 7 days; flagged redemptions inserted as fraud_signals with hold status.
backend.failure_handling: Key generation wrapped in PostgreSQL transaction; on partial batch failure, entire batch rolled back and error logged. Download token expiry returns 410 Gone. Fraud hold queue persists independently of redemption requests.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js App Router: server components for publisher dashboard static frames; client components for key table with pagination and batch actions
frontend.pages_and_flows: Publisher login with TOTP MFA, license management, batch generation wizard, batch export (single-use download), redemption analytics, fraud alert inbox. Admin: fraud case list, case detail with IP/device context, override actions.
frontend.state_management: TanStack Query for license, batch, and fraud case data; optimistic mutations for batch status updates

security.auth_design: Custom JWT (HS256, 1-hour access token, 7-day refresh token); TOTP MFA enforced on batch generation, CSV download, and API key rotation actions; MFA enrollment required during publisher onboarding
security.authorization_model: Publisher: CRUD on own licenses and batches, read own analytics; Storefront API Key: POST /redemption only; Admin: read all fraud cases, write override decisions; no cross-publisher data access
security.secrets_management: Master AES key stored in Kubernetes Secret encrypted with Sealed Secrets operator; PostgreSQL credentials in Kubernetes Secret; TOTP seed encrypted with per-publisher AES key at storage
security.abuse_prevention: Redemption endpoint: 10 req/min per IP via Redis token bucket; 5 failed redemptions per IP per 10 minutes triggers 1-hour block; key IDs not sequentially guessable (random UUID v4 as internal reference, encrypted token as external key value)

data.entities: game_licenses (id UUID PK, publisher_id FK, title TEXT, sku TEXT, platform ENUM steam/gog/humble/direct, active BOOL, created_at). key_batches (id UUID PK, license_id FK, batch_size INT, generated_count INT, download_token_hash TEXT, downloaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM generating/ready/exported/revoked, created_at). game_keys (id UUID PK, batch_id FK, key_token_encrypted BYTEA, key_hash TEXT UNIQUE, status ENUM unredeemed/redeemed/revoked, redeemed_at, redemption_record_id FK). redemption_records (id UUID PK, key_id FK, redeemed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ip_hash TEXT, device_fingerprint_hash TEXT, fraud_status ENUM clean/flagged/reviewed_clean/confirmed_fraud). fraud_signals (id UUID PK, redemption_record_id FK, rule_triggered TEXT, severity ENUM low/medium/high, status ENUM hold/cleared/escalated, reviewer_id FK, resolved_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with streaming replication to hot standby; Patroni for automatic failover; daily pg_dump to Hetzner Object Storage S3-compatible API. Redis 7 for rate limiting counters and one-time download tokens (TTL 300s).
data.retention_and_deletion: redemption_records and fraud_signals retained 7 years; publisher account data deleted within 30 days of account closure; fraud IP and device hashes anonymized after 2 years per privacy policy

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster on Hetzner: 3-node control plane, 4 worker nodes (cx51 class). Namespaces: production, staging, monitoring. Services: fastify-api Deployment (3 replicas), next-app Deployment (2 replicas), PostgreSQL StatefulSet with Patroni, Redis Deployment (single node dev / Sentinel 3-node prod).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: lint (ruff + mypy), pytest (80% coverage gate), Docker build, push to Hetzner Registry, kubectl rollout with canary via Argo Rollouts. Migration handled by Alembic applied as Kubernetes Job before rollout.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping FastAPI /metrics endpoint (via prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator); custom metrics: key_redemptions_total, fraud_flags_total, batch_generation_duration_seconds, download_token_consumed_total; Grafana dashboards per metric family; Sentry for unhandled FastAPI exceptions
devops.rollback_strategy: Argo Rollouts canary at 20% traffic for 10 minutes; auto-rollback on error rate > 1%; GitLab pipeline stores previous image tag for manual one-command rollback

target_output.thinking_summary: Security-hardening focused key distribution platform. Critical decisions: AES-256-GCM with HKDF-derived per-publisher keys prevents bulk decryption if DB is compromised; single-use Redis download tokens enforce one-time CSV export at infrastructure level; PostgreSQL trigger-enforced append-only audit log prevents application-layer tampering; Patroni on Hetzner replaces managed database since no managed PG available on bare-metal deployment target.
target_output.title: Game Key Distribution and License Management Platform Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A security-hardened fullstack platform for indie game publishers to generate, distribute, and manage digital game keys with fraud detection and one-time export controls. Built with Next.js 14 and Python FastAPI on bare-metal Kubernetes (Hetzner), using AES-256-GCM encrypted key storage, TOTP MFA for sensitive publisher operations, rule-based fraud detection with IP and velocity signals, and a Patroni-managed PostgreSQL cluster with append-only audit logging. Designed for European data residency with GDPR-compliant redemption record handling.
target_output.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 frontend serves publisher dashboard and admin panel via server-side rendering with client-side TanStack Query data fetching. FastAPI backend provides RESTful APIs for license management, key batch operations, and fraud case workflows, plus a public key redemption endpoint protected by Redis rate limiting. PostgreSQL 16 with Patroni automatic failover stores the encrypted key inventory, redemption records, and fraud signals. Redis 7 handles redemption rate limiting token buckets and one-time batch download tokens. All communication is TLS 1.3. Deployed on bare-metal Kubernetes on Hetzner with Argo Rollouts canary deployments. Observability via Prometheus and Grafana with Sentry for error tracking.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, TanStack Query. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Pydantic v2, SQLAlchemy (async), prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator, pyotp (TOTP). Database: PostgreSQL 16 with Patroni (3-node HA) and Alembic migrations. Cache: Redis 7 Sentinel cluster (3 nodes production). Auth: Custom HS256 JWT with TOTP enforced on sensitive operations; Sealed Secrets for Kubernetes secret management. Infrastructure: Bare-metal Kubernetes on Hetzner, Argo Rollouts, Hetzner Object Storage (S3-compatible), Hetzner Registry. CI: GitLab CI. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, Sentry.
target_output.functional_feature_map: License Management: publishers define game SKUs per platform (Steam, GOG, Humble, Direct) with activation rules. Batch Key Generation: CSPRNG token generation with AES-256-GCM encryption per publisher key; batch sizes up to 100k keys with async generation job. One-Time CSV Export: batch download via single-use Redis token (TTL 300s, consumed on first GET). Storefront API Key Issuance: HMAC-SHA256 signed API keys for automated distribution partner integration stored as hash only. Public Key Redemption: stateless redemption endpoint with IP/velocity fraud evaluation and hold queue. Fraud Case Management: admin panel for reviewing flagged redemptions with IP geolocation context, device fingerprint matches, and override decisions. Publisher Analytics: redemption rate, fraud flag rate, batch utilization, revenue by SKU. Payout Reports: CSV export of redemption counts per SKU for publisher financial reconciliation.
target_output.system_components: Next.js App (2 Kubernetes replicas): publisher dashboard, admin panel, server-side rendering. FastAPI API (3 replicas): all REST endpoints including public redemption. PostgreSQL 16 (Patroni StatefulSet 3 nodes: 1 primary + 2 replicas): game_licenses, key_batches, game_keys, redemption_records, fraud_signals, publisher_accounts, audit_log. Redis 7 (Sentinel 3-node): rate limiting token buckets, one-time download tokens, fraud evaluation cache. Hetzner Object Storage: daily PostgreSQL logical backups, batch export audit records. Argo Rollouts: canary traffic shifting for API deployments. Prometheus + Grafana: metrics and dashboards. Sentry: exception tracking.
target_output.workflows: Key Batch Generation: publisher authenticates with JWT + TOTP step-up, submits POST /api/v1/batches with license_id and batch_size; FastAPI enqueues an async generation task (asyncio background task); each key token generated via secrets.token_hex(16), encrypted with AES-256-GCM using a per-publisher key derived via HKDF from the master key, bulk-inserted into game_keys table in 1000-row chunks within a transaction; on completion batch status=ready, a one-time download_token generated and its hash stored; publisher receives 201 with download_url containing signed token. One-Time CSV Download: publisher GETs the download URL; FastAPI validates token against Redis (GETDEL for atomic consume); if token exists, decrypts game_keys rows for the batch, assembles CSV in memory using Python csv module, streams response with Content-Disposition: attachment; subsequent requests to same URL return 410 Gone. Key Redemption with Fraud Gate: customer POSTs /api/v1/redeem with {key: '...'} from storefront or direct; FastAPI hashes the key, looks up game_keys by key_hash; if not found returns 404; fraud rules evaluated: IP hit count in Redis INCR with 1-hour TTL, device fingerprint collision check, account age if authenticated; rules pass: redemption_records row inserted, key status=redeemed, 200 OK with license activation payload; rules flag: redemption_records inserted with fraud_status=flagged, fraud_signals row created, 202 Accepted with 'activation pending review' message.
target_output.data_model: publisher_accounts (id UUID PK, email TEXT UNIQUE, password_hash TEXT, totp_secret_encrypted BYTEA, api_key_hash TEXT, mfa_enrolled BOOL, created_at). game_licenses (id UUID PK, publisher_id FK, title TEXT, sku TEXT, platform ENUM steam/gog/humble/direct, active BOOL, created_at). key_batches (id UUID PK, license_id FK, batch_size INT, generated_count INT, download_token_hash TEXT, downloaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM generating/ready/exported/revoked, created_at). game_keys (id UUID PK, batch_id FK, key_token_encrypted BYTEA NOT NULL, key_hash TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL, status ENUM unredeemed/redeemed/revoked, redeemed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, redemption_record_id FK). redemption_records (id UUID PK, key_id FK, redeemed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ip_hash TEXT, device_fingerprint_hash TEXT, fraud_status ENUM clean/flagged/reviewed_clean/confirmed_fraud, reviewed_by FK, reviewed_at). fraud_signals (id UUID PK, redemption_record_id FK, rule_triggered TEXT, severity ENUM low/medium/high, status ENUM hold/cleared/escalated, reviewer_id FK, resolved_at). audit_log (id BIGSERIAL PK, actor_id UUID, action TEXT NOT NULL, resource_type TEXT, resource_id UUID, details_json JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()) -- append-only enforced via PostgreSQL rule preventing DELETE and UPDATE.
target_output.api_design: REST at /api/v1. Publisher endpoints (JWT + TOTP for sensitive ops): POST /publishers/register, POST /publishers/mfa/enroll, POST /licenses, GET /licenses, POST /batches (TOTP required), GET /batches/:id/status, GET /batches/:id/download?token=... (single-use), GET /analytics/redemptions (date range, SKU filter), GET /analytics/fraud-summary. Storefront API (ApiKey header): POST /redemption (public, rate limited 10/min/IP). Admin endpoints (admin role JWT): GET /fraud/cases?status=hold, GET /fraud/cases/:id (IP geo, device context), POST /fraud/cases/:id/resolve {action: clear|escalate|confirm_fraud}, POST /keys/:id/revoke. Webhook not applicable -- redemption is synchronous. All 4xx and 5xx responses follow RFC 7807 Problem Details JSON.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Key material security: AES-256-GCM with per-publisher HKDF-derived key from master key stored in Kubernetes Sealed Secret; batch keys bulk-inserted in 1000-row transactions; key_hash (SHA-256 of plaintext token) used for lookup without decryption. TOTP: pyotp with RFC 6238 TOTP enforced on batch generation, CSV download, and API key rotation actions. API keys: secrets.token_hex(32) generated on issuance, immediately hashed with SHA-256 and hash stored only; plaintext shown once in response. Audit log: PostgreSQL rule CREATE RULE no_delete_audit AS ON DELETE TO audit_log DO INSTEAD NOTHING; application layer also restricted. Rate limiting: Redis token bucket at 10 req/min per IP with 5-strike 1-hour block on failed redemptions. GDPR: redemption IP and device fingerprint stored as SHA-256 hashes (pseudonymization); erasure anonymizes hash fields to NULL after 2 years. PCI scope minimization: no primary card data stored; publisher payouts via Stripe Connect or manual bank transfer keep card data off platform. SOC 2 target: audit log and access controls documented in trust service criteria mapping.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Bare-metal Kubernetes on Hetzner (3 cx51 worker nodes, 3 cpx31 control plane nodes). FastAPI runs as Deployment with 3 replicas, HPA on CPU 70%. Next.js runs as Deployment with 2 replicas. PostgreSQL runs as StatefulSet with Patroni sidecar, 3 nodes (cx51 with 80GB NVMe SSD), Patroni HAProxy service for primary routing. Redis runs as 3-node Sentinel cluster. Namespaces: production, staging, monitoring. GitLab CI: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest (80% coverage gate with coverage.py), Docker build, Hetzner Registry push, kubectl set image then Argo Rollouts canary (20% for 10 minutes). Alembic migrations run as Kubernetes Job before rollout. Daily pg_dump to Hetzner Object Storage via CronJob. Secrets managed via Sealed Secrets controller.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes FastAPI /metrics via prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator. Custom Prometheus metrics: key_redemptions_total (counter, labels: license_id, fraud_status), fraud_flags_total (counter, labels: rule_triggered), batch_generation_duration_seconds (histogram), download_token_consumed_total (counter), download_token_expired_total (counter), api_key_auth_failures_total (counter). Grafana dashboards: redemption rate and fraud rate by publisher, batch generation performance, PostgreSQL replication lag, Redis memory utilization. Sentry captures FastAPI unhandled exceptions with release version tag. Alertmanager rules: PostgreSQL replica lag > 30 seconds, fraud flag rate > 10% of redemptions, batch generation > 60 seconds for batches < 10k keys.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated €1,400/month on Hetzner: 4 cx51 worker nodes ~€640, 3 cpx31 control plane ~€210, Hetzner Object Storage ~€50, Hetzner Registry ~€5, Redis and monitoring overhead ~€100, miscellaneous ~€395. Versus AWS equivalent (~€4,800/month) the bare-metal choice saves ~70% for European data residency. Scales to €2,200/month at 500k keys/day throughput by adding 2 worker nodes. PostgreSQL handles 100k key lookups per day on a single primary; at 1M/day consider partitioning game_keys by batch_id.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Beta (10 weeks): publisher registration with TOTP MFA, game license CRUD, CSPRNG key batch generation up to 10k keys, one-time CSV download with Redis token, public redemption endpoint with IP/velocity fraud rules, basic redemption analytics, Patroni PostgreSQL HA, GitLab CI pipeline, Grafana observability. Phase 2 Launch (8 weeks): storefront API key issuance for automated distribution, fraud case admin panel with override workflows, payout reports CSV export, batch sizes up to 100k keys with background generation, Argo Rollouts canary deployments, GDPR erasure tooling, SOC 2 control documentation. Phase 3 Expansion (6 weeks): GOG and Humble automated integration, bundle management module grouping multiple licenses, ML fraud scoring model replacing rule engine, publisher revenue analytics dashboard.
target_output.development_guidelines: All key material handling (generation, encryption, decryption) isolated to KeyVaultService module with 100% unit test coverage. TOTP verification tested with pyotp time-travel fixtures. PostgreSQL audit_log rule verified in integration tests by asserting DELETE raises no-op. FastAPI routes type-annotated with Pydantic v2 models -- no Dict[str, Any] in route signatures. ruff and mypy enforced in CI with zero-warning policy. All fraud rule logic unit-tested against synthetic redemption fixtures covering each rule trigger condition. Feature flags (LaunchDarkly or environment-variable-based for self-hosted) gate storefront API and bundle features.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Patroni failover causes up to 30-second PostgreSQL primary unavailability during node failure, blocking key generation and redemption. Mitigation: redemption endpoint returns 503 with Retry-After header; publishers notified via status page. Risk: one-time download token loss (browser crash before CSV is saved) leaves publisher unable to re-download batch. Mitigation: ops admin endpoint to re-issue download token with audit log entry and TOTP re-authentication required. Tradeoff: AES-256-GCM encryption of every key row increases storage ~50% and adds ~1ms CPU overhead per key decryption during export; accepted because it prevents bulk plaintext exposure from a database dump. Tradeoff: bare-metal Kubernetes adds operational burden (Patroni management, Sealed Secrets) versus managed cloud but saves ~70% infrastructure cost for cost-sensitive indie publisher market.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Key storage encryption: AES-256-GCM with HKDF per-publisher key derivation from Kubernetes Sealed Secret master key -- resolved. One-time download enforcement: Redis GETDEL atomic consume with 300-second TTL -- resolved. Fraud service data residency: on-premises rule engine with no third-party SaaS -- resolved per constraint. PostgreSQL HA on bare-metal: Patroni StatefulSet with HAProxy primary routing and daily logical backups to Hetzner Object Storage -- resolved.

metadata.notes: First-pass game key distribution platform with AES-256-GCM key encryption, TOTP MFA, one-time CSV download tokens, and Patroni HA on Hetzner bare-metal.

project_goal.value: Internal web platform for player moderation and trust-and-safety operations enabling moderators to review player reports, manage chat ban appeals, and coordinate cross-game account suspensions with immutable audit trails
project_goal.rationale: Replaces disparate spreadsheet-based moderation workflow

target_users.value: Trust-and-safety moderators, senior moderators, compliance reviewers, and legal team members within the game studio
target_users.rationale: Internal staff only; no public player-facing access

project_class.rationale: Internal web admin application with complex RBAC and audit trail requirements

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Full internal web app with admin workflows, reporting, and role-based access

complexity_level.rationale: Complex RBAC, cross-game ban coordination, and immutable audit trails add architectural complexity

risk_level.rationale: Processes player personal data and behavioral records; unauthorized access would expose PII and create legal liability

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores player reports, chat transcripts, appeal submissions, and moderation decisions affecting real individuals

external_exposure.rationale: Accessible only via corporate VPN; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: Azure AD SAML SSO with MFA enforced; RBAC with roles: moderator, senior_moderator, compliance_reviewer, legal_admin; all actions scoped by role capabilities
access_model.rationale: GDPR and internal policy require strict access controls with MFA for all staff accessing PII

feature_scope.value: Player report queue with auto-severity scoring, case management with evidence attachment, ban record management, cross-game account suspension coordination, chat ban appeals workflow, compliance reporting dashboard, role-based action audit trail
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature set for trust-and-safety operations

mvp_scope.value: Player report queue, basic case management with sanction actions, ban record tracking, and audit log for all moderator actions
mvp_scope.rationale: Core moderation workflow before appeal and cross-game features

security_baseline.value: Azure AD MFA enforced for all accounts; TLS 1.3; all PHI/PII fields encrypted at rest; append-only audit_log_entries table; VPN-only access enforced at network layer
security_baseline.rationale: Internal tool handling personal data requires defense-in-depth

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for fast admin UI load; TypeScript for type-safe form handling in complex case workflows

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async performance for concurrent moderator sessions; Pydantic validation for strict input sanitization

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server; Redis 7 on Azure Cache for Redis for session cache
data_platform.rationale: Relational integrity for case relationships; Redis for moderator session state

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) in West Europe with VPN gateway restricting ingress
hosting_target.rationale: AKS for container orchestration; West Europe for GDPR data residency

privacy_retention_policy.value: Player report and case data retained 5 years per legal hold policy; moderation decisions retained permanently for appeal purposes; data related to minors purged on request per GDPR Article 8
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Legal hold requirements and GDPR data subject rights

future_scope.value: ML-based report severity auto-classification, automated cross-game ban propagation API, player-facing appeal status portal
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP automation roadmap

constraints.value: Must integrate with existing game backend ban API (REST webhook); deployment to AKS only; no third-party SaaS tools storing player PII
constraints.rationale: Existing infrastructure and data sovereignty constraints

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor and Application Insights for APM; structured JSON logging to Log Analytics workspace; alert on audit_log write failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Audit log integrity is a compliance requirement; write failures must page on-call

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints on Azure DevOps; staging deploy on PR merge; production deploy with approval gate
execution_preference.rationale: Internal tooling deployment cadence

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 5 (data minimization, purpose limitation for moderation data); GDPR Article 17 (right to erasure for minors); internal data classification policy for personal behavioral data; legal hold obligations for active investigations
compliance_context.rationale: Processing player personal and behavioral data with minors exposure requires GDPR compliance controls

project.goal: Internal trust-and-safety moderation platform for player reports, bans, and appeals
project.target_users: Studio moderators and compliance staff
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks, full platform in 18 weeks

frontend.state: React Query for API data, Zustand for active case UI state
frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with internal design tokens

backend.framework: FastAPI with Pydantic v2 for request validation
backend.api: REST at /api/v1; no public OpenAPI exposure

security.auth: Azure AD SAML SSO via NextAuth.js; MFA enforced in Azure AD policy

data.db: Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server
data.cache: Azure Cache for Redis 7 for session tokens

constraints.network: VPN-only ingress via Azure VPN Gateway
constraints.ban_api: Must call existing game backend REST webhook on suspension actions

open_questions.severity_classifier: Scope of auto-severity scoring -- rules-based or ML model

confirmed_decisions.audit_log: Append-only PostgreSQL table; no soft deletes; no UPDATE on audit records

reasoner_reviews.product: The report queue with auto-severity scoring is the highest-value feature for moderator efficiency. The audit trail is non-negotiable from a compliance standpoint. Appeals workflow is lower priority but must be scoped before any player-facing appeal portal is built.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI with Pydantic v2 is well-suited for the strict input validation this platform requires. The append-only audit_log_entries table design is correct and must be enforced at the database layer with a trigger preventing UPDATE and DELETE. The cross-game ban webhook integration needs a retry queue with dead-letter handling to ensure suspension propagation is reliable.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure AD MFA enforcement must be verified in the Azure AD conditional access policy before launch, not just assumed from app configuration. All queries touching player PII should log the accessing user_id to the audit trail. Chat transcript storage must be encrypted at column level, not just at-rest disk encryption.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The VPN-only constraint must be enforced with an AKS network policy and an Azure VPN Gateway, not just relying on absence of a public IP. The no-third-party-SaaS constraint rules out services like Intercom or Zendesk for case management -- all case workflow must be built in-house.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with domain routers: reports (ingest and queue), cases (lifecycle management), bans (record and propagation), appeals (workflow), audit (append-only write). Middleware: Azure AD token validation, RBAC enforcement decorator per endpoint.
backend.api_patterns: REST at /api/v1; all endpoints require Bearer token from Azure AD. No public API. Webhook client for game backend ban propagation with retry via Celery + Azure Service Bus dead-letter queue.
backend.failure_handling: Ban webhook failures queued to Azure Service Bus dead-letter with 3 retry attempts before alerting on-call. Audit log write failures trigger immediate PagerDuty P1 alert.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 app router; SSR for report queue and case list pages. Client-side React Query for case detail updates.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Report Queue (paginated table with severity filter), Case Detail (evidence viewer, sanction form, audit timeline), Ban Records (search by player_id or game_id), Appeals Dashboard (pending/reviewed queues), Compliance Reports (date-range exports)
frontend.accessibility_notes: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance required for internal tooling; keyboard navigation for all case action forms

security.auth_design: NextAuth.js SAML provider configured against Azure AD tenant. Session tokens stored in Redis with 8-hour TTL. Azure AD conditional access enforces MFA for all users.
security.authorization_model: FastAPI dependency injection enforces role check on every endpoint. Moderators can view and act on assigned queues; senior_moderators can override sanctions; compliance_reviewers read-only on all data; legal_admin exports raw data for legal hold.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_log_entries table with trigger DENY UPDATE/DELETE. Fields: id, actor_user_id, action_type, target_entity_type, target_entity_id, diff_json, ip_address, user_agent, created_at.

data.entities: player_reports (id, reporter_player_id, reported_player_id, game_id, report_type, evidence_s3_key, severity_score, status, created_at), moderation_cases (id, report_id, assigned_moderator_id, status ENUM open/pending_review/resolved/escalated, resolution_type, resolved_at), ban_records (id, player_id, game_id, ban_type ENUM chat/game/account, reason, issued_by, issued_at, expires_at, is_active), appeal_submissions (id, ban_record_id, player_external_id, appeal_text, submitted_at, reviewed_by, decision, decided_at), audit_log_entries (id, actor_user_id, action_type, target_entity_type, target_entity_id, diff_json, ip_address, created_at)
data.retention_and_deletion: Cases and ban records retained 5 years. Minor player data erasure job runs weekly, soft-flagging records for legal review before permanent deletion.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster in West Europe with private ingress via Azure Internal Load Balancer; VPN Gateway for staff access. Separate namespaces for staging and production.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipeline: on PR merge, run pytest (85% gate), build Docker image, push to Azure Container Registry, deploy to staging. Production deploy requires manual approval gate from team lead.
devops.backup_and_recovery: PostgreSQL automated backups every 6 hours with 35-day retention. Point-in-time restore tested quarterly.

ISS-001.title: Audit log not enforced at database layer -- relies only on application logic
ISS-001.detail: The previous plan described an append-only audit log in the application layer via a FastAPI middleware, but no database-level trigger or constraint prevents direct UPDATE or DELETE on audit_log_entries. A compromised admin account or a buggy migration could silently corrupt the audit trail.

ISS-002.title: Ban webhook retry has no dead-letter handling -- failed propagations silently dropped
ISS-002.detail: The round 1 plan mentioned calling the game backend ban webhook but had no retry mechanism or dead-letter queue. If the game backend is unavailable when a ban is issued, the suspension will not propagate, allowing banned players to continue playing.

ISS-003.title: Chat transcript storage lacks column-level encryption
ISS-003.detail: Chat transcripts are stored as plaintext TEXT columns in the player_reports table. Disk-level encryption alone is insufficient for this category of personal data given GDPR Article 32 requirements for appropriate technical measures.

focus_issues.title: Audit log not enforced at database layer
focus_issues.detail: Application-layer-only append enforcement is insufficient; a DB-level trigger denying UPDATE/DELETE on audit_log_entries is required.

focus_issues.title: Ban webhook retry has no dead-letter handling
focus_issues.detail: Failed ban webhook calls must be queued for retry with a dead-letter channel to prevent silently unpropagate suspensions.

latest_recommendations: Add a PostgreSQL trigger that raises an exception on any UPDATE or DELETE attempt against audit_log_entries
Introduce a Celery task queue backed by Azure Service Bus for ban webhook retries with 3 attempts and dead-letter alerting
Encrypt chat_transcript column using pgcrypto or Azure Column Encryption before storing

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity issues unresolved; audit trail integrity and ban propagation reliability are blocking

previous_audits.summary: The architecture covers the core moderation workflow but has two high-severity gaps: the audit log lacks database-level immutability enforcement, and ban webhook failures have no retry mechanism, making suspension propagation unreliable.
strengths: Azure AD MFA and RBAC design is appropriate for internal PII tooling
PostgreSQL schema with append-only audit intention is correctly conceptualized
VPN-only AKS ingress topology is appropriate
concerns: Audit log immutability relies solely on application logic with no database constraint
Ban webhook failure handling is absent -- failed suspensions silently dropped
Chat transcript plaintext storage does not meet GDPR Article 32 appropriate technical measures
blocking_issues: No database-level trigger preventing UPDATE/DELETE on audit_log_entries
No retry queue for ban webhook propagation failures
recommendations: Add PostgreSQL trigger BEFORE UPDATE OR DELETE ON audit_log_entries that raises exception with custom error code
Implement Celery task for ban webhook with exponential backoff (3 retries) and Azure Service Bus dead-letter queue
Add pgcrypto column encryption for chat_transcript field

issue_updates.title: Audit log not enforced at database layer
issue_updates.detail: Plan relies on FastAPI middleware only; no DB trigger present

issue_updates.title: Ban webhook has no retry
issue_updates.detail: Webhook call is fire-and-forget with no error queue

previous_plan.title: Player Moderation Platform -- Round 1 Draft
previous_plan.executive_summary: Internal trust-and-safety web application built with Next.js 14 and FastAPI on AKS, enabling moderators to review player reports, issue sanctions, and manage appeals with a role-based access model and audit logging.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Three-tier web app: Next.js SSR frontend behind Azure Internal Load Balancer, FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL for persistence. Azure AD SAML SSO enforces MFA for all staff. All actions logged to audit_log_entries table.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Azure AD MFA enforced. RBAC via FastAPI dependency injection. Audit log written by application layer on every moderator action. Chat transcripts stored in TEXT column.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: AKS West Europe with VPN Gateway. Azure DevOps CI/CD with manual production approval gate.

target_output.thinking_summary: Addressing the two blocking issues from round 1: audit log immutability must be enforced at the PostgreSQL layer with a trigger, not just application middleware. Ban webhook reliability requires a Celery task queue with Azure Service Bus dead-letter. Chat transcript encryption via pgcrypto closes the GDPR Article 32 gap. The overall architecture is sound; this revision hardens the security and reliability foundations without structural changes.

fix_report.action_taken: Added PostgreSQL trigger BEFORE UPDATE OR DELETE ON audit_log_entries that raises sqlstate '45000' with message 'audit_log_entries is immutable'; documented trigger migration in flyway script V3__audit_immutability_trigger.sql
fix_report.expected_outcome: Any UPDATE or DELETE attempt against audit_log_entries from application code, admin consoles, or accidental migrations will be rejected at the database level, guaranteeing audit trail integrity

fix_report.action_taken: Introduced Celery worker with Azure Service Bus as broker for ban webhook propagation; task retries up to 3 times with exponential backoff (30s, 90s, 270s); failed tasks routed to dead-letter queue with PagerDuty P1 alert on dead-letter message count > 0
fix_report.expected_outcome: Ban suspension propagation to game backend is reliable with automatic retry; failed propagations are surfaced to on-call within minutes rather than silently dropped

target_output.title: Player Moderation Platform -- Hardened Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: Internal trust-and-safety web platform built with Next.js 14 and FastAPI on Azure Kubernetes Service, serving studio moderators reviewing player reports, issuing sanctions, and processing appeals. This revision hardens audit trail immutability via a PostgreSQL trigger, introduces a Celery + Azure Service Bus ban propagation queue with dead-letter alerting, and adds pgcrypto column encryption for chat transcripts to satisfy GDPR Article 32.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier internal web application accessible only via Azure VPN Gateway. Next.js 14 SSR frontend served through AKS ingress with Azure Internal Load Balancer communicates over HTTPS to a FastAPI backend. Azure AD SAML SSO with MFA enforced provides identity. FastAPI handlers write all moderation actions to an append-only audit_log_entries table protected by a database trigger that rejects any UPDATE or DELETE. A separate Celery worker process consumes ban_propagation tasks from Azure Service Bus, calling the game backend REST webhook with retry and dead-letter routing. PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server stores all relational data with chat_transcript columns encrypted via pgcrypto. Redis caches session tokens with 8-hour TTL.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, NextAuth.js (SAML). Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Pydantic v2, Celery 5, azure-servicebus SDK, pgcrypto extension. Database: Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server, pgcrypto for column encryption, Flyway for migrations. Cache: Azure Cache for Redis 7. Orchestration: AKS West Europe. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Monitoring: Azure Monitor, Application Insights, Log Analytics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Report Queue: ingested reports auto-scored by severity classifier (rules-based: keyword matching + report frequency heuristics); paginated queue with severity filter and assignment controls. Case Management: case detail with evidence viewer (S3-backed attachment previewer), sanction form (ban_type, duration, reason), and full audit timeline. Ban Records: searchable by player_id or game_id; suspension status synced via Celery webhook worker. Appeals Workflow: pending/reviewed/decided queues; senior_moderator decision form; automated email notification on decision. Compliance Dashboard: date-range filter exports of moderation activity counts and resolution rates for legal review.
target_output.system_components: Next.js 14 SSR frontend, Azure Internal Load Balancer, AKS Ingress Controller, FastAPI backend (2 replicas), Celery worker (ban_propagation tasks), Azure Service Bus (retry queue + dead-letter), Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 (append-only audit_log trigger), Azure Cache for Redis 7 (session cache), Azure Blob Storage (evidence attachments), Azure AD (SAML SSO + MFA conditional access), Application Insights (APM), Log Analytics workspace.
target_output.workflows: Report Triage: a player report arrives via the game backend webhook POST /api/v1/reports/ingest; the FastAPI ingest handler extracts player_id, game_id, report_type, and evidence_s3_key; a severity score is computed by the rules engine (keyword weight + reporter history factor); the report is written to player_reports and an audit_log_entries row is created; the report appears in the moderator queue sorted by severity_score descending. Ban Issuance with Propagation: a senior_moderator selects a case, fills the sanction form (ban_type=account, duration=permanent, reason=harassment_confirmed), and submits; FastAPI writes a ban_records row, commits an audit_log_entries row, and enqueues a ban_propagation Celery task; the Celery worker calls the game backend REST webhook POST /internal/bans with the player_id and ban details; on success the ban_records.propagated_at is updated; on failure after 3 retries the task moves to the Azure Service Bus dead-letter queue and PagerDuty fires.
target_output.data_model: player_reports (id UUID PK, reporter_player_id TEXT, reported_player_id TEXT, game_id TEXT, report_type TEXT, evidence_s3_key TEXT, chat_transcript_encrypted BYTEA, severity_score REAL, status TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). moderation_cases (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK, assigned_moderator_id UUID FK, status TEXT CHECK IN (open, pending_review, resolved, escalated), resolution_type TEXT, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ban_records (id UUID PK, player_id TEXT, game_id TEXT, ban_type TEXT CHECK IN (chat, game, account), reason TEXT, issued_by UUID FK, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, is_active BOOLEAN, propagated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). appeal_submissions (id UUID PK, ban_record_id UUID FK, player_external_id TEXT, appeal_text TEXT, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, reviewed_by UUID FK, decision TEXT CHECK IN (upheld, overturned, reduced), decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_log_entries (id UUID PK, actor_user_id UUID, action_type TEXT, target_entity_type TEXT, target_entity_id UUID, diff_json JSONB, ip_address INET, user_agent TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). PostgreSQL trigger: CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_immutable() RETURNS trigger AS $$ BEGIN RAISE EXCEPTION 'audit_log_entries is immutable'; END; $$ LANGUAGE plpgsql; CREATE TRIGGER enforce_audit_immutability BEFORE UPDATE OR DELETE ON audit_log_entries FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_immutable();
target_output.api_design: REST at /api/v1 (internal only, no public exposure). POST /reports/ingest (game backend webhook), GET /reports?status=&severity_min= (moderator queue), GET /cases/:id (case detail), POST /cases/:id/sanction (issue ban), GET /bans?player_id= (ban lookup), POST /appeals/:ban_id (submit appeal), PATCH /appeals/:id/decide (senior moderator decision), GET /audit-log?actor=&entity_type=&from=&to= (compliance export), GET /compliance/report?from=&to= (summary stats). All endpoints require Azure AD Bearer token; role enforcement via FastAPI dependency injected check_permission(required_role).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD MFA via conditional access policy enforced before any token issuance. FastAPI RBAC: each endpoint decorated with Depends(check_permission(role)) rejecting requests from insufficient roles. Audit trail: audit_log_entries protected by PostgreSQL BEFORE UPDATE OR DELETE trigger raising exception; application layer writes on every moderator action. Chat transcript encryption: pgcrypto pgp_sym_encrypt/pgp_sym_decrypt using AES-256; key stored in Azure Key Vault, fetched at startup via managed identity. GDPR: compliance_reviewer export scoped to aggregated data only; legal_admin export includes PII with audit log entry; minor player erasure pipeline soft-flags records, notifies legal team, then deletes after 14-day hold. Evidence attachments stored in Azure Blob with private access and SAS URL generation scoped to authenticated sessions.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS West Europe cluster with two namespaces: staging and production. FastAPI deployment: 2-4 replicas with HPA on CPU 70%. Celery worker deployment: 1-2 replicas consuming from Azure Service Bus queue ban_propagation. Azure Internal Load Balancer with VPN Gateway enforces internal-only access; no public ingress controller. Azure DevOps Pipeline: on PR merge, pytest (85% coverage gate), Docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, deploy to staging via kubectl rolling update; production deploy requires manual approval from team lead. Flyway migration runs as Kubernetes Job pre-deploy. Secrets stored in Azure Key Vault referenced via AKS CSI driver.
target_output.observability: Application Insights SDK in FastAPI for distributed tracing. Structured JSON logs to Log Analytics workspace with fields: request_id, user_id, action, entity_type, latency_ms. Custom metrics: moderation_queue_depth (gauge), ban_propagation_retry_count (counter), audit_log_write_failures (counter -- must be 0). Alert rules: audit_log_write_failures > 0 triggers PagerDuty P1; ban dead-letter queue message count > 0 triggers PagerDuty P1; API error rate > 2% triggers P2.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $600/month for internal tool at 50 concurrent moderators: AKS node pool 2x D4s_v3 ($400), Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server General Purpose ($120), Azure Cache for Redis C1 ($55), Azure Service Bus Standard ($25). No CDN required for internal tooling. Scaling primarily driven by report ingestion volume; Celery worker scales to 4 replicas during high-volume moderation events.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): report queue with auto-severity scoring, case management with sanction form, ban record tracking, append-only audit log with PostgreSQL trigger, Azure AD MFA, Azure DevOps CI. Phase 2 (8 weeks): appeals workflow with senior moderator decision forms, Celery ban propagation with Azure Service Bus dead-letter, pgcrypto chat transcript encryption, GDPR erasure pipeline, compliance export dashboard. Phase 3: ML severity classifier replacing rules engine, cross-game automated ban propagation, player-facing appeal status portal.
target_output.development_guidelines: Pydantic v2 models required for all request and response shapes; no dict unpacking of untrusted input. Every FastAPI endpoint tested with pytest-asyncio including RBAC negative tests (wrong role must receive 403). audit_log_entries trigger tested via direct psycopg2 UPDATE attempt in pytest asserting pg exception. Celery tasks unit-tested with mock game backend webhook responses including failure simulation. Flyway migrations append-only; existing scripts never modified. No third-party SaaS with player PII access; all integrations via internal REST webhooks.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Celery worker Azure Service Bus connection drops during high-volume ban events causing task queue backup. Mitigation: Celery visibility timeout tuned to 600s; worker health check in AKS liveness probe restarts stalled workers. Tradeoff: pgcrypto symmetric encryption over Azure Transparent Data Encryption for chat transcripts -- chosen because TDE provides at-rest protection only while pgcrypto protects against SQL injection data exfiltration at the column level. Risk: Azure AD tenant misconfiguration disabling MFA enforcement. Mitigation: audit Azure AD conditional access policy in CI using az cli assertion test on policy state.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Audit log immutability enforced at DB layer via PostgreSQL trigger -- application-layer enforcement alone was insufficient. Ban webhook reliability achieved via Celery + Azure Service Bus with dead-letter alerting. Severity classifier scoped to rules-based for MVP; ML classifier deferred to Phase 3 to avoid training data dependency at launch.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision of internal moderation platform; fixes audit log DB immutability and ban webhook retry queue; weak quality reflects thin sections despite fixes.

project_goal.value: Automated game server performance regression detector that runs after each build, spins up ephemeral EC2 spot instances, executes scripted load scenarios, captures frame time and memory profiling data, and compares results against baseline benchmarks to flag regressions before they reach production
project_goal.rationale: Current manual profiling process misses regressions that reach players; automated detection at CI time prevents performance rollbacks

target_users.value: Game server engineers and CI/CD pipeline automation; results surfaced to PR authors via GitHub status checks
target_users.rationale: Automation runs headlessly; engineers interact via PR status checks and Grafana reports

project_class.rationale: Headless automation pipeline triggered by CI events, not a user-facing application

capabilities.rationale: Covers orchestration scripts, GitHub CI integration, EC2 lifecycle batch jobs, and InfluxDB analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Ephemeral EC2 fleet lifecycle, parallel load scenarios, and statistical regression detection require careful orchestration

risk_level.rationale: False positives block PRs; false negatives miss regressions; EC2 spot interruptions can corrupt runs

data_sensitivity.rationale: Profiling data and server binaries are internal engineering assets; no player data involved

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs in AWS VPC; Grafana reports accessible on internal network only

access_model.value: IAM roles for EC2 instances and GitHub Actions runners; no human-facing auth beyond Grafana SSO via Okta for report dashboards
access_model.rationale: Headless automation; human access limited to Grafana dashboards only

feature_scope.value: EC2 spot fleet provisioning per build, server binary deployment from S3, scripted load scenario execution, frame time and memory profiling collection, InfluxDB storage of profiling timeseries, statistical comparison against stored baselines, GitHub PR status check posting, Grafana regression report dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full automation scope including fleet lifecycle and analytics

mvp_scope.value: Single load scenario (200 concurrent bots), frame time p50/p99 regression detection, memory high-watermark comparison, GitHub status check posting; multi-scenario and baseline branching deferred to Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Single scenario MVP validates end-to-end automation before adding complexity

security_baseline.value: IAM instance roles with least-privilege EC2 and S3 access; server binaries fetched from private S3 bucket; profiling results written to private S3; no public endpoints; VPC security groups restrict inter-instance communication to load test traffic only
security_baseline.rationale: Internal tool with proprietary game binary; S3 access controls prevent binary leakage

frontend_stack.value: None; reports delivered via Grafana dashboard and GitHub PR status checks; no custom frontend built
frontend_stack.rationale: Automation tool with no browser UI

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 orchestration scripts running in GitHub Actions and AWS Lambda for fleet lifecycle hooks
backend_stack.rationale: Python chosen for ecosystem support for boto3, influxdb-client, and scipy for statistical tests

data_platform.value: InfluxDB Cloud for profiling timeseries metrics; PostgreSQL 16 RDS for benchmark baselines and run metadata; S3 for raw profiling data archives and server binaries
data_platform.rationale: InfluxDB for high-resolution timeseries; PostgreSQL for structured baseline comparisons

hosting_target.value: GitHub Actions for CI orchestration; AWS EC2 spot instances (c5.2xlarge) for game server and load generator; Lambda for fleet teardown hooks; all in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Spot instances reduce cost for ephemeral compute; Lambda for reliable fleet cleanup on spot interruption

privacy_retention_policy.value: Profiling timeseries retained in InfluxDB for 90 days; raw profiling archives in S3 retained 30 days with lifecycle policy; baseline snapshots retained indefinitely in PostgreSQL
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Short retention for high-volume profiling data; permanent baselines for regression history

future_scope.value: Multi-scenario parallel runs, per-subsystem profiling (networking, physics, AI), baseline auto-promotion on clean main branch merges, Slack notification for regression summaries
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 items after MVP validation

constraints.value: Total regression run must complete within 30 minutes to fit within PR CI budget; spot interruption must trigger automatic run retry; cost per run must stay below $5
constraints.rationale: Engineer productivity requires fast feedback; cost control for high-frequency builds

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards over InfluxDB for frame time and memory trends per build; GitHub Actions step-level logs; CloudWatch metrics for EC2 fleet health and spot interruption events
observability_baseline.rationale: Trend visibility and spot interruption alerting needed for reliable CI integration

execution_preference.value: Ship as GitHub Actions reusable workflow invoked by game server repo CI; Python orchestration scripts versioned in a dedicated tooling repo
execution_preference.rationale: Reusable workflow pattern allows adoption across multiple game server repos without duplication

project.goal: Automated performance regression detection for game server builds via ephemeral EC2 fleet
project.target_users: Game server engineers via GitHub PR status checks
project.timeline: 8-week MVP

backend.api: GitHub Checks API for status reporting

security.auth: IAM roles

open_questions.baseline_branching: Should baselines be tracked per branch or only per main?

reasoner_reviews.product: The 30-minute run budget is tight for a full game server load test including fleet spin-up and teardown. The MVP single-scenario approach is correct; parallel scenarios in Phase 2 can be achieved by running multiple spot instances simultaneously. Grafana trend views add value beyond per-PR pass/fail.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using Lambda for fleet teardown hooks is the right call — spot interruptions are unpredictable and a Lambda listening to EC2 spot interruption notices via EventBridge ensures cleanup even if the GitHub Actions runner is gone. The Python scipy Mann-Whitney U test is appropriate for non-parametric frame time distribution comparison. Baseline storage in PostgreSQL allows per-branch and per-game-mode baseline versioning.
reasoner_reviews.security: IAM instance roles must be scoped to read S3 binaries and write S3 profiling results only — no EC2 describe or IAM manipulation permissions on instance roles. Spot instance metadata endpoint should have IMDSv2 enforced to prevent SSRF-based metadata access.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 30-minute budget requires c5.2xlarge spot with pre-warmed AMI to minimize cold start. Cost $5 limit is achievable with spot pricing at ~$0.12/hr for c5.2xlarge for under 30 minutes of two instances. Spot interruption retry adds at most 5 minutes overhead if the replacement instance starts promptly.

backend.service_design: Orchestration script (Python) runs in GitHub Actions: provision EC2 spot fleet (1 server instance + 1 load generator), deploy server binary from S3, run load scenario script, collect profiling samples, upload to InfluxDB and S3, compare against PostgreSQL baseline, post GitHub status check. Lambda function subscribed to EC2 spot interruption EventBridge rule handles fleet cleanup on interruption.
backend.background_jobs: Fleet teardown Lambda: triggered by AWS/EC2 Spot Instance Interruption Warning EventBridge event; terminates remaining fleet instances and marks run as interrupted in PostgreSQL run_metadata table. Retry logic: GitHub Actions workflow detects interrupted status and re-queues the run up to 2 times.
backend.failure_handling: Spot interruption triggers Lambda teardown and GitHub Actions retry. If InfluxDB write fails, raw profiling data still archived to S3 and comparison can be replayed from S3. Baseline comparison script catches statistical test failures and posts a warning (non-blocking) rather than failing the PR on insufficient sample size.
backend.backend_risks: Spot interruption mid-run produces incomplete profiling data that could generate false regressions. Mitigation: run metadata tracks sample_count; comparison script rejects runs with sample_count below minimum threshold and marks check as inconclusive rather than failed.

security.auth_design: GitHub Actions OIDC federation to AWS IAM role for fleet provisioning permissions. EC2 instance roles scoped to s3:GetObject on binaries bucket and s3:PutObject on profiling results bucket. Lambda teardown role scoped to ec2:TerminateInstances on fleet tag only.
security.secrets_management: InfluxDB write token and PostgreSQL connection string stored in GitHub Actions secrets; injected as environment variables in workflow. EC2 instances receive no secrets; they use IAM instance role for S3 access.
security.security_risks: Compromised game server binary in S3 could execute malicious code on EC2 instances. Mitigation: S3 bucket policy restricts write access to the CI pipeline IAM role only; S3 object checksum verified by orchestration script before deployment.

data.entities: build_artifacts (build_id VARCHAR PK, repo, branch, commit_sha, s3_binary_key, uploaded_at). benchmark_scenarios (scenario_id VARCHAR PK, name, bot_count INT, duration_seconds, script_s3_key). performance_baselines (baseline_id UUID PK, scenario_id FK, branch, commit_sha, p50_frame_ms FLOAT, p99_frame_ms FLOAT, memory_high_watermark_mb INT, sample_count INT, captured_at). test_run_results (run_id UUID PK, build_id FK, scenario_id FK, status ENUM running/completed/interrupted/inconclusive, p50_frame_ms FLOAT, p99_frame_ms FLOAT, memory_high_watermark_mb INT, regression_detected BOOLEAN, statistical_confidence FLOAT, started_at, completed_at). profiling_samples stored in InfluxDB measurement server_perf with tags build_id, scenario_id, instance_id and fields frame_time_ms, memory_resident_mb, cpu_percent at 1-second resolution.
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS for structured baseline and run metadata. InfluxDB Cloud for high-resolution profiling timeseries. S3 bucket for raw profiling archives (CSV per run) and server binaries with 30-day lifecycle.
data.data_risks: InfluxDB Cloud outage prevents live metrics during run but raw S3 archive allows post-hoc replay. PostgreSQL baseline data loss would require re-establishing baselines from git history — critical data must be backed up daily.

devops.deployment_topology: No persistent compute beyond PostgreSQL RDS and InfluxDB Cloud. All load testing infrastructure is ephemeral EC2 spot. Lambda teardown function deployed via SAM in the tooling repo. GitHub Actions reusable workflow hosted in dedicated tooling repo and referenced by game server repos.
devops.ci_cd_design: Reusable GitHub Actions workflow: OIDC auth to AWS, boto3 provision spot fleet with launch template, poll fleet status until running, SSH deploy binary and scenario script, run scenario for configured duration, collect profiling output, upload to InfluxDB + S3, run comparison script, post GitHub Check Run result, terminate fleet.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch metrics for EC2 spot interruption frequency and fleet provision time. GitHub Actions step logs for per-run orchestration trace. Grafana over InfluxDB showing frame time p50/p99 trend per commit on main branch.
devops.rollback_strategy: No deployment rollback needed for automation tool itself. If orchestration script has a bug, update tooling repo and game server PRs automatically pick up new version on next run.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an ephemeral performance regression automation tool for game server CI. The key risk is spot interruption mid-run producing incomplete profiling data; the Lambda teardown hook with EventBridge spot interruption notice is the correct pattern to ensure fleet cleanup and trigger retry. The statistical comparison using Mann-Whitney U on frame time distributions is more robust than simple mean comparison for non-normal frame time data.
target_output.title: Game Server Performance Regression Detector
target_output.executive_summary: An automated regression detection pipeline that provisions ephemeral EC2 spot fleets on each game server build, runs scripted load scenarios with 200 concurrent bots, collects frame time and memory profiling into InfluxDB, compares results against PostgreSQL baselines using Mann-Whitney U statistical tests, and posts blocking GitHub PR status checks on detected regressions. Spot interruptions handled via Lambda EventBridge hooks with automatic retry.
target_output.architecture_overview: Headless automation pipeline triggered by GitHub Actions on game server build completion.
