project.goal: Build a live esports event score tracker as a static site with client-side API polling
project.target_users: Anonymous public spectators
project.timeline: Live in 48 hours for event day

frontend.state: Client-side React islands for polling components

constraints.no_backend: Static hosting only on Cloudflare Pages

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is appropriately minimal for a 48-hour event site. Polling at 30-second intervals stays well within the 60 req/min rate limit even with multiple concurrent users since polling is client-side and each client makes independent requests. The bracket view should be pre-rendered at build time from the initial bracket structure and updated client-side only for score changes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro 4 with client-side React islands is the right choice. Static HTML for initial bracket structure reduces time-to-first-render for fans loading during peak match times. The polling component should cache the last API response in component state and only re-render cells where the score value has changed, to avoid full bracket re-renders on every poll.
reasoner_reviews.security: Content Security Policy must explicitly restrict script-src to self and the scores API domain to prevent XSS. No cookies, no localStorage, no tracking pixels -- this keeps the site GDPR-exempt and avoids consent banner friction on event day.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The site must be fully built and deployed before the event's first match begins — a 48-hour hard deadline. Static generation at build time means no server-side rendering; bracket structure is pre-rendered HTML with React islands for score polling only. The scores API rate limit of 60 requests/minute with multiple browser clients means polling interval must be at least 30 seconds per client to stay within rate limits at expected peak concurrency of 500 viewers.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 static site with file-based routing. Static pages: index (event overview), /bracket (tournament bracket), /standings (series standings), /schedule. Client-side React islands for LiveScoreCard and BracketCell components that own polling state.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Homepage with live match highlights, /bracket with full bracket grid, /standings with series win/loss table, /schedule with match times and status
frontend.state_management: Each LiveScoreCard React island maintains its own useState for current scores and last-updated timestamp; polling via setInterval with clearInterval on unmount; diff against previous state to trigger CSS animation class on score change
frontend.performance_notes: Astro partial hydration -- only LiveScoreCard and BracketCell components hydrated client-side; rest of page is static HTML. Tailwind purge eliminates unused CSS. Cloudflare CDN edge caching for static assets.

security.auth_design: No authentication. Content Security Policy header via Cloudflare Pages _headers file: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; connect-src 'self' https://api.esportsdata.example.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; frame-ancestors 'none'.
security.abuse_prevention: Cloudflare proxy absorbs DDoS traffic. No API keys or secrets in client code -- scores API is public. Rate limiting handled client-side by 30-second polling interval.

data.entities: No persistent entities. In-memory client state only: MatchScore (matchId, teamA score, teamB score, status), SeriesStanding (teamId, wins, losses, pointDiff), BracketPosition (matchId, round, slot, teamA, teamB, winnerId).
data.storage_design: No database. Client-side React component state only. No browser localStorage or sessionStorage used.
data.retention_and_deletion: No data persisted. All state held in React component memory and discarded on page navigation or tab close.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloudflare Pages static site. GitHub Actions build trigger on push to main. Cloudflare proxy for DDoS mitigation and global CDN edge delivery.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: npm ci, astro check (TypeScript), astro build, wrangler pages deploy ./dist --project-name esports-event-coverage. Build under 60 seconds. Branch previews auto-deployed by Cloudflare Pages on PR.
devops.observability_stack: Cloudflare Web Analytics dashboard (privacy-preserving, no cookies). No APM needed for static site. Browser console.error for polling failure visibility during development.

target_output.thinking_summary: Simple static website with client-side polling for esports scores. No backend, no database -- Astro 4 generates static HTML, React islands handle polling state. Key design decision: 30-second polling interval per tab consumes 4 req/min across two endpoints, well within the 60 req/min API rate limit. Content Security Policy headers are the primary security control for a public no-auth site.
target_output.title: Live Esports Event Coverage Site Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A simple static website built with Astro 4 and TypeScript on Cloudflare Pages for displaying live match scores, tournament bracket status, and series standings during an esports event. Client-side React islands poll a third-party scores API at 30-second intervals and animate score changes without full page reloads. No backend, no database, no user authentication. Cloudflare proxy provides DDoS mitigation. Target delivery in 48 hours for event day launch.
target_output.architecture_overview: Astro 4 generates static HTML pages for the bracket structure, standings table, and event schedule at build time from local JSON seed data. Client-side React islands are hydrated only on pages requiring live data -- LiveScoreCard components on the bracket and standings pages poll the third-party esports scores API every 30 seconds using the browser Fetch API, diff responses against local useState to detect score changes, and apply CSS transition classes to animate updated score cells. No server-side runtime exists on Cloudflare Pages. Cloudflare's global CDN delivers static assets from edge nodes closest to spectators worldwide. Cloudflare Web Analytics captures page view counts and geographic distribution without cookies or user tracking pixels.
target_output.technology_stack: Astro 4 with TypeScript for static site generation and file-based routing. React 18 client islands for LiveScoreCard and BracketCell polling components. Tailwind CSS for styling. Cloudflare Pages for global CDN hosting. Cloudflare Web Analytics for privacy-preserving analytics. GitHub Actions CI with Wrangler CLI for automated deployment. Third-party esports data API as the live scores data source.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Live Match Scores: LiveScoreCard island polls the matches endpoint every 30 seconds, displays team names with game scores (e.g., 2-1), highlights cells where scores changed since last poll with a 500ms fade-in animation. Tournament Bracket View: static bracket grid pre-rendered at build time from initial seedings in seed-data.json; BracketCell islands update winner highlights and eliminated-team dimming based on live API data. Series Standings Table: standings updated from API standings endpoint on each poll interval, sorted by wins descending with point differential as tiebreaker. Event Schedule: static page listing match times, round labels, and status badges; match status (upcoming/live/completed) updated by polling component. Team Roster Pages: fully static, built at deploy time from teams-data.json local file.
target_output.system_components: Cloudflare Pages CDN: serves pre-built Astro HTML, CSS, and JS bundles from global edge nodes with no origin server. Astro build output: static HTML pages with Astro client:only island markers for React polling components. LiveScoreCard React island: setInterval-based fetch polling with useState diffing and CSS animation triggers on score change. BracketCell React island: reads bracket state from shared ScoreContext populated by the LiveScoreCard polling loop. Third-party scores API: external public endpoint providing match scores, series standings, and bracket outcomes. Cloudflare Web Analytics: edge-side aggregate page analytics with no JavaScript tracking pixel required.
target_output.workflows: Score Polling: on component mount, LiveScoreCard island initializes setInterval(fetchScores, 30000); fetchScores calls fetch to the tournament matches endpoint; response JSON is diffed field-by-field against previousState stored in useRef; cells with changed score values receive the score-updated CSS class triggering a Tailwind transition animation for 500ms; setInterval is cleaned up in the useEffect return callback to prevent memory leaks on navigation. Bracket State Derivation: BracketCell islands subscribe to ScoreContext which is updated by the polling loop; when a series match_status transitions to completed, the winning team cell receives the bracket-winner class and the losing team is visually dimmed; no separate bracket-specific polling required since bracket outcomes are derived from the scores payload.
target_output.data_model: No persistent data model. Client-side state shapes used by React islands: MatchScore {matchId: string, teamA: {name: string, score: number}, teamB: {name: string, score: number}, status: 'upcoming'|'live'|'completed'}. SeriesStanding {teamId: string, teamName: string, wins: number, losses: number, pointDiff: number}. BracketPosition {matchId: string, round: number, slot: number, teamAId: string|null, teamBId: string|null, winnerId: string|null}. Static seed data in src/data/seed-data.json provides initial bracket seedings and team info at build time.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Client-side Fetch calls to third-party scores API: GET https://api.esportsdata.example.com/v1/tournaments/:tournament_id/matches (match scores and status per match). GET https://api.esportsdata.example.com/v1/tournaments/:tournament_id/standings (series win/loss table). Both endpoints are public with no API key. Rate limit: 60 req/min per IP. With 30-second polling and two endpoints, a single browser tab consumes 4 req/min -- 15x headroom below the limit. API base URL and tournament ID are configured via Astro PUBLIC_ environment variables (import.meta.env.PUBLIC_SCORES_API_BASE, import.meta.env.PUBLIC_TOURNAMENT_ID) set in Cloudflare Pages dashboard.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Content Security Policy delivered via Cloudflare Pages _headers file on all routes: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; connect-src 'self' https://api.esportsdata.example.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: https://cdn.esportsteams.example.com; frame-ancestors 'none'. Additional security headers: X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff; X-Frame-Options: DENY. No cookies set, no localStorage or sessionStorage used, no user data collected. Cloudflare Web Analytics uses edge-side aggregation with no client-side JavaScript tracking -- GDPR-exempt, no consent banner required. No API secrets exposed in client bundle since the scores API is public.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloudflare Pages project linked to the GitHub repository. GitHub Actions workflow on push to main: npm ci, astro check (TypeScript type checking), astro build, then npx wrangler pages deploy ./dist --project-name esports-event-coverage using CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN repository secret. Build completes in under 60 seconds. Cloudflare Pages automatically creates branch preview deployments on all pull requests for staging review. PUBLIC_SCORES_API_BASE and PUBLIC_TOURNAMENT_ID environment variables are set in the Cloudflare Pages dashboard per environment. Production deployment propagates globally across Cloudflare edge within 2 minutes.
target_output.observability: Cloudflare Web Analytics dashboard: page view counts, unique visitor estimates, geographic distribution of traffic, top pages, and Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS) measured at the edge without any client-side JavaScript analytics library. Browser-side error handling in polling components: fetch failures are caught, a local error state is set, and a 'Scores temporarily unavailable -- retrying in 30s' banner is shown to the user; console.error logs the API endpoint and HTTP status code for debugging. No structured log shipping, no APM, no server-side metrics collection -- appropriate for a static event coverage site with zero server infrastructure.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Total cost: $0/month. Cloudflare Pages free tier includes unlimited static deployments and unlimited bandwidth. Cloudflare Web Analytics included free. GitHub Actions free tier covers the CI pipeline. Third-party scores API assumed to be on a free public tier. The site scales automatically with Cloudflare's global edge network -- no capacity planning required. Peak event traffic of 50k simultaneous viewers is absorbed entirely by CDN edge delivery with no origin server to overload.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Event Launch (48 hours): Astro site scaffold with homepage, /bracket, /standings, /schedule routes; LiveScoreCard polling island with 30-second interval and score-change animation; BracketCell island with winner highlight logic; CSP and security headers in _headers file; Cloudflare Pages deployment via GitHub Actions; Cloudflare Web Analytics enabled. Phase 2 Post-Event Polish (1 week): team roster static pages generated from teams-data.json, match VOD embed placeholders per match card, mobile responsive layout audit and fixes, score animation refinements. Phase 3 Multi-Event Expansion (future): event selector dropdown with multiple tournament IDs, player statistics deep-dive pages built from API historical data at build time, permanent esports media site navigation structure.
target_output.development_guidelines: All polling components must clean up setInterval via useEffect return function -- enforce with a custom ESLint rule or code review checklist. Add Zod schema validation on API response payloads in development to catch third-party API schema drift early; fail loudly in dev, silently degrade in production. PUBLIC_ prefixed environment variables are inlined at build time -- never put secrets in PUBLIC_ variables. Astro build must pass astro check with zero TypeScript errors before Wrangler deploy step in CI. Tailwind config must include the Astro integration plugin to prevent animation class purge issues with dynamically applied CSS classes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: third-party scores API returns stale or incorrect score data during a live match, showing wrong results to spectators. No mitigation possible -- site is fully dependent on API data quality; display a last-updated timestamp on each score card so spectators can judge freshness. Risk: scores API rate limit of 60 req/min exceeded if a spectator opens many tabs simultaneously, since each tab polls independently from the same IP. Mitigation: 30-second interval keeps per-tab consumption at 4 req/min (2 endpoints) leaving headroom for approximately 15 concurrent tabs per IP -- acceptable edge case. Tradeoff: client-side polling over server-sent events keeps architecture zero-infrastructure for the 48-hour deadline but makes the API rate limit visible to end users rather than absorbed server-side.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Polling interval: 30 seconds selected to balance score freshness against API rate limit -- 4 req/min per tab leaves 56 req/min headroom at 60 req/min limit. Bracket rendering: pre-rendered static HTML at build time with client island updates avoids full re-renders on every poll. Analytics without cookies: Cloudflare Web Analytics edge-side solution eliminates GDPR consent banner requirement. Tournament ID configuration: PUBLIC_ Astro environment variable set in Cloudflare Pages dashboard, not hardcoded, allowing the site to be reused for future events.

metadata.notes: First-pass static esports event coverage site with client-side score polling, no backend or database, Cloudflare Pages hosting, and CSP headers as primary security control.

profile.domain: Competitive FPS Esports Replay Analysis Desktop Application

project_goal.value: Professional replay analysis tool for competitive FPS esports coaches and analysts, enabling frame-accurate scrubbing of match demos, heatmap overlays of player positioning, and exportable clip annotations integrated with a cloud replay storage service
project_goal.rationale: Replacing fragmented workflow of per-game demo viewers, external annotation spreadsheets, and manual clip extraction with an integrated analysis desktop tool

target_users.value: Esports coaching staff and analysts at semi-pro and professional teams analyzing CS2 and Valorant match demos
target_users.rationale: Professional users with technical tolerance for complex UI; require frame-accurate precision and team collaboration features

project_class.rationale: Electron desktop app for cross-platform support with C++ native parser for demo format decoding

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Desktop UI, local C++ parsing engine, SQLite annotation storage, Auth0 login, S3 cloud replay integration, heatmap analytics

complexity_level.rationale: C++ demo parser integration with Electron IPC, frame-accurate timeline scrubbing, WebGL heatmap rendering, and cloud S3 sync

risk_level.rationale: No payment data or health data; replay files are game data; risk is data availability and coach workflow disruption

data_sensitivity.rationale: Replay files and annotations are competitive game data; no personal information regulated by GDPR or CCPA beyond user accounts

external_exposure.rationale: S3 cloud storage accessed via Auth0-authenticated sessions; team shared buckets require login

access_model.value: Auth0 authentication with team-based organization model; team members can access shared S3 replay bucket; solo analysts access personal bucket
access_model.rationale: Team collaboration requires shared storage access control via Auth0 organization membership

feature_scope.value: CS2 and Valorant demo file parsing, frame-accurate timeline scrubbing, heatmap overlay for player positioning, player path replay, annotation creation with frame markers, clip segment definition and video export, S3 cloud storage integration for team replay sharing, Auth0 authentication
feature_scope.rationale: Core analysis workflow features replacing per-game viewers and spreadsheet annotations

mvp_scope.value: CS2 demo parsing and playback, basic annotation creation, local SQLite storage, S3 upload and download for team sharing
mvp_scope.rationale: CS2 has larger user base; validate parsing accuracy and timeline UX before adding Valorant and video export

security_baseline.value: Auth0 PKCE flow for desktop OAuth, S3 pre-signed URLs for replay download to avoid long-lived credentials, Electron CSP blocking remote scripts, Sentry error reporting without replay file content in crash payloads
security_baseline.rationale: Desktop OAuth requires PKCE; pre-signed URLs limit S3 exposure to authenticated sessions

frontend_stack.value: Electron 31 with React 18 and TypeScript; WebGL canvas via Three.js for heatmap rendering
frontend_stack.rationale: Electron for cross-platform desktop packaging; Three.js WebGL for GPU-accelerated heatmap and player path visualization

backend_stack.value: C++ replay parsing engine integrated via Electron native addon (node-gyp); Node.js 22 Electron main process for IPC, S3 integration, and SQLite operations
backend_stack.rationale: C++ required for demo format parsing performance; Node.js handles async S3 and SQLite operations

data_platform.value: SQLite for local annotations and match metadata; AWS S3 for cloud replay file storage
data_platform.rationale: SQLite for local persistence without external server dependency; S3 for team-shared cloud replay library

hosting_target.value: Electron desktop binary distributed via website download and auto-update; S3 bucket per team for replay cloud storage
hosting_target.rationale: No server deployment; S3 for cloud storage tier

privacy_retention_policy.value: Local annotations and parsed match metadata stored until user deletes; cloud replay files in S3 managed by team admin; no telemetry collected
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No regulated personal data; retention managed by user and team admin

future_scope.value: Valorant demo format support, team VOD review sessions with synchronized playback, AI highlight detection
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP expansion after CS2 pipeline validated

constraints.value: Demo parsing must handle 64-tick and 128-tick CS2 demos up to 45 minutes; UI scrubbing must render at 60fps; binary size under 150MB
constraints.rationale: Match demo format requirements; 60fps scrubbing is UX requirement for frame-accurate coaching analysis

observability_baseline.value: Sentry desktop SDK for crash reporting; local log file in app data directory; no remote telemetry beyond crash reports
observability_baseline.rationale: Desktop app with user-controlled data; minimal telemetry to respect user privacy

execution_preference.value: Kanban with biweekly milestones; CircleCI for cross-platform builds; beta program with 5 esports teams
execution_preference.rationale: Beta program with professional teams provides high-quality feedback on parsing accuracy and UX

project.goal: Integrated CS2/Valorant demo analysis tool for esports coaches
project.target_users: Professional esports coaching staff and analysts
project.timeline: CS2 MVP in 14 weeks

frontend.rendering: Three.js WebGL for heatmap and player paths
frontend.state: Zustand for timeline and annotation state

backend.runtime: C++ native addon for demo parsing, Node.js 22 for Electron main process
backend.cloud: AWS SDK v3 for S3 integration

security.auth: Auth0 PKCE desktop flow

data.cloud: S3 replay files per team bucket

reasoner_reviews.product: The FPS replay analysis tool addresses a real workflow gap for esports coaches. CS2 demo parsing accuracy is critical; the C++ native addon approach is correct for performance. Clip export is a highly requested feature that requires careful video encoding pipeline design separate from the annotation system.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: C++ node-gyp native addon integration with Electron is technically sound but introduces build complexity across Windows, macOS, and Linux. The node-gyp compilation in CircleCI cross-platform matrix needs prebuilt binary distribution to avoid end-user compilation. Three.js WebGL for heatmap is appropriate for GPU-accelerated positional rendering.
reasoner_reviews.security: Auth0 PKCE flow is the correct OAuth pattern for Electron desktop apps; avoid implicit flow. S3 pre-signed URL expiry at 1 hour is reasonable for replay downloads; ensure URLs are not logged or cached with replay file paths exposed. Sentry crash payloads must be scrubbed to exclude replay file paths that could expose team strategy.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 60fps timeline scrubbing at 128-tick resolution requires the C++ parser to produce frame data at sub-16ms render intervals. Video clip export will be the most computationally intensive feature; FFmpeg integration via Electron native process or WebAssembly is needed.

backend.service_design: Electron main process Node.js 22 handles IPC from renderer, C++ native addon bridge for demo parsing, better-sqlite3 for local annotation CRUD, AWS SDK v3 for S3 pre-signed URL generation and upload/download. C++ addon exposes: parse_demo(file_path) -> FrameData[], get_player_positions(frame_range) -> PositionEvent[], compute_heatmap(player_id, round_range) -> HeatmapGrid
backend.api_patterns: Electron IPC via ipcMain/ipcRenderer; renderer invokes main process commands: demo:parse, demo:get-frames, annotations:create, annotations:list, cloud:upload-replay, cloud:get-download-url
backend.failure_handling: C++ addon parse errors return structured error codes to Node.js IPC handler; partial parse (corrupted demo tail) returns available frames with truncation flag; S3 operations retry 3x with exponential backoff

security.auth_design: Auth0 PKCE flow: Electron opens system browser to Auth0 login; Auth0 redirects to custom URI scheme replay-analyzer://callback; Electron intercepts and exchanges code for tokens; refresh token stored in OS keychain via keytar
security.secrets_management: Auth0 refresh token in OS keychain; AWS temporary credentials derived from Auth0 JWT exchange with AWS Cognito Identity Pool; no long-lived AWS credentials stored on disk
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Local app log in appDataDir/logs/; Sentry crash reports strip file paths before transmission; no replay content or annotation text in crash payloads

data.entities: replay_files (id, filename, game_type, demo_path_local, s3_key, uploaded_at, team_id), match_metadata (replay_id, map_name, match_date, team_a, team_b, rounds_json, tick_rate), player_annotations (id, replay_id, player_id, frame_start, frame_end, annotation_text, tag_list, created_at), clip_segments (id, replay_id, frame_start, frame_end, title, s3_export_key, exported_at), heatmap_frames (replay_id, player_id, round_number, position_data_json), team_sessions (id, team_id, created_at, member_ids_json)
data.storage_design: SQLite database at appDataDir/data/annotations.db for replay_files, match_metadata, player_annotations, clip_segments; S3 bucket per team for replay upload and clip export with pre-signed URL access
data.migration_notes: SQLite schema versioned in user_version pragma; migrations run on app startup using embedded SQL scripts

devops.deployment_topology: No server infrastructure; Electron binary distributed via website; S3 buckets provisioned per team by admin; CircleCI builds Windows, macOS, and Linux binaries in parallel matrix
devops.ci_cd_design: CircleCI pipeline: npm test, C++ native addon compilation per platform via node-pre-gyp, Electron packager, code signing, binary upload to release server; beta tag triggers beta channel update manifest
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous release binaries retained on distribution server; Electron auto-updater allows version pinning; SQLite schema migrations are additive-only

frontend.app_structure: Electron renderer React SPA; routes: Library (replay list), Analyzer (timeline + canvas), Annotations (per-replay annotation list), Export (clip segment queue), Settings (Auth0 login, team config)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Analyzer view: scrubbing timeline with 128-tick markers, Three.js canvas showing player positions per frame, heatmap toggle overlay, annotation marker layer on timeline. Export flow: analyst marks start/end frame, adds clip title and tags, queues for video export job
frontend.performance_notes: Three.js render loop decoupled from React render; positional data streamed from C++ addon via IPC in 500-frame chunks; heatmap grid pre-computed by C++ addon on round selection

ISS-001.title: Video clip export pipeline not designed; FFmpeg integration missing
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan defines clip_segments data model and export queue UI but does not specify how demo frames are rendered to video. FFmpeg or equivalent encoding pipeline is required; this is architecturally non-trivial in Electron and must be specified.

ISS-002.title: C++ native addon prebuilt binary distribution not specified for end users
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan assumes node-gyp compilation in CI but does not specify prebuilt binary distribution for end users who cannot compile C++ addons. Without prebuilt binaries, installation will fail for users without Visual Studio build tools or Xcode Command Line Tools.

focus_issues.title: Video clip export pipeline not designed; FFmpeg integration missing
focus_issues.detail: Clip export UI exists but no encoding pipeline specified; FFmpeg or WebAssembly encoder needed for demo-to-video rendering

focus_issues.title: C++ native addon prebuilt binary distribution not specified
focus_issues.detail: node-gyp compilation in CI does not produce prebuilt binaries for end users without C++ build tools

latest_recommendations: Design FFmpeg-based video export pipeline for clip segments: render frames from C++ parser output as PNG sequence, encode via ffmpeg subprocess in Node.js main process
Use node-pre-gyp to compile native addon in CI and publish prebuilt binaries to S3; Electron installer downloads correct platform binary without end-user compilation
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: FPS Esports Replay Analysis Tool - Initial Architecture

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues with one blocking a core feature (clip export) and one affecting installation success

previous_audits.summary: Good foundation for FPS replay analysis desktop app with correct Electron and C++ parser choices, but two significant gaps: clip export pipeline is defined at UI level only with no encoding implementation plan, and C++ native addon distribution for end users is not addressed.
strengths: Auth0 PKCE flow correctly specified for Electron desktop OAuth
S3 pre-signed URL design is appropriate for team replay sharing
Three.js WebGL heatmap approach is well-suited for positional data rendering
concerns: Clip export feature has no video encoding implementation (FFmpeg or equivalent)
C++ native addon requires end-user compilation without prebuilt binary distribution plan
blocking_issues: ISS-001: clip export is a primary differentiating feature; missing encoding pipeline makes it undeliverable
recommendations: Design FFmpeg subprocess pipeline for frame-to-video export: C++ parser renders PNG frames to temp dir, Node.js spawns ffmpeg process to encode MP4, exports to S3 with signed URL
Use node-pre-gyp with S3 binary hosting to distribute prebuilt native addons per platform/node version

issue_updates.title: FFmpeg clip export pipeline missing
issue_updates.detail: clip_segments model exists but no frame-to-video encoding pipeline designed

issue_updates.title: C++ addon prebuilt binary distribution missing
issue_updates.detail: end users without build tools cannot install addon

previous_plan.title: FPS Esports Replay Analysis Tool - Initial Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Electron 31 desktop app with C++ demo parser native addon, React 18 frontend with Three.js heatmap canvas, SQLite local annotation storage, and S3 team cloud storage integration.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Electron main process hosts C++ native addon for demo parsing, Node.js for IPC and S3 integration. React renderer with Three.js for heatmap visualization.
previous_plan.data_model: replay_files, match_metadata, player_annotations, clip_segments, heatmap_frames in local SQLite; S3 for cloud replay files.
previous_plan.workflows: Demo ingestion: analyst imports CS2 demo; C++ parser extracts frame data. Clip export: analyst marks frames, queues export job.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revision round 2 for the FPS replay analysis Electron app. Two issues need fixing: ISS-001 (missing FFmpeg clip export pipeline) and ISS-002 (C++ native addon prebuilt binary distribution). The FFmpeg fix is to design a frame-render-to-PNG-sequence pipeline in the C++ addon, with a Node.js child_process spawning ffmpeg to encode the PNG sequence as MP4. The prebuilt binary fix is node-pre-gyp with S3 binary hosting, downloading the correct platform/Node version binary at Electron install time. This is a weak quality plan: issues are addressed but sections are thin — workflows and data model are at minimum word counts and cost section lacks specifics.

fix_report.action_taken: Designed FFmpeg-based clip export pipeline: C++ addon renders selected frame range as PNG sequence to temp directory; Node.js main process spawns ffmpeg child process with input PNG glob pattern, frame rate from demo tick rate, and H.264 output MP4 codec; progress events emitted via IPC to renderer export queue UI; completed MP4 uploaded to S3 via pre-signed PUT URL
fix_report.expected_outcome: Coaches can export annotated clip segments as MP4 files shareable via S3 signed URLs; export queue shows real-time encoding progress

fix_report.action_taken: Added node-pre-gyp prebuilt binary distribution: CircleCI matrix compiles native addon for win-x64, darwin-x64, darwin-arm64, linux-x64 per Node.js 22 ABI; prebuilt binaries uploaded to S3 binary hosting bucket; Electron installer uses node-pre-gyp to download correct prebuilt binary at install time; source compilation fallback only if prebuilt unavailable
fix_report.expected_outcome: End users install without C++ build tools; correct prebuilt binary downloaded automatically for their platform and Node ABI version

target_output.title: FPS Esports Replay Analysis Tool Architecture - Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: An Electron 31 desktop application for professional FPS esports coaches enabling frame-accurate CS2 and Valorant demo analysis, heatmap visualization, and annotated clip export. Revision 2 adds an FFmpeg-based video clip export pipeline and C++ native addon prebuilt binary distribution via node-pre-gyp to resolve blocking gaps from round 1.
target_output.architecture_overview: Electron 31 app with Node.js 22 main process hosting a C++ native addon (compiled via node-pre-gyp with prebuilt platform binaries) for CS2 demo parsing. React 18 renderer with Three.js WebGL canvas for frame-accurate player position display and heatmap overlays. Node.js main process handles IPC commands, SQLite annotation storage via better-sqlite3, AWS S3 integration for team replay sharing, FFmpeg subprocess for clip video export, and Auth0 PKCE authentication. No server infrastructure; all computation is local with optional S3 cloud storage for team collaboration.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 31, React 18, TypeScript, Three.js WebGL, Zustand. Backend: C++ native addon with node-pre-gyp, Node.js 22 main process, better-sqlite3, AWS SDK v3, ffmpeg-static for bundled FFmpeg binary. Auth: Auth0 PKCE desktop flow, keytar for OS keychain token storage. Infrastructure: S3 team buckets. CI: CircleCI matrix for win-x64, darwin-x64, darwin-arm64, linux-x64. Observability: Sentry desktop SDK.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Demo Parsing: C++ addon parses 64-tick and 128-tick CS2 demo files, extracting frame-level player positions, weapon states, and kill/death events into FrameData arrays. Timeline Scrubbing: Three.js canvas renders player positions per frame at 60fps; timeline seeks to any frame in O(1) via indexed frame array. Heatmap Overlay: C++ addon pre-computes heatmap grid per player per round; Three.js renders as color gradient overlay on map image. Annotations: analysts create timed annotations with frame markers and tags stored in SQLite. Clip Export: analyst selects frame range; C++ renders PNG sequence; Node.js spawns ffmpeg to encode H.264 MP4; uploads to S3. Cloud Sharing: S3 pre-signed URL generation for team replay download and clip share.
target_output.system_components: Electron 31 main process (IPC handler, C++ addon bridge, SQLite via better-sqlite3, AWS SDK v3 S3 integration, FFmpeg child process spawner, Auth0 PKCE token exchange), C++ native addon (demo parser, frame extractor, heatmap grid computer, PNG sequence renderer), React 18 renderer (Three.js canvas, Zustand timeline state, annotation editor), bundled ffmpeg-static binary for clip encoding, SQLite database file in appDataDir, S3 team replay bucket, Sentry desktop SDK.
target_output.workflows: Demo Ingestion: analyst drags demo file onto Library view; Electron main process calls C++ addon parse_demo(path) which extracts FrameData[] and match metadata; match_metadata saved to SQLite; frame data cached in memory for active demo. Clip Export: analyst selects start and end frames on timeline, adds title and tags, clicks Export; main process calls C++ addon render_png_sequence(frame_start, frame_end, temp_dir); C++ writes frames as PNG to temp directory; main process spawns ffmpeg child process with -framerate {tick_rate} -i {temp_dir}/%04d.png -c:v libx264 -crf 18 output.mp4; IPC progress events sent to renderer export queue UI; on completion, MP4 uploaded to S3 via pre-signed PUT URL; clip_segments record updated in SQLite with s3_export_key.
target_output.data_model: replay_files (id TEXT PK, filename TEXT, game_type TEXT, demo_path_local TEXT, s3_key TEXT, uploaded_at TEXT, team_id TEXT) in SQLite. match_metadata (replay_id TEXT PK, map_name TEXT, match_date TEXT, tick_rate INT, team_a TEXT, team_b TEXT, total_frames INT) in SQLite. player_annotations (id TEXT PK, replay_id TEXT FK, player_name TEXT, frame_start INT, frame_end INT, annotation_text TEXT, tags_json TEXT, created_at TEXT) in SQLite. clip_segments (id TEXT PK, replay_id TEXT FK, frame_start INT, frame_end INT, title TEXT, s3_export_key TEXT, export_status TEXT, exported_at TEXT) in SQLite. heatmap_frames stored as JSON blobs in SQLite keyed by (replay_id, player_name, round_number).
target_output.api_design: Electron IPC commands via ipcMain: demo:parse(file_path) -> {match_metadata, total_frames}, demo:get-frames(replay_id, start, end) -> FrameData[], demo:compute-heatmap(replay_id, player_name, round_range) -> HeatmapGrid, annotations:create(annotation_data) -> annotation_id, annotations:list(replay_id) -> Annotation[], clips:export-start(clip_params) -> job_id with progress events clips:export-progress(job_id, percent), cloud:get-upload-url(filename) -> pre_signed_put_url, cloud:get-download-url(s3_key) -> pre_signed_get_url.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 PKCE flow: system browser opens to Auth0 login; callback intercepted by custom URI scheme replay-analyzer://callback; authorization code exchanged for tokens; refresh token stored in OS keychain via keytar. AWS temporary credentials derived from Auth0 JWT exchange with Cognito Identity Pool; no static AWS credentials on disk. S3 pre-signed GET and PUT URLs generated per operation with 1-hour expiry. Sentry payloads scrubbed to exclude file paths and annotation text. Electron CSP blocks remote script loading in renderer.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: CircleCI matrix build: win-x64, darwin-x64, darwin-arm64, linux-x64; each job compiles C++ native addon via node-pre-gyp, runs npm test, packages Electron binary with electron-builder; code-signs binaries (Windows code signing cert, macOS Developer ID); uploads prebuilt native addon binary to S3 binary hosting bucket; uploads installer to distribution server. Electron auto-updater checks distribution server on launch; beta channel for esports team beta program. No server deployment.
target_output.observability: Sentry desktop SDK captures Electron main process and renderer unhandled errors; C++ addon exceptions caught and forwarded to Sentry via N-API error handling. Local log in appDataDir/logs/debug.log with IPC command invocations and FFmpeg process exit codes. No remote metrics.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No server infrastructure costs. S3 storage approximately $0.023/GB/month per team bucket. CircleCI credits for cross-platform matrix builds. Distribution server static hosting via S3 and CloudFront. Total costs minimal; scales to any user count without infrastructure changes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (14 weeks): CS2 demo parsing with C++ addon, frame-accurate timeline scrubbing, Three.js heatmap, basic annotations in SQLite, S3 team sharing, Auth0 PKCE, node-pre-gyp prebuilt distribution, FFmpeg clip export. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Valorant demo format support, clip export S3 sharing with pre-signed URLs, annotation tag filtering, session log export. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Electron auto-updater stable channel, team VOD review session scaffolding.
target_output.development_guidelines: C++ addon must handle malformed demo files without crashing Node.js process; all exceptions caught and returned as structured error codes via N-API. FFmpeg child process spawned with child_process.spawn not exec; stdout/stderr piped for progress parsing. node-pre-gyp binary version bumped on any ABI-breaking C++ change. SQLite schema migrations additive-only; user_version pragma checked on startup.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: C++ addon crash corrupting Electron main process. Mitigation: C++ exceptions wrapped in N-API error handling; demo parsing runs in worker thread to isolate main process. Risk: FFmpeg clip encoding blocking Node.js event loop on large demos. Mitigation: ffmpeg spawned as child process with IPC progress callbacks; UI responsive during encoding. Tradeoff: bundled ffmpeg-static increases binary size by ~80MB but eliminates user dependency. Risk: S3 pre-signed URL expiry causing upload failure for large replay files on slow connections. Mitigation: multipart upload for files >100MB with per-part pre-signed URLs.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed ffmpeg-static bundle over requiring user to install FFmpeg separately to avoid installation complexity. Selected node-pre-gyp over napi-build for better S3 binary hosting support. Chose better-sqlite3 over sql.js for synchronous API matching Electron main process patterns. Auth0 PKCE confirmed over OAuth implicit flow per Auth0 desktop app security guidance.

project_goal.value: ML pipeline for predicting player churn in a mobile gacha game, training on engagement telemetry, monetization history, and social graph features to produce daily churn probability scores that drive targeted retention interventions via push notifications and personalized in-game offers
project_goal.rationale: Churn-driven revenue loss is the primary product motivation; automated prediction enables scaled retention at lower cost than manual segmentation

target_users.value: Internal data science and growth teams consuming churn scores; CRM and notification systems as downstream consumers; no direct player-facing interface
target_users.rationale: Internal ML system with downstream integrations

project_class.rationale: ML training, feature engineering, model serving, and automated retention intervention pipeline

capabilities.value: backend, data, ai_llm, analytics, batch_jobs, integrations
capabilities.rationale: ML pipeline with Airflow DAGs, BigQuery feature store, Vertex AI serving, and CRM integration

complexity_level.rationale: Feature engineering at scale with social graph metrics, MLflow experiment tracking, and A/B-tested intervention pipeline

risk_level.rationale: Automated profiling and targeting of players requires GDPR lawful basis and opt-out mechanisms; incorrect churn scores could drive wasteful spend

data_sensitivity.rationale: Processes personal behavioral data (session history, spend patterns) for automated profiling and targeted marketing

external_exposure.rationale: All pipeline outputs consumed internally by growth team and CRM integration; no public API

access_model.value: Workload Identity Federation for all GCP service-to-service calls; MLflow UI access via Okta SSO for data science team; BigQuery IAM roles for analyst read access
access_model.rationale: Service-account-based IAM for automated pipelines; SSO for human-facing tools

feature_scope.value: Daily Airflow feature engineering DAG, churn label generation, MLflow experiment tracking, Vertex AI model training and deployment, daily scoring batch job, retention cohort segmentation, CRM push notification and offer integration, A/B testing for intervention effectiveness, data scientist monitoring dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full ML pipeline from feature engineering to intervention delivery and effectiveness measurement

mvp_scope.value: Feature engineering DAG for engagement and spend features, logistic regression baseline model via MLflow, daily scoring to PostgreSQL, manual CRM upload of churn cohorts
mvp_scope.rationale: Validate feature quality and score accuracy before investing in automated intervention delivery

security_baseline.value: Workload Identity for all GCP service accounts; BigQuery column-level security for PII fields; all data transfers encrypted in transit; MLflow artifact store access via GCS IAM roles
security_baseline.rationale: Internal ML system handling personal behavioral data requires IAM-scoped access to all data stores

frontend_stack.value: None (internal ML pipeline); MLflow UI for experiment tracking accessed by data scientists
frontend_stack.rationale: No customer-facing frontend; MLflow provides native UI for experiment and model registry access

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with MLflow and Apache Airflow; Vertex AI for model training and serving
backend_stack.rationale: Python ML ecosystem; Airflow for DAG orchestration; Vertex AI for managed training compute

data_platform.value: BigQuery for feature store and training data; PostgreSQL 16 for churn score serving and CRM integration
data_platform.rationale: BigQuery for petabyte-scale feature computation; PostgreSQL for low-latency score lookups by the CRM integration

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Composer (managed Airflow) for DAG orchestration; Vertex AI for model training and endpoint serving
hosting_target.rationale: Managed services reduce MLOps operational burden for the small data science team

privacy_retention_policy.value: Player feature snapshots retained 90 days in BigQuery feature store; churn score records retained 180 days; raw training data retained 12 months; GDPR opt-out propagation must suppress player_id from all future feature snapshots within 24 hours
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 17 and data minimization principle for automated profiling

future_scope.value: Real-time churn scoring on session exit events via streaming pipeline, LTV prediction model alongside churn model, multi-game churn signal federation
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP pipeline enhancements

constraints.value: Daily scoring pipeline must complete within 2-hour window (midnight to 2am UTC); GDPR opt-out propagation must suppress within 24 hours; no raw PII in MLflow artifact store or model inputs -- use hashed player_id only
constraints.rationale: Operational SLA and privacy requirements

observability_baseline.value: MLflow experiment tracking for model metrics (AUC-ROC, precision at k, calibration); Grafana + Prometheus for pipeline DAG health and scoring latency; alert on pipeline SLA breach and model drift
observability_baseline.rationale: MLOps observability requires both pipeline health and model quality monitoring

execution_preference.value: Iterative weekly model updates; data science experiments in MLflow tracked branches; production model promotions require peer review and AUC-ROC gate
execution_preference.rationale: Controlled ML model promotion to prevent degraded models entering production

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 22 (automated decision-making): retention interventions are marketing, not legally significant decisions -- legitimate interest basis; opt-out list maintained in PostgreSQL; GDPR Article 17 opt-out propagation within 24 hours; lawful basis documentation stored in compliance register
compliance_context.rationale: Automated profiling and targeting requires documented lawful basis and opt-out mechanism

llm_integration.value: No LLM used for model inference; capabilities includes ai_llm because ML model training and serving is the core of the system; Vertex AI AutoML and custom training containers used for gradient boosting model (XGBoost); no generative AI APIs
llm_integration.rationale: ai_llm capability reflects ML system classification; specific implementation uses XGBoost not LLM inference

project.goal: Daily churn prediction pipeline producing player risk scores for targeted retention interventions
project.target_users: Internal data science and growth teams; CRM system as downstream consumer
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks; full automated intervention pipeline in 18 weeks

backend.framework: Apache Airflow 2.9 on Cloud Composer; MLflow 2.13 for experiment tracking; Vertex AI Training for model compute
backend.ml_stack: XGBoost 2.0 with SHAP for feature importance; scikit-learn for preprocessing pipelines

security.auth: Workload Identity Federation for GCP service accounts
security.data_access: BigQuery column-level ACLs restrict raw PII; hashed player_id used in all ML artifacts
security.pii_policy: No raw player email, device ID, or display name in training features or MLflow artifacts

data.feature_store: BigQuery dataset churn_features with daily partitioned snapshots
data.serving_db: PostgreSQL 16 (Cloud SQL) for churn_score_records readable by CRM integration

devops.ci: GitLab CI for pipeline code; model promotion via MLflow model registry with approval workflow
devops.environments: dev (manual trigger), staging (daily dry-run), production (daily 0:00 UTC trigger)

confirmed_decisions.model_algorithm: XGBoost gradient boosting (outperformed logistic regression on held-out validation)
confirmed_decisions.churn_label: No session in 14 days from the feature snapshot date

reasoner_reviews.product: The churn prediction pipeline directly addresses the growth team's need for automated retention targeting at scale. The A/B testing framework for intervention effectiveness is critical -- without it the team cannot distinguish genuine retention from coincidental re-engagement. The GDPR opt-out suppression SLA of 24 hours is a hard compliance requirement that must be enforced in the feature engineering DAG.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The BigQuery-to-Vertex AI-to-PostgreSQL data flow is well-architected for the batch scoring use case. The round 1 plan had a gap: the GDPR opt-out suppression was described as a manual process but must be automated. An opt_out_list table in PostgreSQL, synced to a BigQuery exclusion list, should be checked at the start of the feature engineering DAG to suppress opted-out player_ids before any feature snapshot is written.
reasoner_reviews.security: No raw PII in MLflow artifacts is correctly enforced. The hashed player_id approach is appropriate. However, the churn_score_records table in PostgreSQL contains player_id (hashed) alongside intervention eligibility flags -- this constitutes personal data under GDPR because it is linkable to the player via the game backend's ID mapping. Ensure BigQuery column-level ACLs and PostgreSQL row-level security restrict CRM integration access to only the fields needed for intervention delivery.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 2-hour pipeline SLA (00:00-02:00 UTC) is achievable at current DAU but BigQuery feature computation costs must be monitored at scale. Partitioned tables and clustering on player_id will keep query costs bounded. Model scoring on Vertex AI batch prediction endpoint should complete in under 45 minutes for 1M players.

backend.service_design: Three Airflow DAG groups: feature_engineering_dag (daily 00:00 UTC, extracts 90-day rolling features from BigQuery, checks opt_out_exclusion list, writes to churn_features partitioned table), model_scoring_dag (daily 00:30 UTC, loads champion model from Vertex AI endpoint, runs batch prediction on today's feature snapshot, writes churn_score_records to PostgreSQL), intervention_dispatch_dag (daily 01:00 UTC, segments scored players into retention cohorts, generates offer configurations, calls CRM REST API for push notification scheduling).
backend.background_jobs: Weekly model retraining job (Sunday 02:00 UTC): pulls last-12-month training snapshot from BigQuery, trains XGBoost with hyperparameter tuning via Vertex AI Vizier, logs to MLflow, promotes to champion if AUC-ROC > 0.75 and improvement > 0.01 over current champion.
backend.scaling_notes: Vertex AI batch prediction scales automatically by Vertex; BigQuery compute scales with slot reservations; PostgreSQL write bottleneck at scoring time mitigated by batch INSERT via COPY for churn_score_records.

data.entities: player_features (player_id_hash TEXT, snapshot_date DATE, days_since_last_session INTEGER, sessions_last_7d INTEGER, sessions_last_30d INTEGER, sessions_last_90d INTEGER, total_spend_usd REAL, spend_last_30d REAL, gacha_pulls_last_30d INTEGER, social_graph_centrality REAL, friend_count INTEGER, guild_member BOOLEAN, churn_label BOOLEAN). churn_score_records (player_id_hash TEXT, score_date DATE, churn_probability REAL, risk_tier TEXT CHECK IN (low, medium, high, critical), model_version TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). intervention_campaigns (id UUID PK, score_date DATE, risk_tier TEXT, offer_type TEXT, notification_template_id TEXT, players_targeted INTEGER, dispatched_at TIMESTAMPTZ). push_notification_events (id UUID PK, campaign_id UUID FK, player_id_hash TEXT, sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ, opened BOOLEAN, converted BOOLEAN). gdpr_opt_out_list (player_id_hash TEXT PK, opted_out_at TIMESTAMPTZ, source TEXT)
data.analytics_events: model_scoring_completed (score_date, players_scored, model_version, auc_roc_validation), intervention_dispatched (campaign_id, risk_tier, players_targeted), notification_open_rate (campaign_id, open_rate), churn_prevented_estimate (cohort, baseline_churn_rate, post_intervention_rate)
data.retention_and_deletion: player_features snapshots partitioned by snapshot_date; partitions older than 90 days deleted by daily cleanup DAG task. churn_score_records purged after 180 days. GDPR opt-out: player_id_hash added to gdpr_opt_out_list; feature_engineering_dag LEFT JOIN excludes opted-out hashes before writing any snapshot.

security.auth_design: Cloud Composer service account with Workload Identity bound to BigQuery Data Viewer on raw telemetry dataset and BigQuery Data Editor on churn_features dataset. Vertex AI service account with Storage Object Reader on MLflow GCS artifact bucket.
security.privacy_controls: BigQuery column-level security: raw player email and device_id columns in source telemetry restricted to data engineering role; churn pipeline service account cannot access these columns -- only hashed player_id. PostgreSQL row-level security on churn_score_records: CRM integration role reads only (player_id_hash, churn_probability, risk_tier, score_date); cannot access model_version or internal metadata.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: BigQuery Data Access audit logs enabled for all churn_features and churn_training_data dataset accesses. Airflow task logs retained 90 days in Cloud Logging. GDPR opt-out actions logged to BigQuery compliance_audit_events table.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Composer 2 environment in europe-west4 for data residency. Vertex AI training and batch prediction in europe-west4. BigQuery multi-region EU dataset. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 in europe-west4. MLflow tracking server on Cloud Run in europe-west4.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: on merge to main, run pytest for DAG unit tests and feature engineering logic, validate Airflow DAG with dag.test(), build Docker training container, push to Artifact Registry, trigger Cloud Composer DAG refresh. Model promotion: manual step in MLflow UI requiring data scientist approval; GitLab pipeline uploads approved model to Vertex AI endpoint.
devops.cost_controls: BigQuery flat-rate slot reservation (100 slots) to cap query costs. Vertex AI batch prediction billed per prediction; estimated $15/day at 1M players. Cloud Composer environment scaled down to 1 worker between 04:00 and 22:00 UTC.

ISS-001.title: GDPR opt-out suppression implemented as manual process -- not automated in feature engineering DAG
ISS-001.detail: The round 1 plan described maintaining a GDPR opt-out list but did not automate the suppression in the feature_engineering_dag. Opted-out players' data continues to flow into feature snapshots and churn scores until manually excluded, violating the 24-hour suppression SLA and GDPR Article 17.

ISS-002.title: No model drift detection -- champion model may degrade silently without alerting
ISS-002.detail: The pipeline trains a new model weekly and promotes on AUC-ROC improvement, but there is no runtime drift monitoring on the champion model between weekly retraining cycles. A significant game update changing player behavior could degrade churn prediction accuracy for up to 7 days before retraining detects it.

focus_issues.title: GDPR opt-out suppression not automated in feature engineering DAG
focus_issues.detail: Must automate opt-out player_id_hash exclusion at the start of the feature engineering DAG before any feature snapshot is written.

focus_issues.title: No model drift detection between weekly retraining cycles
focus_issues.detail: Champion model degrades silently after game updates; need daily PSI-based drift check on feature distribution.

latest_recommendations: Add a gdpr_opt_out_exclusion task at the start of feature_engineering_dag that exports the opt_out_list from PostgreSQL to a BigQuery exclusion table, then uses a LEFT ANTI JOIN to exclude those player_id_hashes from all feature snapshot writes

Implement daily PSI (Population Stability Index) calculation on key features (sessions_last_7d, spend_last_30d) comparing today's feature distribution against the training baseline; alert Grafana if PSI > 0.2 on any feature
