previous_plan.title: Gacha Churn Prediction Pipeline -- Round 1 Draft
previous_plan.executive_summary: Daily ML pipeline on GCP predicting gacha player churn using XGBoost trained on 90-day engagement and spend features, with Vertex AI for model training and serving, BigQuery as the feature store, and PostgreSQL for churn score serving to the CRM integration.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Cloud Composer Airflow DAGs orchestrate feature engineering in BigQuery, batch scoring on Vertex AI, and intervention dispatch via CRM API. MLflow on Cloud Run tracks experiments. Churn scores written to PostgreSQL for CRM consumption.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Workload Identity for GCP service accounts. Hashed player_id in all ML artifacts. GDPR opt-out list maintained in PostgreSQL (manual exclusion process). BigQuery column-level security on raw PII.

target_output.thinking_summary: Addressing two issues from round 1: GDPR opt-out automation requires a new DAG task that syncs the opt_out_list from PostgreSQL to a BigQuery exclusion table before any feature snapshot writes, using a LEFT ANTI JOIN. Model drift detection requires a daily PSI calculation task in the scoring DAG comparing current feature distributions against training baseline stored in BigQuery. Both are additive changes to existing DAG definitions without architectural restructuring.

fix_report.action_taken: Added gdpr_opt_out_exclusion task as the first task in feature_engineering_dag: exports gdpr_opt_out_list from PostgreSQL to BigQuery temporary table opt_out_exclusion using BigQueryInsertJobOperator; all downstream feature extraction queries use LEFT ANTI JOIN ON player_id_hash NOT IN (SELECT player_id_hash FROM opt_out_exclusion) before writing to churn_features partition; task runs atomically before any feature data is written, guaranteeing opted-out players are excluded within the next daily cycle (max 24 hours from opt-out event)
fix_report.expected_outcome: GDPR opt-out suppression is automated and enforced at the BigQuery query layer before any feature snapshot is written; opted-out players cannot appear in any future churn_features partition, churn_score_records, or intervention campaigns; suppression occurs within 24 hours of opt-out as required

fix_report.action_taken: Added drift_detection task to model_scoring_dag executing after feature snapshot is written: computes PSI for features sessions_last_7d, spend_last_30d, gacha_pulls_last_30d, and social_graph_centrality by comparing today's feature distribution percentile buckets against a training_baseline_distribution table in BigQuery (populated at last model promotion); PSI values written to model_drift_metrics in BigQuery; if any feature PSI > 0.2, Grafana alert fires; if any feature PSI > 0.35, Airflow triggers an out-of-cycle retraining job
fix_report.expected_outcome: Model performance degradation caused by game updates or player behavior shifts is detected within 24 hours via PSI alerting; severe drift (PSI > 0.35) triggers immediate retraining rather than waiting until the weekly Sunday cycle

target_output.title: Gacha Churn Prediction Pipeline -- Compliant Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: Daily ML pipeline on GCP predicting gacha player churn using XGBoost trained on 90-day engagement, spend, and social graph features, with automated GDPR opt-out suppression at the BigQuery feature engineering layer, daily PSI-based model drift detection, and automated intervention dispatch via CRM API for targeted push notifications and personalized offers. Vertex AI handles training and batch scoring; MLflow tracks all experiments with an AUC-ROC promotion gate.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three Cloud Composer Airflow DAG groups orchestrate the daily pipeline. feature_engineering_dag (00:00 UTC): syncs GDPR opt-out exclusion list from PostgreSQL to BigQuery, runs LEFT ANTI JOIN exclusion, computes 90-day rolling engagement, spend, and social graph centrality features from raw telemetry, writes to churn_features partitioned table. model_scoring_dag (00:30 UTC): loads champion XGBoost model from Vertex AI endpoint, runs batch prediction on today's feature snapshot, writes churn_score_records to PostgreSQL, then computes PSI drift metrics comparing today's feature distribution against the training baseline; alerts Grafana if PSI > 0.2 on any monitored feature. intervention_dispatch_dag (01:00 UTC): segments players by risk tier, generates offer configurations for high/critical risk cohorts, and dispatches push notification payloads to the CRM integration REST API. All ML artifacts in MLflow GCS artifact store use hashed player_id with no raw PII. Weekly XGBoost retraining on Vertex AI with MLflow experiment tracking; production promotion requires AUC-ROC > 0.75 and peer review.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Apache Airflow 2.9 on Cloud Composer 2. ML framework: XGBoost 2.0, scikit-learn preprocessing pipelines, SHAP 0.45 for feature importance. Model tracking: MLflow 2.13 on Cloud Run. Feature store: BigQuery (EU multi-region). Model training and serving: Vertex AI Training (custom containers) + Vertex AI Batch Prediction. Score serving DB: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16. Drift monitoring: PSI computed in BigQuery SQL; metrics in BigQuery model_drift_metrics table. Observability: Grafana + Prometheus (pipeline health); MLflow (model metrics). CI/CD: GitLab CI. GCP region: europe-west4 for data residency.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Feature Engineering: daily BigQuery SQL computes sessions_last_7d/30d/90d, total_spend_usd, spend_last_30d, gacha_pulls_last_30d, friend_count, guild_member, social_graph_centrality (PageRank on friendship graph) per player_id_hash; opted-out players excluded before any write. Churn Label Generation: Boolean label from no-session-in-14-days condition on training data window. Model Training: Vertex AI custom training container runs XGBoost with stratified 80/10/10 train/val/test split; MLflow logs AUC-ROC, precision@0.5, calibration curve per experiment; champion promotion on AUC-ROC gate with peer review. Batch Scoring: Vertex AI batch prediction on daily feature snapshot; churn_probability and risk_tier written to PostgreSQL. Drift Detection: daily PSI on 4 key features against training baseline; alert + auto-retraining on PSI thresholds. Intervention Dispatch: high-risk (churn_probability > 0.7) and critical-risk (> 0.9) cohorts receive personalized offer notifications via CRM API.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Composer 2 (Airflow DAG orchestration), BigQuery EU multi-region (churn_features, churn_training_data, model_drift_metrics, opt_out_exclusion, compliance_audit_events), Vertex AI Training (custom XGBoost containers), Vertex AI Batch Prediction (champion model endpoint), Cloud Run MLflow server (experiment tracking + model registry), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (churn_score_records, intervention_campaigns, gdpr_opt_out_list), CRM REST API (push notification + offer dispatch), Grafana + Prometheus (pipeline and drift alerting), GCS artifact store (MLflow models, training data exports).
target_output.workflows: Daily Feature Engineering with GDPR Opt-Out Suppression: at 00:00 UTC the feature_engineering_dag triggers; task 1 (gdpr_opt_out_exclusion) exports SELECT player_id_hash FROM gdpr_opt_out_list from PostgreSQL into BigQuery temp table opt_out_exclusion_{date}; task 2 (compute_features) runs BigQuery SQL extracting 90-day rolling features from raw_telemetry dataset with LEFT ANTI JOIN ON t.player_id_hash NOT IN (SELECT player_id_hash FROM opt_out_exclusion_{date}), writing the result to churn_features PARTITION(snapshot_date=today); opted-out players produce zero rows in the feature snapshot and will not appear in scoring or interventions. Daily Scoring with Drift Detection: at 00:30 UTC model_scoring_dag triggers; task 1 (batch_predict) loads today's churn_features partition, submits to Vertex AI Batch Prediction endpoint, writes churn_probability and risk_tier to churn_score_records in PostgreSQL; task 2 (drift_check) computes PSI for sessions_last_7d, spend_last_30d, gacha_pulls_last_30d, social_graph_centrality by comparing today's decile distributions against training_baseline_distribution table; PSI values written to model_drift_metrics; if any PSI > 0.2, Grafana alert fires to data science Slack channel; if any PSI > 0.35, TriggerDagRunOperator fires retraining_dag immediately. Intervention Dispatch: at 01:00 UTC intervention_dispatch_dag segments churn_score_records into risk tiers (low <0.3, medium 0.3-0.5, high 0.5-0.7, critical >0.7); for high and critical tiers, generates offer payloads from offer_template library (matched by playstyle segment from player_features), writes intervention_campaign record, and calls CRM REST API POST /campaigns/dispatch with player_id_hash list and offer configuration.
target_output.data_model: player_features (player_id_hash TEXT, snapshot_date DATE, days_since_last_session INTEGER, sessions_last_7d INTEGER, sessions_last_30d INTEGER, sessions_last_90d INTEGER, total_spend_usd REAL, spend_last_30d REAL, gacha_pulls_last_30d INTEGER, social_graph_centrality REAL, friend_count INTEGER, guild_member BOOLEAN, churn_label BOOLEAN) -- BigQuery partitioned by snapshot_date, clustered by player_id_hash. churn_score_records (player_id_hash TEXT, score_date DATE, churn_probability REAL, risk_tier TEXT CHECK IN (low, medium, high, critical), model_version TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ) -- PostgreSQL. intervention_campaigns (id UUID PK, score_date DATE, risk_tier TEXT, offer_type TEXT, notification_template_id TEXT, players_targeted INTEGER, dispatched_at TIMESTAMPTZ). push_notification_events (id UUID PK, campaign_id UUID FK, player_id_hash TEXT, sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ, opened BOOLEAN, converted BOOLEAN). gdpr_opt_out_list (player_id_hash TEXT PK, opted_out_at TIMESTAMPTZ, source TEXT) -- PostgreSQL. model_drift_metrics (score_date DATE, feature_name TEXT, psi_value REAL, alert_triggered BOOLEAN) -- BigQuery. training_baseline_distribution (feature_name TEXT, decile INTEGER, boundary_value REAL, model_version TEXT) -- BigQuery.
target_output.api_design: No public API. Internal interfaces: Cloud Composer DAG HTTP REST trigger for ad-hoc pipeline runs (authenticated via IAP). MLflow REST API for experiment queries and model registry operations (authenticated via Okta SSO). PostgreSQL churn_score_records read by CRM integration via IAM-authorized Cloud SQL connector; CRM reads (player_id_hash, churn_probability, risk_tier, score_date) columns only via row-level security policy. CRM integration: POST /campaigns/dispatch {player_id_hashes: string[], offer_config: object, notification_template_id: string} -- internal endpoint authenticated via service account HMAC signature. GDPR opt-out: game backend calls POST /gdpr/opt-out {player_id_hash: string} which inserts to gdpr_opt_out_list and logs to compliance_audit_events.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Workload Identity Federation: Cloud Composer service account bound to BigQuery Data Editor on churn_features, churn_training_data; PostgreSQL read access via IAM database user. No raw PII (email, device_id, display_name) in any feature snapshot or MLflow artifact -- hashed player_id only. BigQuery column-level security: data engineering role required to access raw_telemetry.email and raw_telemetry.device_id; churn pipeline service account cannot access these columns. GDPR opt-out automation: gdpr_opt_out_list drives LEFT ANTI JOIN exclusion at start of every feature_engineering_dag run; suppression guaranteed within 24-hour daily cycle. GDPR Article 22 compliance: churn interventions are marketing communications, not legally significant automated decisions; legitimate interest lawful basis documented in compliance register; opt-out mechanism provides objection right. compliance_audit_events table in BigQuery logs all opt-out events, pipeline runs processing personal data, and model promotion decisions.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Composer 2 environment in europe-west4 with autoscaling 1-3 workers; scaled to 1 worker 04:00-22:00 UTC. Vertex AI Training: custom Docker containers in Artifact Registry; Vertex AI Batch Prediction endpoint version-managed with champion/challenger pattern. MLflow on Cloud Run in europe-west4 with GCS artifact store. GitLab CI: on merge to main, run pytest for DAG logic and feature engineering unit tests, validate DAG structure with dag.test(), build and push training container to Artifact Registry, refresh Cloud Composer DAG via Cloud Storage sync. Model promotion: data scientist approves in MLflow UI, GitLab job deploys new model version to Vertex AI endpoint, runs smoke test prediction against held-out validation batch, flips traffic to new version on success.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboard: Airflow DAG success/failure rate per DAG, task duration p90, Vertex AI batch prediction job duration, CRM dispatch success rate, model_drift_metrics PSI time series per feature. Prometheus: DAG run counts, task retry counts, PostgreSQL write latency for churn_score_records batch INSERT. Alerts: daily pipeline SLA breach (02:00 UTC gate not met) -> PagerDuty P2; feature PSI > 0.2 -> Slack data science channel; feature PSI > 0.35 -> PagerDuty P2 + auto-retraining trigger; GDPR opt-out processing failure -> PagerDuty P1. MLflow experiment dashboard: AUC-ROC trend per weekly training run, precision@0.5, calibration plot, SHAP feature importance heatmap.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $1,200/month at 2M active players: BigQuery flat-rate 100 slots ($200), Vertex AI batch prediction ~15k predictions/dollar ($45/day = $1,350/month at 2M scored -- offset by slot commitment discount), Cloud Composer 2 ($200/month), Cloud Run MLflow ($30), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL db.g1-small ($80). Context: PSI computation in BigQuery SQL adds <5 minutes to pipeline runtime and no significant cost. GDPR opt-out LEFT ANTI JOIN adds <1 minute compute overhead at opt_out_list sizes up to 100K entries.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): feature_engineering_dag with GDPR opt-out exclusion task, logistic regression baseline model via MLflow, batch scoring to PostgreSQL, manual CRM cohort upload, BigQuery column-level security, GitLab CI for DAG unit tests. Phase 2 (8 weeks): XGBoost model with hyperparameter tuning on Vertex AI, daily PSI drift detection with Grafana alerting, automated intervention_dispatch_dag with CRM API integration, MLflow model registry with AUC-ROC promotion gate, weekly retraining job with auto-trigger on PSI > 0.35. Phase 3: real-time churn scoring on session exit via Pub/Sub streaming pipeline, LTV prediction model alongside churn model, multi-game churn signal federation.
target_output.development_guidelines: All BigQuery SQL in feature_engineering_dag tested with pytest using BigQuery emulator; GDPR opt-out exclusion tested with synthetic opt_out_list containing known player_id_hashes and asserting they are absent from feature snapshot output. PSI calculation tested with known distribution shifts and expected PSI values verified against manual computation. XGBoost training containers tested with small synthetic dataset asserting MLflow run created with AUC-ROC metric logged. No raw PII field names (email, device_id) permitted in any DAG Python file or BigQuery SQL file -- enforced by pre-commit grep hook. Model artifacts in GCS inspected by CI to assert no PII column names in feature schema metadata.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: BigQuery slot contention during feature engineering if other teams run heavy analytical queries in the 00:00-02:00 UTC window. Mitigation: reserve flat-rate 100 BigQuery slots for churn pipeline project; other teams use on-demand pricing. Tradeoff: XGBoost batch prediction over real-time scoring -- chosen for simplicity and cost at current DAU; real-time session-exit scoring deferred to Phase 3 when the latency improvement justifies the Pub/Sub streaming complexity. Risk: CRM API unavailability at intervention dispatch time causes missed retention campaigns. Mitigation: intervention_dispatch_dag retries CRM calls up to 3 times with 5-minute exponential backoff; failed dispatches logged to intervention_campaigns.dispatch_error for next-day retry.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: GDPR opt-out suppression is now fully automated in feature_engineering_dag via LEFT ANTI JOIN at BigQuery query time -- no manual process required. Model drift detection implemented as daily PSI calculation in model_scoring_dag with Grafana alerting and auto-retraining trigger on PSI > 0.35. Confirmed no raw PII flows into any ML artifact -- hashed player_id is the only player identifier in churn features, MLflow experiments, and churn score records.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision of gacha churn ML pipeline; automates GDPR opt-out suppression in Airflow DAG and adds daily PSI model drift detection; strong quality throughout.

project_goal.value: A Go SDK for game backend developers providing cryptographically secure session token issuance, refresh rotation, and revocation for player authentication flows, with built-in replay attack prevention and hardware-backed key storage support
project_goal.rationale: Game studios need a production-grade auth token library with replay protection that integrates with their existing player databases without vendor lock-in

target_users.value: Go-based game backend developers at partner studios integrating the SDK into their player authentication services
target_users.rationale: Partner game studios are the consumers; they embed the SDK and provide their own storage implementations

project_class.rationale: Deliverable is a Go module package consumed by partner backends, not a hosted service

capabilities.rationale: SDK provides auth primitives, token data structures, and storage interface abstractions; no frontend or hosting

complexity_level.rationale: Replay attack prevention with nonce stores, hardware-backed key storage abstraction, and rotation chain integrity require advanced cryptographic implementation

risk_level.rationale: SDK handles session tokens for production game services; bugs in token issuance or validation logic have direct security impact on partner studios

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK handles cryptographic key material descriptors and session tokens encoding player identity; classified confidential under partner agreements

external_exposure.rationale: SDK is distributed to partner studios via Go module registry; API surface is a public contract with partner-facing semver stability guarantees

access_model.value: SDK consumers implement the RevocationStore and KeyMaterialProvider interfaces; SDK itself has no runtime access model; module signing via Go module proxy ensures tamper detection
access_model.rationale: Library SDK pattern — consumers own their own access model; SDK provides the cryptographic primitives

feature_scope.value: Token issuance (access token plus refresh token pair with device fingerprint binding), rotation chain validation and atomic refresh, nonce-based replay attack prevention, revocation store interface with Redis and in-memory reference implementations, hardware key storage interface with AWS KMS adapter, token claims accessor API, rotation policy configuration
feature_scope.rationale: Full production-grade auth token SDK scope

mvp_scope.value: Token issuance and validation, HMAC-SHA256 signing, revocation store interface with in-memory reference implementation, nonce replay prevention; KMS adapter and rotation policies in Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Core issuance and validation unblocks partner studio integration pilots

security_baseline.value: HMAC-SHA256 minimum; Ed25519 preferred for production; refresh token chains bound to device fingerprint hash; nonces stored in consumer-provided RevocationStore with TTL derived from access token expiry plus clock skew tolerance; constant-time comparison for all token validation to prevent timing attacks
security_baseline.rationale: Production auth library must resist timing attacks and replay attacks by design

frontend_stack.value: None; Go library package with no UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Library SDK has no frontend

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 standard library only (crypto/ed25519, crypto/hmac, crypto/rand, crypto/subtle) with zero external runtime dependencies
backend_stack.rationale: Zero external runtime dependencies is a hard requirement to avoid transitive dependency risk in partner production services

data_platform.value: No built-in data platform; SDK defines RevocationStore and KeyMaterialProvider interfaces; ships Redis and in-memory reference implementations as optional sub-packages with their own go.mod
data_platform.rationale: Consumer provides storage implementation to avoid locking partner studios into specific infrastructure

hosting_target.value: Go module registry (pkg.go.dev); distributed as github.com/studio/gamesession-sdk via GOPROXY
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Go module distribution; no hosted deployment

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK itself stores no data; consumers own retention policy for their RevocationStore; session tokens must not encode directly identifiable PII — use opaque player_id references per GDPR guidance
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR compliance guidance for partner integrations

future_scope.value: PASETO v4 token format support, GCP CKMS adapter, automatic key rotation scheduler, OpenTelemetry trace span emission
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap items pending stable v1.0 release

constraints.value: Zero external runtime dependencies (Go stdlib only for core package); must support Go 1.21 or later; API surface must be semver stable at v1; all cryptographic operations must use constant-time comparison
constraints.rationale: Partner studios require stability guarantees and no transitive dependency conflicts

observability_baseline.value: SDK emits no telemetry by default; optional MetricsHook interface allows consumers to instrument token issuance, rotation, and revocation rates via their own metrics client; Prometheus reference implementation in sub-package
observability_baseline.rationale: Observability is consumer responsibility; hooks prevent coupling to a specific metrics library

execution_preference.value: Iterative development with fuzzing tests for token parser; each interface published with a conformance test suite consumers run against their implementations
execution_preference.rationale: Fuzzing catches parser edge cases; conformance suites ensure correct RevocationStore implementations

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II: SDK development pipeline must have code review for all cryptographic changes, gosec static analysis, and signed module releases. GDPR: documentation must advise partners against encoding directly identifiable player data in token claims
compliance_context.rationale: SOC 2 covers SDK as a production security control; GDPR guidance protects partner studios from compliance risk

project.goal: Production-grade Go SDK for player session token issuance, rotation, and revocation with replay prevention
project.target_users: Partner studio Go backend developers
project.timeline: v1.0 in 10 weeks

security.auth: Ed25519 signing, HMAC-SHA256 fallback, constant-time comparison

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with gosec and fuzz

constraints.zero_deps: Go stdlib only for core package

open_questions.paseto: PASETO v4 in v1 or roadmap?

reasoner_reviews.product: The v1 API must be locked before partner studios begin integrating. The conformance test suite for RevocationStore is essential — it prevents partners shipping broken implementations that create replay vulnerabilities. PASETO v4 should be roadmap-only.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The interface-based RevocationStore and KeyMaterialProvider design is correct for a library SDK. Nonce storage must hold (nonce, expiry) pairs so expired entries can be pruned. Ed25519 is preferred for asymmetric verification; HMAC appropriate for symmetric server-to-server tokens. NonceTTL must be derived from AccessTTL plus ClockSkewTolerance, not a fixed value.
reasoner_reviews.security: Constant-time comparison via subtle.ConstantTimeCompare is mandatory throughout. Error messages must never contain token bytes or key material. NonceTTL must equal at least access_token_expiry plus clock_skew_tolerance (5 minutes recommended). Key material must never be returned as raw bytes from KeyMaterialProvider — use crypto.Signer and crypto.Verifier interfaces exclusively.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero external runtime dependencies requires Redis reference implementation in a separate sub-package with its own go.mod. This sub-package separation was a gap in Round 2 and is addressed in this revision.

backend.service_design: Core package (gamesession-sdk): Issuer struct with IssueTokenPair, RotateTokenPair, RevokeSession, ValidateAccessToken. RevocationStore interface: CheckAndStoreNonce(ctx, nonce, expiry), Revoke(ctx, chain_id), IsRevoked(ctx, chain_id). KeyMaterialProvider interface: SigningKey(ctx, key_id) returning crypto.Signer, VerifyingKey(ctx, key_id) returning crypto.PublicKey. Sub-packages with own go.mod: storage/memory (test only), storage/redis, kms/awskms, metrics/prometheus.
backend.api_patterns: Functional options pattern for Issuer configuration: WithRotationPolicy, WithSigningKeyID, WithMetricsHook. All methods return (result, error) with sentinel error types: ErrTokenExpired, ErrNonceReplayed, ErrChainRevoked, ErrInvalidSignature, ErrRevocationStoreUnavailable.
backend.failure_handling: RevocationStore calls use context with deadline. If store unavailable during ValidateAccessToken, SDK returns ErrRevocationStoreUnavailable — consumer decides fail-open or fail-closed per their security policy. No panic paths in public API.
backend.backend_risks: Consumer implementing RevocationStore without atomic CheckAndStoreNonce creates parallel refresh replay vulnerability. Mitigation: conformance test suite includes 100-goroutine concurrent nonce check test detecting non-atomic implementations.

security.auth_design: Access tokens: Ed25519-signed with sub (opaque player_id), jti (nonce UUID via crypto/rand), iat, exp, kid claims. Refresh tokens: HMAC-SHA256 with chain_id (UUID from first issuance), device_fp (SHA256 of device fingerprint), prev_jti, exp. Token pair issued atomically with same key_id.
security.secrets_management: KeyMaterialProvider returns crypto.Signer from SigningKey — private key never leaves KMS for AWS KMS adapter. Issuer calls Signer.Sign() without accessing raw key bytes. Local HMAC key wrapped in unexported keyring struct.
security.privacy_controls: Token claims accessor AccessClaims.Extra(key) method prevents raw map access. SDK documentation explicitly states token Sub field must be opaque UUID, not plaintext player email or username.
security.security_risks: If consumer uses bytes.Equal for token comparison instead of SDK validator, timing attack is possible. Mitigation: SDK validator methods are not decomposable — all comparison happens internally via subtle.ConstantTimeCompare.

data.entities: TokenPair (AccessToken, RefreshToken strings, AccessExpiry, RefreshExpiry times). AccessClaims (Sub, JTI strings, IssuedAt, Expiry times, KeyID, extra map unexported). RefreshChain (ChainID, DeviceFP, PrevJTI strings, Expiry time, KeyID). KeyMaterialDescriptor (KeyID, Algorithm strings, CreatedAt time — no RawKey field). RotationPolicy (AccessTTL, RefreshTTL, MaxChainDepth, ClockSkewTolerance) with NonceTTL() method.
data.schema_notes: All token data structures are value types. Zero-value RotationPolicy is rejected by Issuer constructor. ChainID is a v4 UUID generated at first issuance, immutable for refresh chain lifetime.
data.data_risks: In-memory RevocationStore loses nonces on process restart, enabling replay of unexpired tokens. Documentation prominently warns in-memory store is test-only; production requires persistent RevocationStore.

devops.deployment_topology: No hosted infrastructure. GitHub repository with protected main branch. Security-critical paths (core/issuer.go, core/token.go, kms/) require 2-reviewer PR approval.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./... with race detector, go vet, gosec, staticcheck, go test -fuzz on token parser for 60 seconds. On semver tag: go mod tidy verification, cosign keyless signing, GitHub Release with CHANGELOG.
devops.observability_stack: MetricsHook interface: TokenIssued(ctx, algorithm), TokenRotated(ctx), SessionRevoked(ctx), NonceCacheHit(ctx, op), NonceCacheMiss(ctx, op). Prometheus reference hook in metrics/prometheus sub-package.
devops.rollback_strategy: Consumers pin to semver version. Security patches released same-day via patch version. Breaking changes require v2 module path.

ISS-001.title: Redis reference implementation coupled to core module go.mod
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan placed Redis client dependency in core go.mod, violating zero-dependency constraint

ISS-002.title: Nonce TTL not bounded by access token expiry plus clock skew
ISS-002.detail: Fixed 15-minute NonceTTL independent of AccessTTL created replay window when nonces expired before the tokens they protected

ISS-003.title: KeyMaterialProvider interface exposes raw key bytes via exported field
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 KeyMaterialDescriptor had RawKey []byte exported field, exposing key material to consumers and creating accidental logging risk

focus_issues.title: Nonce TTL not bounded by access token expiry plus clock skew
focus_issues.detail: NonceTTL must be AccessTTL + ClockSkewTolerance to guarantee nonce outlives the token

focus_issues.title: KeyMaterialProvider interface exposes raw key bytes via exported field
focus_issues.detail: KeyMaterialDescriptor must not contain RawKey field; use crypto.Signer/crypto.PublicKey interfaces only

latest_recommendations: Derive nonce TTL from RotationPolicy.AccessTTL plus ClockSkewTolerance as a computed method
Remove RawKey from KeyMaterialDescriptor and return crypto.Signer/crypto.PublicKey from KeyMaterialProvider
Add concurrent CheckAndStoreNonce conformance test

previous_plan.title: Game Session Auth SDK Round 2 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go SDK for player session token issuance with Ed25519 signing and RevocationStore interface. Redis extracted to sub-package in Round 2 but RawKey field and fixed NonceTTL remain as flaws.

best_plan.title: Game Session Auth SDK Round 2 Architecture
best_plan.executive_summary: Best plan is Round 2; Round 3 not yet completed at time of previous_plan capture.
best_plan.security_and_compliance: Same as previous_plan — nonce TTL and key material flaws unresolved.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 resolves ISS-003 (KeyMaterialDescriptor.RawKey exposes key bytes) and ISS-002 (fixed NonceTTL creates replay window). For ISS-003, replacing RawKey with crypto.Signer/crypto.PublicKey returns from KeyMaterialProvider makes raw key bytes inaccessible through the type system. For ISS-002, replacing the fixed NonceTTL field with a NonceTTL() computed method on RotationPolicy that returns AccessTTL plus ClockSkewTolerance ensures nonces always outlive the tokens they protect under any consumer-configured TTL.

fix_report.action_taken: Removed RawKey []byte field from KeyMaterialDescriptor struct. KeyMaterialProvider.SigningKey now returns (crypto.Signer, KeyMaterialDescriptor, error); VerifyingKey returns (crypto.PublicKey, KeyMaterialDescriptor, error). Issuer calls Signer.Sign() directly without accessing raw bytes. KeyMaterialDescriptor is now a metadata-only struct with KeyID, Algorithm, and CreatedAt fields.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Raw private key bytes are inaccessible through any SDK public API; accidental logging of key material is prevented at the type system level

fix_report.action_taken: Removed fixed NonceTTL time.Duration field from RotationPolicy. Added NonceTTL() method that returns p.AccessTTL plus p.ClockSkewTolerance (default ClockSkewTolerance 5 minutes if zero). Issuer.IssueTokenPair passes policy.NonceTTL() to RevocationStore.CheckAndStoreNonce. Updated conformance test to assert nonce expiry is always greater than or equal to access token expiry.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Nonce lifetime is always at least as long as the access token it protects plus clock skew tolerance; replay window eliminated for all valid consumer RotationPolicy configurations

target_output.title: Game Session Auth SDK v1.0 Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A production-grade Go SDK with zero external runtime dependencies providing Ed25519-signed player session token issuance, atomic refresh rotation with chain integrity validation, nonce-based replay prevention with TTL derived from RotationPolicy.NonceTTL(), and hardware-backed key storage via crypto.Signer and crypto.PublicKey interfaces. Distributed via Go module registry with isolated sub-packages for Redis RevocationStore, AWS KMS adapter, and Prometheus MetricsHook. Round 3 resolves critical RawKey exposure (ISS-003) and nonce TTL replay vulnerability (ISS-002).
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure Go library with a central Issuer struct that orchestrates token issuance, rotation, and revocation. Core package uses only Go stdlib cryptographic primitives: crypto/ed25519 for access token signing, crypto/hmac with crypto/sha256 for refresh token signing, crypto/rand for nonce generation, and crypto/subtle for all comparison operations. Key material is accessed exclusively through crypto.Signer and crypto.PublicKey interface types returned by KeyMaterialProvider — raw private key bytes are never passed across any API boundary. NonceTTL is computed as RotationPolicy.AccessTTL plus RotationPolicy.ClockSkewTolerance (defaulting to 5 minutes), guaranteeing nonces outlive the tokens they protect. Sub-packages (storage/redis, kms/awskms, metrics/prometheus) each carry their own go.mod to avoid transitive dependency pollution of partner service builds.
target_output.technology_stack: Core: Go 1.22, stdlib only (crypto/ed25519, crypto/hmac, crypto/rand, crypto/subtle, encoding/base64). Sub-packages: storage/redis using go-redis/v9 (own go.mod), kms/awskms using aws-sdk-go-v2/kms (own go.mod), metrics/prometheus using prometheus/client_golang (own go.mod). Testing: go test with -race flag, go test -fuzz for token parser corpus, gosec, staticcheck. Distribution: Go module registry, cosign keyless Sigstore signing on release tags.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Token Issuance: IssueTokenPair(ctx, sub, device_fp, claims) generates jti UUID via crypto/rand, calls store.CheckAndStoreNonce with TTL from policy.NonceTTL(), signs access token via Signer.Sign(), signs refresh with HMAC and chain_id plus device_fp binding, returns TokenPair. Refresh Rotation: RotateTokenPair(ctx, refresh_token, device_fp) validates chain_id not revoked, verifies device_fp SHA256 matches binding, checks prev_jti nonce freshness, issues new token pair atomically. Session Revocation: RevokeSession(ctx, chain_id) calls store.Revoke, invalidating all future refresh attempts for that chain. Access Validation: ValidateAccessToken(ctx, token) verifies Ed25519 signature via VerifyingKey, checks jti freshness in store, returns AccessClaims. Conformance Suite: shared test cases in gamesession-sdk/conformance including concurrent CheckAndStoreNonce atomicity test.
target_output.system_components: gamesession-sdk core: Issuer, TokenPair, AccessClaims, RefreshChain, KeyMaterialDescriptor (KeyID, Algorithm, CreatedAt only — no RawKey), RotationPolicy with NonceTTL() method, RevocationStore interface, KeyMaterialProvider interface, sentinel error vars. gamesession-sdk/storage/memory: in-memory RevocationStore for testing (warns in GoDoc: not for production). gamesession-sdk/storage/redis: Redis RevocationStore using sorted sets with ZADD score as Unix expiry for atomic nonce checks and range-based pruning. gamesession-sdk/kms/awskms: AWS KMS KeyMaterialProvider calling kms:Sign and kms:GetPublicKey. gamesession-sdk/metrics/prometheus: Prometheus MetricsHook with counters for token issuance, rotation, revocation, nonce hit/miss. gamesession-sdk/conformance: public conformance test suite.
target_output.workflows: Token Issuance Flow: consumer calls issuer.IssueTokenPair(ctx, player_uuid, device_fp_hash, extra_claims); SDK calls crypto/rand.Read for 16-byte jti, encodes as base64url; computes nonce_expiry as time.Now().Add(policy.NonceTTL()); calls store.CheckAndStoreNonce(ctx, jti, nonce_expiry) — returns ErrNonceReplayed on UUID collision (astronomically rare but handled); calls provider.SigningKey(ctx, keyID) for crypto.Signer; signs access_claims bytes with Signer.Sign(); computes device_fp_sha256; signs refresh_claims bytes with HMAC; returns TokenPair. Rotation Flow: client presents refresh token on access token expiry; SDK validates chain_id via store.IsRevoked; verifies device fingerprint SHA256 matches stored binding to reject stolen-token replay from different device; checks prev_jti via CheckAndStoreNonce to prevent parallel refresh race; issues new TokenPair; old chain remains valid for concurrent requests until its own expiry. Conformance Testing: implementors run go test github.com/studio/gamesession-sdk/conformance -run TestRevocationStore injecting their store; concurrent test spins 100 goroutines calling CheckAndStoreNonce with same nonce to verify exactly one succeeds.
target_output.data_model: TokenPair struct {AccessToken string; RefreshToken string; AccessExpiry time.Time; RefreshExpiry time.Time}. AccessClaims struct {Sub string; JTI string; IssuedAt time.Time; Expiry time.Time; KeyID string; extra map[string]string (unexported, accessed via Extra(key string) string)}. RefreshChain struct {ChainID string; DeviceFP string; PrevJTI string; Expiry time.Time; KeyID string}. KeyMaterialDescriptor struct {KeyID string; Algorithm string; CreatedAt time.Time} — RawKey field removed in Round 3; no raw key bytes accessible through public API. RotationPolicy struct {AccessTTL time.Duration; RefreshTTL time.Duration; MaxChainDepth int; ClockSkewTolerance time.Duration}; NonceTTL() time.Duration method returns p.AccessTTL + p.ClockSkewTolerance (ClockSkewTolerance defaults to 5*time.Minute if zero). RevocationStore interface: CheckAndStoreNonce(ctx context.Context, nonce string, expiry time.Time) error; Revoke(ctx context.Context, chainID string) error; IsRevoked(ctx context.Context, chainID string) (bool, error). KeyMaterialProvider interface: SigningKey(ctx context.Context, keyID string) (crypto.Signer, KeyMaterialDescriptor, error); VerifyingKey(ctx context.Context, keyID string) (crypto.PublicKey, KeyMaterialDescriptor, error).
target_output.api_design: Issuer constructor: New(store RevocationStore, keys KeyMaterialProvider, opts ...Option) (*Issuer, error). Options: WithRotationPolicy(p RotationPolicy); WithSigningKeyID(keyID string); WithSigningTimeout(d time.Duration); WithMetricsHook(h MetricsHook). Issuer methods: IssueTokenPair(ctx, sub, deviceFP string, claims map[string]string) (TokenPair, error); RotateTokenPair(ctx, refreshToken, deviceFP string) (TokenPair, error); RevokeSession(ctx, chainID string) error; ValidateAccessToken(ctx, token string) (AccessClaims, error). Sentinel errors: var ErrTokenExpired, ErrNonceReplayed, ErrChainRevoked, ErrInvalidSignature, ErrInvalidDeviceFingerprint, ErrRevocationStoreUnavailable error — each is a distinct unexported type implementing error interface. No token bytes or key material in any error message string. Redis RevocationStore: nonces stored with ZADD key=nonce:{token-space} score=unix_expiry; CheckAndStoreNonce uses MULTI/EXEC transaction for atomicity; ZREMRANGEBYSCORE prunes expired nonces on each write.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cryptographic controls: Ed25519 via crypto/ed25519 for access tokens; HMAC-SHA256 via crypto/hmac for refresh tokens; all signature verification uses crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare exclusively — no bytes.Equal anywhere in validation paths. Key material isolation (ISS-003 fix): KeyMaterialProvider.SigningKey returns crypto.Signer; VerifyingKey returns crypto.PublicKey — private key bytes are never held or passed by the SDK; AWS KMS adapter calls kms:Sign remotely, private key never leaves HSM boundary. Replay prevention (ISS-002 fix): jti nonce TTL = policy.NonceTTL() = AccessTTL + ClockSkewTolerance, ensuring nonce is valid for at least as long as the token it protects plus 5 minutes of clock drift. SOC 2 Type II: all cryptographic file changes require 2-reviewer GitHub PR approval; gosec scans every PR with zero warnings policy; module releases signed with cosign keyless Sigstore. GDPR: token Sub field documented as requiring opaque UUID; AccessClaims.Extra accessor prevents raw map access that could expose unintended fields.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No hosted deployment. GitHub branch protection on main: 2 approvals required for changes to core/issuer.go, core/token.go, and all files in kms/ and storage/ directories. Release pipeline (GitHub Actions on semver tag): go test -race ./..., go test -fuzz ./core/... for 60 seconds, gosec with zero-warning gate, staticcheck, go mod verify all sub-modules, cosign sign module tag with keyless Sigstore, generate CHANGELOG from conventional commits, create GitHub Release. Sub-packages released independently under their own semver tags using convention storage/redis/v0.2.0. Security advisory process: GitHub Security Advisory with CVE for any cryptographic vulnerability; same-day patch release targeting affected major version.
target_output.observability: SDK emits no default telemetry. MetricsHook interface methods: TokenIssued(ctx context.Context, algorithm string); TokenRotated(ctx context.Context); SessionRevoked(ctx context.Context); NonceCacheHit(ctx context.Context, operation string); NonceCacheMiss(ctx context.Context, operation string). Consumer injects hook via WithMetricsHook option. Prometheus reference hook (gamesession-sdk/metrics/prometheus) registers counters: gamesession_tokens_issued_total with algorithm label, gamesession_rotations_total, gamesession_revocations_total, gamesession_nonce_checks_total with result label (hit or miss). Sub-package ships a Grafana dashboard JSON template for token issuance rate, rotation rate, and nonce cache hit ratio visualization.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost for SDK itself. GitHub Actions CI: approximately 3 minutes per PR, 8 minutes for release pipeline with fuzz run. AWS KMS adapter: partner studios pay kms:Sign at $0.03 per 10,000 calls; at 100,000 token issuances per day per game service, approximately $0.30/day. Redis RevocationStore scales horizontally with keyspace partitioning by token type. In-memory store handles millions of nonces per process for load testing. SDK has no compute or memory scaling constraints as a stateless library.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Core v1.0 (weeks 1-6): Implement Issuer with IssueTokenPair, RotateTokenPair, RevokeSession, ValidateAccessToken using Ed25519 and HMAC-SHA256. Implement RotationPolicy with NonceTTL() computed method. Implement RevocationStore interface with in-memory reference. Write conformance test suite with concurrent atomicity test. Fuzz token parser. Release v1.0.0. Phase 2 — Sub-Packages (weeks 7-10): Implement Redis RevocationStore with sorted-set nonce storage and MULTI/EXEC atomic check. Implement AWS KMS KeyMaterialProvider adapter. Implement Prometheus MetricsHook. Release each sub-package at v0.1.0. Phase 3 — Production Hardening (post v1.0): PASETO v4 format support under opt-in option flag. GCP CKMS adapter. OpenTelemetry span emission hook. Automatic key rotation scheduler utility.
target_output.development_guidelines: All code in core package passes go vet, gosec, and staticcheck with zero warnings. No reflection-based token parsing — explicit struct field encoding only. Error messages contain no token bytes, key IDs, or player identifiers. Core package test coverage 95% minimum enforced in CI. Fuzz corpus maintained in testdata/fuzz/ directories. Conformance test package is public-facing API — backward compatibility maintained within major version. All exported types and methods carry GoDoc comments with security guidance where applicable. Sub-package go.mod files pinned to minimum required versions; no replace directives.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: consumer implements RevocationStore with non-atomic CheckAndStoreNonce, creating parallel refresh replay vulnerability. Mitigation: conformance concurrent atomicity test catches this before production; GoDoc on RevocationStore interface documents atomicity requirement explicitly. Risk: crypto.Signer returned by KeyMaterialProvider called without deadline on context causing token issuance timeout under KMS latency spikes. Mitigation: WithSigningTimeout option on Issuer with 500ms default; documented tuning guidance for KMS vs in-process signing latency profiles. Tradeoff: separate go.mod per sub-package requires consumers to add explicit dependencies for Redis or KMS support but eliminates transitive dependency conflicts in partner production service builds — the correct tradeoff for a widely distributed SDK.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-003 resolved: KeyMaterialDescriptor.RawKey field removed; KeyMaterialProvider returns crypto.Signer and crypto.PublicKey — raw private key bytes inaccessible through any public SDK API path. ISS-002 resolved: fixed NonceTTL config field replaced with NonceTTL() computed method on RotationPolicy returning AccessTTL plus ClockSkewTolerance — replay window eliminated for all valid consumer configurations. PASETO v4 confirmed as post-v1.0 roadmap item. MaxChainDepth in RotationPolicy caps refresh chain length to prevent indefinite session extension without re-authentication events.

metadata.notes: Round 3 Go auth SDK — resolves critical RawKey exposure and nonce TTL replay vulnerability for partner game studios.

project_goal.value: High-volume telemetry ingestion and analytics pipeline for a live-service battle royale game processing billions of in-game events per day including player actions, item interactions, zone transitions, and kill feeds to power real-time dashboards and post-match analytics
project_goal.rationale: Current ad-hoc analytics cannot handle event volume at peak concurrent player counts; dedicated pipeline needed

target_users.value: Game analytics team, live operations team, and data engineers maintaining the pipeline
target_users.rationale: Analytics consumers are internal; pipeline operates headlessly with dashboards for live ops monitoring

project_class.rationale: Primary deliverable is an event ingestion and processing pipeline, not a user-facing application

capabilities.rationale: Covers Kafka ingestion, Flink stream processing, ClickHouse analytics, S3 archival, and Grafana dashboard integration

complexity_level.rationale: Billions of events per day at peak require multi-partition Kafka topics, parallel Flink job topology, and ClickHouse cluster sizing for sub-second query response

risk_level.rationale: Pipeline failure degrades analytics and live ops visibility but does not impact game gameplay directly

data_sensitivity.rationale: Event data contains anonymized player action telemetry; no PII beyond opaque player_id references

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline and dashboards accessible only on internal studio network via VPN

access_model.value: IAM roles for Kafka SASL/SCRAM and EKS pod service accounts; Grafana SSO via Okta for dashboard access; no player-facing auth
access_model.rationale: Internal tooling; human access via Grafana with Okta SSO only

feature_scope.value: Kafka ingestion with 64-partition sensor-data topic partitioned by match_id; Flink stream processing with real-time player statistics aggregation; ClickHouse OLAP for analytics queries; S3 Parquet archival of raw events; post-match batch rollup job; Grafana dashboards for live ops
feature_scope.rationale: Full pipeline scope from game server emit to analytics dashboard

mvp_scope.value: Kafka ingestion for kill_feed and zone_transition event types; basic Flink counting job; ClickHouse write and Grafana kill feed dashboard; S3 raw archive; post-match rollup deferred to Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Kill feed and zone transitions are highest priority for live ops team

security_baseline.value: Kafka SASL/SCRAM authentication for producer and consumer connections; EKS pod service accounts with IRSA for S3 and ClickHouse access; VPC-private endpoints for all data stores; ClickHouse user-level access control for analytics team; no public endpoints
security_baseline.rationale: Internal pipeline; VPC isolation and service account auth sufficient

frontend_stack.value: None; analytics dashboards delivered via Grafana; no custom frontend built
frontend_stack.rationale: Data pipeline project; no frontend development in scope

backend_stack.value: Apache Kafka (MSK) for ingestion; Apache Flink 1.18 on Flink Kubernetes Operator for stream processing; Python 3.12 for batch rollup scripts
backend_stack.rationale: Kafka and Flink are industry standard for high-volume event stream processing

data_platform.value: ClickHouse cluster for analytics OLAP; S3 with Apache Iceberg table format for raw event archive; MSK Kafka 3.7 with 3-broker cluster
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse for sub-second analytics queries at billions of rows; Iceberg for time-travel queries on raw archive

hosting_target.value: EKS on AWS in us-east-1; Flink Kubernetes Operator manages Flink cluster; MSK for managed Kafka; ClickHouse self-hosted on EKS
hosting_target.rationale: EKS provides managed Kubernetes for Flink; MSK reduces Kafka operational overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw events retained in S3 Iceberg for 2 years with lifecycle to Glacier after 90 days; ClickHouse hot data retained 30 days; match summaries retained indefinitely; player_id references anonymized after account deletion
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 2-year archive supports long-term analytics; 30-day ClickHouse hot tier fits cluster sizing budget

future_scope.value: Player behavior segmentation ML pipeline, real-time cheat detection stream, cross-season analytics with full historical replay, A/B test event tagging
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch roadmap items pending stable ingestion pipeline

constraints.value: Pipeline must sustain 500,000 events/second at peak match concurrency; end-to-end latency from game server emit to ClickHouse query availability must be under 10 seconds; EKS in us-east-1 only; MSK cluster cost must stay under $3,000/month
constraints.rationale: Live ops team needs near-real-time kill feed for broadcast events; budget constrained

observability_baseline.value: Prometheus and Grafana for Kafka consumer lag, Flink checkpoint duration, ClickHouse insert rate and query latency; PagerDuty on consumer lag exceeding 60 seconds or Flink job restart exceeding 3 times per hour
observability_baseline.rationale: Consumer lag and Flink restart rate are primary pipeline health indicators

execution_preference.value: Event type by event type rollout: kill_feed first, then zone_transitions, then player_actions, then item_interactions; each type validated in staging before production
execution_preference.rationale: Incremental rollout limits blast radius of schema mismatches

project.goal: Battle royale telemetry pipeline processing billions of events per day for live ops and analytics
project.target_users: Analytics and live ops teams
project.timeline: Q3 kill_feed MVP, Q4 full pipeline

backend.runtime: Apache Kafka MSK + Apache Flink 1.18 + Python 3.12 batch
backend.api: Kafka producer protocol from game servers

security.auth: Kafka SASL/SCRAM; EKS IRSA; VPC-private

open_questions.schema_registry: Should Confluent Schema Registry be used for Avro schemas?

reasoner_reviews.product: The event-type-by-event-type rollout strategy is the right approach for managing schema risk across 4 event types. Kill feed first is correct — it is the highest priority for live ops broadcasts. Post-match rollup can safely be deferred to Phase 2 since match summaries are not needed for the live dashboard MVP.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Flink with 64-partition Kafka topics can handle 500K events/second with the right parallelism configuration — Flink job parallelism should match partition count. ClickHouse MergeTree with a time-based partition key (toYYYYMM(event_ts)) will enable efficient range queries for Grafana dashboards. The S3 Iceberg archival job should run on a tumbling window trigger, not a continuous sink, to produce well-sized Parquet files.
reasoner_reviews.security: Kafka SASL/SCRAM credentials must be stored in AWS Secrets Manager and rotated quarterly; not in Kubernetes ConfigMaps. IRSA binds Flink pods to IAM roles allowing S3 PutObject to the archive bucket and ClickHouse write user credentials fetched from Secrets Manager at pod startup. ClickHouse user ACLs should restrict analytics team to SELECT on aggregated tables only, not raw_events.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 500K events/second requires MSK broker sizing at kafka.m5.4xlarge for 3 brokers to stay within the $3K/month budget. Flink checkpoint interval of 30 seconds with incremental checkpoints to S3 will keep checkpoint overhead below 5% of processing time. ClickHouse insert buffer via Kafka engine table or Buffer table pattern prevents write amplification at high throughput.

backend.service_design: Flink job topology: KafkaSource connector reading raw_events topic, flatMap for event type routing (kill_feed, zone_transition, player_action, item_interaction), keyed aggregation streams by match_id and player_id, ClickHouseSink for aggregated metrics, S3FileSink for Iceberg Parquet archive. Separate Flink job for post-match rollup triggered by match_end events.
backend.background_jobs: Post-match rollup Python script: reads raw_events for completed match_id from S3 Iceberg, computes player-level statistics (kills, deaths, damage_dealt, survival_time, items_collected), writes to match_summaries ClickHouse table. Triggered by match_end Kafka event consumed by a Lambda function. Nightly Iceberg compaction job merges small Parquet files.
backend.failure_handling: Flink checkpointing every 30 seconds to S3 with exactly-once semantics via Kafka transaction markers. Consumer group offset managed by Flink; manual offset reset procedure documented for disaster recovery. Dead letter topic for events failing schema validation.
backend.backend_risks: ClickHouse insert buffer saturation at peak event bursts could cause Flink backpressure. Mitigation: ClickHouse Buffer table with 10-second flush interval absorbs burst; Flink async I/O for ClickHouse sink prevents blocking on slow inserts.

security.auth_design: Kafka producers (game servers) authenticate with SASL/SCRAM-SHA-512; credentials per game server region stored in Secrets Manager. Flink pods use IRSA for S3 access and fetch ClickHouse write credentials from Secrets Manager at startup. MSK VPC endpoint prevents Kafka traffic from leaving private subnet.
security.secrets_management: MSK bootstrap credentials, ClickHouse write user password, and Confluent Schema Registry API key stored in Secrets Manager with quarterly rotation. Rotation triggers EKS rolling restart of Flink job manager via GitLab CI pipeline.
security.security_risks: Flink job manager web UI exposed on internal load balancer could leak job topology to internal network if VPC routing is misconfigured. Mitigation: Flink web UI bound to pod ClusterIP only; accessible via kubectl port-forward by data engineers only.

data.entities: raw_events (Kafka topic): {event_id UUID, match_id UUID, player_id UUID, event_type ENUM kill_feed/zone_transition/player_action/item_interaction, payload JSONB, event_ts TIMESTAMPTZ, server_region VARCHAR, game_version VARCHAR}. kill_feed_aggregates (ClickHouse MergeTree): (match_id, killer_id, victim_id, weapon_id, kill_ts, zone_id, is_headshot). zone_transitions (ClickHouse MergeTree): (match_id, player_id, from_zone_id, to_zone_id, transition_ts, alive_status). match_summaries (ClickHouse): (match_id, region, start_ts, end_ts, player_stats Array(Tuple(player_id, kills, deaths, damage, survival_seconds))). performance_baselines in PostgreSQL for regression comparison. aggregated_metrics (ClickHouse): 1-minute tumbling window sums for active_players, total_kills, zone_collapse_events per match.
data.storage_design: MSK Kafka 3.7 with 3 brokers (kafka.m5.4xlarge) and 64 partitions on raw_events topic. ClickHouse cluster: 2-shard 2-replica on EKS with SSD-backed EBS volumes, ReplicatedMergeTree engine, time-partition by toYYYYMM(event_ts). S3 bucket with Iceberg table for raw event archive; Parquet files written by Flink S3FileSink with 128MB target file size. Buffer table in ClickHouse for kill_feed_aggregates absorbs burst inserts.
data.analytics_events: Match start (match_id, region, player_count, game_mode, start_ts), Match end (match_id, duration_seconds, winner_player_id, total_kills, total_zone_collapses), Flink job restart (job_id, restart_count, reason), ClickHouse insert lag (table, seconds_behind_kafka).
data.data_risks: Schema evolution in Kafka events without a schema registry could cause Flink deserialization failures on game client updates. Mitigation: Confluent Schema Registry with Avro schemas and backward compatibility enforcement before producer deploys.

devops.deployment_topology: EKS cluster in us-east-1 with dedicated node pool for Flink (memory-optimized m6i.4xlarge with local NVMe for RocksDB state backend). Flink Kubernetes Operator manages FlinkDeployment custom resources. ClickHouse deployed via Helm chart with persistent EBS volumes. MSK provisioned via Terraform. Grafana and Prometheus in monitoring namespace.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI pipeline: Flink job Java/Scala build, unit tests, Docker push to ECR, kubectl apply FlinkDeployment CRD to staging for smoke test, then production. Terraform plan and apply for MSK and EKS changes with manual approval gate on production. Schema Registry schema registration in CI before producer deploys.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes Flink JobManager and TaskManager metrics: flink_jobmanager_job_lastCheckpointDuration, flink_taskmanager_job_task_numRecordsInPerSecond, kafka_consumer_lag_sum. ClickHouse system.metrics queried by Prometheus exporter for insert_rate and query_latency_p99. PagerDuty: consumer lag >60 seconds, Flink restart count >3 per hour, ClickHouse insert queue size >1M.
devops.rollback_strategy: Flink savepoint created before each job upgrade via Flink REST API; rollback by restoring savepoint with previous job version. ClickHouse schema migrations are additive only (new columns with DEFAULT); rollback is column drop after validation. Kafka topic offsets managed by Flink; consumer group reset to savepoint offset on rollback.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a high-scale battle royale telemetry pipeline. The plan intentionally introduces two contract violations: the deployment section incorrectly references Vercel as a hosting option despite the contract specifying EKS on AWS, and the system_components section references a React analytics dashboard despite the contract specifying no frontend stack and the profile having no frontend capability. These flaws represent realistic mistakes an architect might make when not carefully checking technology consistency.
target_output.title: Battle Royale Telemetry Ingestion and Analytics Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale event streaming pipeline for a live-service battle royale game processing up to 500,000 events per second through MSK Kafka into Apache Flink stream processing jobs, with results landing in a ClickHouse OLAP cluster for sub-second analytics queries and S3 Iceberg for 2-year raw event archive. Grafana dashboards provide live ops visibility with under 10-second end-to-end latency from game server emit to query availability.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven streaming architecture with game servers as Kafka producers publishing kill_feed, zone_transition, player_action, and item_interaction events to a 64-partition MSK raw_events topic partitioned by match_id. Flink Kubernetes Operator manages FlinkDeployment resources on EKS; Flink jobs consume from raw_events, perform keyed aggregation by match_id and player_id, write aggregated metrics to ClickHouse via async sink, and archive raw events to S3 Iceberg via S3FileSink. Post-match rollup Python scripts compute per-player match summaries from S3 Iceberg on match_end events. A React analytics dashboard (FLAWED: contract specifies no frontend; Grafana is the correct tooling per contract) provides live ops visibility.
target_output.technology_stack: Ingestion: Apache Kafka 3.7 on MSK (3 brokers, kafka.m5.4xlarge), 64 partitions on raw_events, Confluent Schema Registry with Avro, SASL/SCRAM-SHA-512. Stream Processing: Apache Flink 1.18 on Flink Kubernetes Operator, Flink Kafka Connector, ClickHouse Flink Connector, S3FileSink for Iceberg. OLAP: ClickHouse cluster (2 shards, 2 replicas, ReplicatedMergeTree). Archive: S3 Iceberg with Apache Iceberg 1.5 and Parquet. Batch: Python 3.12 with PyFlink for rollup scripts. Infra: EKS on AWS us-east-1, Terraform, GitLab CI. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana. FLAW: plan also references Vercel Edge for dashboard delivery which contradicts the EKS hosting contract.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Event Ingestion: game servers publish structured Avro events to raw_events Kafka topic with match_id as partition key; Schema Registry enforces backward-compatible schema evolution. Stream Aggregation: Flink keyed streams compute running kill counts per match, player survival state transitions from zone events, item pickup rates per zone per minute using 1-minute tumbling windows. ClickHouse Landing: aggregated metrics written via async ClickHouseSink with Buffer table absorbing burst inserts. Raw Archive: S3FileSink writes Parquet files with target size 128MB; Iceberg catalog managed by Glue Data Catalog. Post-Match Rollup: Python batch job triggered by match_end event reads S3 Iceberg for match_id, computes player_stats aggregates, writes match_summaries to ClickHouse. Live Ops Dashboard: Grafana dashboards over ClickHouse data source showing active players, kill rate, zone collapse heatmap per match.
target_output.system_components: MSK Kafka 3.7 (3-broker cluster, 64-partition raw_events topic). Confluent Schema Registry (schema validation and evolution). Flink Kubernetes Operator (FlinkDeployment CRD management). Flink cluster (1 JobManager + 8 TaskManagers on m6i.4xlarge nodes with RocksDB state backend). ClickHouse cluster (2-shard 2-replica on EKS with SSD EBS, ReplicatedMergeTree). ClickHouse Buffer table for kill_feed_aggregates. S3 bucket with Apache Iceberg table (raw_events, Parquet, Glue catalog). AWS Lambda (match_end event trigger for rollup Python script). Prometheus and Grafana (pipeline monitoring and live ops dashboards). FLAW: plan also lists a 'React SPA analytics portal hosted on Vercel' which is absent from the contract and contradicts the data_pipeline project class.
target_output.workflows: Stream Ingestion: game server emits JSON event; Kafka producer serializes via Avro Schema Registry and publishes to raw_events partition by match_id hash; Flink KafkaSource reads with exactly-once semantics; Flink flatMap routes by event_type to parallel keyed aggregation streams; aggregated metrics flushed to ClickHouse Buffer table every 10 seconds; S3FileSink batches raw events into 128MB Parquet files and commits Iceberg snapshot. Post-Match Rollup: game server publishes match_end event to match-lifecycle Kafka topic; AWS Lambda consumes event, triggers Python rollup script on EKS as a Kubernetes Job; script reads match_id partition from S3 Iceberg via PyIceberg, computes per-player statistics using vectorized Pandas operations, writes to match_summaries ClickHouse table. Consumer Lag Recovery: if Flink job restarts, it resumes from last successful checkpoint; if checkpoint is stale, on-call engineer runs savepoint restore procedure from S3 savepoint path.
target_output.data_model: raw_events Kafka Avro schema: {event_id: string (UUID), match_id: string (UUID), player_id: string (UUID), event_type: enum {kill_feed, zone_transition, player_action, item_interaction}, event_ts: long (epoch_ms), server_region: string, game_version: string, payload: map<string, string>}. kill_feed_aggregates ClickHouse MergeTree: (match_id UUID, killer_id UUID, victim_id UUID, weapon_id String, kill_ts DateTime64(3), zone_id String, is_headshot UInt8) ORDER BY (match_id, kill_ts). zone_transitions ClickHouse MergeTree: (match_id UUID, player_id UUID, from_zone_id String, to_zone_id String, transition_ts DateTime64(3), alive_status Enum8) ORDER BY (match_id, player_id, transition_ts). match_summaries ClickHouse: (match_id UUID, region String, start_ts DateTime64(3), end_ts DateTime64(3), total_kills UInt32, winner_player_id UUID, player_stats Array(Tuple(UUID, UInt16, UInt16, Float32, UInt32))). aggregated_metrics ClickHouse SummingMergeTree: (window_start DateTime, match_id UUID, active_player_count UInt32, total_kills_in_window UInt32, zone_collapse_count UInt8) ORDER BY (window_start, match_id).
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API surface — pipeline is event-driven. Kafka producer protocol: game servers publish to MSK bootstrap endpoints with SASL/SCRAM credentials; topic raw_events with 64 partitions; Avro serialization with Schema Registry. Flink REST API (internal): POST /jobs/{jobId}/savepoints — create savepoint before upgrade; GET /jobs/{jobId} — monitor job health. ClickHouse HTTP interface (internal): SELECT queries from Grafana data source plugin at http://clickhouse:8123. Glue Catalog API: Iceberg table metadata reads for rollup Python scripts via PyIceberg. Lambda trigger: EventSourceMapping on match-lifecycle Kafka topic for match_end events.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Kafka authentication: SASL/SCRAM-SHA-512 with per-region game server credentials in Secrets Manager; MSK VPC endpoint prevents Kafka traffic from leaving private subnet. EKS security: Flink pods use IRSA binding to IAM role with s3:PutObject on archive bucket and s3:GetObject for rollup reads; ClickHouse credentials fetched from Secrets Manager at pod startup via init container. ClickHouse access control: analytics team users have SELECT on kill_feed_aggregates, zone_transitions, and match_summaries only; raw_events Iceberg accessible only to Flink service account. Secrets rotation: MSK credentials and ClickHouse passwords rotated quarterly via Secrets Manager rotation Lambda with EKS rolling restart. Network: all data stores in private VPC subnets; no public endpoints.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS cluster provisioned via Terraform with dedicated node pool for Flink (m6i.4xlarge, local NVMe for RocksDB). Flink Kubernetes Operator deployed via Helm; FlinkDeployment custom resources define job topology, parallelism (64, matching partition count), and checkpoint configuration (30-second interval, S3 backend). MSK provisioned via Terraform as kafka.m5.4xlarge 3-broker cluster. ClickHouse deployed via operator Helm chart with persistent EBS gp3 volumes. GitLab CI pipeline: Flink job build, unit test, Docker push to ECR, FlinkDeployment apply to staging with smoke test verifying consumer lag drops to zero within 5 minutes, manual approval for production apply. Savepoint created via Flink REST API before each job upgrade. FLAW: deployment section also references 'Vercel Edge Functions for dashboard API routes' which is inconsistent with the EKS on AWS contract and the absence of a frontend capability in the profile.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes Flink JobManager for flink_jobmanager_job_lastCheckpointDuration, flink_taskmanager_job_task_numRecordsInPerSecond, and flink_taskmanager_job_task_numRecordsOutPerSecond. Kafka metrics via MSK CloudWatch exporter: kafka_consumer_lag_sum per consumer group. ClickHouse metrics via prometheus_exporter: ClickHouseProfileEvents_InsertedRows, ClickHouseMetrics_QueryThread, ClickHouseAsyncMetrics_ReplicasMaxRelativeDelay. Grafana dashboards: Kafka consumer lag trend, Flink throughput and checkpoint duration, ClickHouse insert rate vs query p99 latency, live ops kill feed rate and active player count per match. PagerDuty: consumer lag exceeding 60 seconds, Flink job restart more than 3 times per hour, ClickHouse insert queue exceeding 1 million rows.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: MSK 3x kafka.m5.4xlarge: approximately $2,400/month fitting under $3,000 constraint. Flink EKS node pool 4x m6i.4xlarge: approximately $1,800/month. ClickHouse 4 nodes r6i.2xlarge with SSD: approximately $2,400/month. S3 storage at 2TB/day compressed Parquet: approximately $600/month. Lambda rollup triggers: negligible. Total pipeline cost approximately $7,200/month. Scaling: Flink TaskManager count and MSK partition count scale together; ClickHouse horizontal sharding if query volume exceeds 2 shards.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Kill Feed MVP (8 weeks): Provision MSK and EKS via Terraform. Deploy Flink Kubernetes Operator and base FlinkDeployment. Implement Flink KafkaSource and ClickHouseSink for kill_feed events only. Set up ClickHouse kill_feed_aggregates table and Grafana kill feed dashboard. S3FileSink raw archive for kill_feed events. Schema Registry with Avro schema for kill_feed event type. Deploy to staging and validate consumer lag under load. Phase 2 — Full Event Types (4 weeks): Add zone_transition, player_action, and item_interaction event types to Flink job topology. Add corresponding ClickHouse tables. Implement post-match rollup Lambda trigger and Python batch job. Add match_summaries dashboard. Phase 3 — Analytics Hardening (4 weeks): Iceberg compaction job. Advanced Grafana dashboards with player heatmaps. Schema Registry backward-compatibility CI gate. ClickHouse query optimization with materialized views for common aggregations.
target_output.development_guidelines: Flink job code in Java 17; unit tested with flink-test-utils and embedded Kafka via Testcontainers. Schema changes must register new Avro schema version in Schema Registry CI step before producer deployment. ClickHouse schema migrations are additive only — new nullable columns with DEFAULT; no column drops or type changes without explicit data migration. Flink job parallelism must always match Kafka partition count (64) to prevent hot partitions. FlinkDeployment CRD changes reviewed by data engineering lead before apply. Iceberg table format version pinned to version 2 for delete-file support.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Flink backpressure from slow ClickHouse inserts at event burst peaks causes consumer lag spike. Mitigation: ClickHouse Buffer table with 10-second flush absorbs burst; Flink async I/O sink with bounded concurrency prevents blocking. Risk: Schema evolution without Schema Registry causing Flink deserialization failures on game version updates. Mitigation: Schema Registry backward-compatibility gate in CI prevents incompatible schema registration. Tradeoff: RocksDB incremental checkpoints to S3 adds checkpoint overhead but reduces recovery time from minutes to seconds. Tradeoff: ClickHouse over Apache Druid provides better compression and simpler operations but requires manual horizontal scaling rather than automatic tiering.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confluent Schema Registry selected for Avro schema enforcement to prevent game client version mismatches from corrupting Flink deserialization. MSK chosen over self-managed Kafka to reduce operational overhead at the required 3-broker scale. ClickHouse Buffer table pattern selected over Kafka engine table to avoid Kafka consumer group conflicts between Flink job and ClickHouse native consumer. Iceberg compaction scheduled nightly via EKS CronJob using Apache Spark on EMR Serverless to merge small files without blocking Flink S3FileSink writes.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: plan references React dashboard and Vercel hosting contradicting data_pipeline contract with no frontend capability and EKS hosting target.
